topics.title: Query Processing and Optimization: Measures of Query Cost, Algorithms for Selection with Cost Analysis, Algorithms for Join with Cost Analysis, Evaluation of Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST634 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Query Processing and Optimization: Measures of Query Cost, Algorithms for Selection with Cost Analysis, Algorithms for Join with Cost Analysis, Evaluation of Expressions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Query Processing and Optimization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Query Processing** is the sequence of activities undertaken by the Database Management System (DBMS) to retrieve data efficiently from the database in response to a high-level query (typically written in SQL). It encompasses translation, optimization, and execution of the query.
>
> **Query Optimization** is the process of selecting the most efficient query-evaluation plan (or execution strategy) from among the many strategies possible for a given query, especially when the query is complex and involves multiple relations and operations.

The overall pipeline of query processing consists of the following major phases:

1. **Parsing and Translation** – The SQL query is parsed, checked for syntax, and translated into an internal relational-algebra expression.
2. **Optimization** – The relational-algebra expression is converted into an equivalent expression that is more efficient to execute. An *evaluation plan* is generated.
3. **Evaluation / Execution** – The evaluation plan is executed by the query execution engine, which interacts with the storage manager to fetch data from disk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Measure of Query Cost** is the principal metric used by the optimizer to choose among competing evaluation plans. The cost is a function of **disk I/O, CPU time, communication cost, and memory usage**. In centralized DBMSs, the dominant cost is **disk access** (block transfers and seeks).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The GPS Navigator

> [!TIP]
> Imagine you are a delivery driver in a big city. You have 25 packages to deliver at 25 different addresses. There are hundreds of possible routes you could take. A **GPS navigator** is essentially a **query optimizer**:
> - The **package list** = the SQL query
> - The **addresses** = the relations (tables) in the database
> - The **possible routes** = the alternative evaluation plans
> - The **GPS-selected "best" route** = the optimized query evaluation plan
> - The **estimated travel time / fuel consumed** = the **measure of query cost**
>
> Just as the GPS computes route cost based on distance, traffic, and turns (analogous to disk I/O, seeks, and CPU), a DBMS optimizer estimates the cost of a query plan using **statistics on relations, indexes, and hardware parameters**.

### 1.3 Cost Components Used in Modern Optimizers

| Cost Component | Symbol | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block transfer cost | $t_{transfer}$ | Time to read/write one disk block | Dominant in magnetic disks |
| Seek cost | $t_{seek}$ | Time to position the disk arm | Random access penalty |
| CPU cost | $t_{cpu}$ | Time per tuple processed | For in-memory operations |
| Hash table build cost | $h$ | Cost per bucket operation | Specific to hash-based algorithms |
| Number of relation blocks | $b_r$ | Integer | Total blocks in relation $r$ |
| Number of tuples in $r$ | $n_r$ | Integer | Cardinality of $r$ |
| Index height | $h_i$ | Integer | Levels of $\text{B}^+$-tree index |
| Number of distinct values | $V(r, A)$ | Integer | For attribute $A$ in relation $r$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For board examinations in KTU 2024, the **assumed cost model** is: *Number of block transfers from disk + number of seeks*. We will use this cost model throughout.

**Total Estimated Cost (Simplified KTU Model):**
$$Cost = b_{transfers} \times t_{transfer} + n_{seeks} \times t_{seek}$$

When $t_{transfer}$ and $t_{seek}$ are normalized to 1 unit (as in most textbook problems), this collapses to:
$$Cost = b_{transfers} + n_{seeks}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Query Processing Activities

- **Query Parsing**: Lexical and syntactic analysis of the SQL statement. Produces a parse tree.
- **Query Translation**: Converts parse tree into a **relational-algebra expression** (logical plan).
- **Query Optimization**:
  * *Heuristic / Rule-based*: Applies equivalence rules (e.g., push selections down).
  * *Cost-based*: Estimates cost of multiple plans using statistics and picks the cheapest.
- **Query Evaluation / Execution**: The chosen plan is executed by the runtime engine, which invokes file-scan, index-scan, sort, join, and projection operators.
- **Result Generation**: Output tuples are formatted and returned to the user.

### 2.2 Measures of Query Cost – Detailed Components

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Define the cost of executing a query. List the factors considered."* The examiner expects the following five factors.

1. **Number of block transfers (I/O)** – How many disk blocks must be read/written.
2. **Number of seeks** – How many times the disk arm must be repositioned.
3. **CPU time** – Time spent processing tuples in memory (joins, projections, sorts).
4. **Communication cost** – For distributed DBMSs only; not the focus of Module 1.
5. **Memory usage** – Buffer pool usage, sort memory.

For a relation $r$ stored over $b_r$ blocks:
- **Linear Scan** of $r$ requires $b_r$ block transfers and **1 seek** (start of scan).
- **Sequential read with no seek** between blocks reduces cost to $b_r$ block transfers.

### 2.3 Algorithms for Selection Operation ($\sigma$)

The selection operation $\sigma_{\theta}(r)$ retrieves tuples from $r$ that satisfy predicate $\theta$. KTU 2024 requires mastery of the following algorithms.

> [!TIP]
> **Why so many selection algorithms?** Different data layouts (unsorted heap, sorted file, indexed) and different predicates (equality, range, conjunction) lead to vastly different optimal strategies. The optimizer must choose.

#### 2.3.1 A1 – Linear Search (Brute Force)

- **When applicable**: Always, for any predicate. Particularly useful for unsorted relations with no index, or for non-key selections.
- **Procedure**: Scan every block of $r$; test every tuple against $\theta$.
- **Cost Analysis**:
$$Cost_{A1} = b_r \cdot t_{transfer} + 1 \cdot t_{seek}$$
- For an **equality selection on a key**:
$$Cost_{A1} = \bigl\lfloor b_r \mathrel{\big/} 2 \bigr\rfloor \cdot t_{transfer} + 1 \cdot t_{seek} \text{ (on average)}$$
- *Why divide by 2?* On average, the matching tuple lies in the middle of the file.

#### 2.3.2 A2 – Binary Search (Sorted File, Equality)

- **When applicable**: Relation is **sorted on the selection attribute**; predicate is **equality**.
- **Procedure**: Jump to the middle block, compare, halve the search range.
- **Cost Analysis**:
$$Cost_{A2} = \bigl\lceil \log_2(b_r) \bigr\rceil \cdot (t_{transfer} + t_{seek})$$
- *Why the sum?* Each step requires a seek + a block read.

#### 2.3.3 A3 – Selection Using Primary Index ($\text{B}^+$-tree on key, equality)

- **When applicable**: A primary (clustered) $\text{B}^+$-tree index exists on the search key; predicate is equality.
- **Procedure**: Traverse the $\text{B}^+$-tree from root to leaf, then read the data block.
- **Cost Analysis**:
$$Cost_{A3} = (h_i + 1) \cdot (t_{transfer} + t_{seek})$$
- $h_i$ = height of the $\text{B}^+$-tree index.

#### 2.3.4 A4 – Selection Using Primary Index (non-key, equality)

- **When applicable**: Clustered index on a non-key attribute.
- **Procedure**: Multiple tuples may share the same key. After locating the first, additional matching tuples are read sequentially.
- **Cost Analysis**:
$$Cost_{A4} = h_i \cdot (t_{transfer} + t_{seek}) + b \cdot t_{transfer} + 1 \cdot t_{seek}$$
- where $b$ = number of blocks containing matching tuples $= \lceil n_r \mathrel{\big/} V(r, A) \rceil$ (approximately, when the index is clustered).

#### 2.3.5 A5 – Selection Using Secondary Index (equality)

- **When applicable**: Secondary (unclustered) $\text{B}^+$-tree on the attribute.
- **Procedure**: Traverse index to get pointers; each pointer may require a separate disk read.
- **Cost Analysis (worst case, all matches)**:
$$Cost_{A5} = (h_i + n_r) \cdot (t_{transfer} + t_{seek})$$
- *Why $n_r$?* In the worst case (every tuple matches), each tuple is fetched by a separate I/O because the index is unclustered.

#### 2.3.6 A6 – Selection Using Secondary Index (comparison, e.g., $A > v$)

- **Cost Analysis** (for $A > v$):
$$Cost_{A6} = h_i \cdot (t_{transfer} + t_{seek}) + b \cdot t_{transfer} + 1 \cdot t_{seek}$$
- where $b \approx n_r \mathrel{\big/} 2$ (half the tuples may match).

#### 2.3.7 A7 – Conjunctive Selection ($\theta_1 \wedge \theta_2 \wedge \ldots$)

- **Approach 1 (Naive)**: Apply A1–A6 with the most selective single condition, then check remaining conditions in memory.
- **Approach 2 (Composite Index)**: Use a composite index if one exists on all conjuncts.
- **Approach 3 (Intersection of Record Pointers)**: Use individual indexes for each condition; intersect the result sets in memory.

### 2.4 Algorithms for Join Operation ($\bowtie$)

The join is the most expensive operation in query processing. KTU requires the following five algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notation used:
> - $r$ = outer relation with $b_r$ blocks and $n_r$ tuples
> - $s$ = inner relation with $b_s$ blocks and $n_s$ tuples
> - Index on inner $s$ has height $h_i$

#### 2.4.1 B1 – Nested-Loop Join (NLJ)

- **Procedure**: For every tuple $t_r \in r$, scan the entire relation $s$ to find matches.
- **Cost Analysis**:
$$Cost_{B1} = n_r \cdot b_s + b_r \quad \text{(block transfers + seeks, normalized)}$$
- *Step-by-step derivation*:
  1. Outer loop reads all $b_r$ blocks of $r$ (one read, no repeated seeks if cached) $\Rightarrow b_r$.
  2. For each block of $r$ (containing $n_s$-ratio tuples), we do a complete scan of $s$ costing $b_s$ blocks.
  3. There are $n_r$ such scans (one per tuple) $\Rightarrow n_r \cdot b_s$.
  4. Total = $b_r + n_r \cdot b_s$.

> [!TIP]
> **Optimization**: Make the smaller relation the outer. This reduces cost from $b_r + n_r b_s$ to $b_s + n_s b_r$. Even better: block nested-loop.

#### 2.4.2 B2 – Block Nested-Loop Join (BNLJ)

- **Procedure**: Use the buffer pool efficiently; for each block of $r$, scan $s$ once.
- **Cost Analysis**:
$$Cost_{B2} = b_r \cdot b_s + b_r \quad \text{(block transfers + seeks)}$$
- *Improvement*: With $M$ buffer blocks ($M-2$ for $r$, $1$ for $s$, $1$ for output):
$$Cost_{B2} = \bigl\lceil b_r \mathrel{\big/} (M-2) \bigr\rceil \cdot b_s + b_r$$

#### 2.4.3 B3 – Index Nested-Loop Join (INLJ)

- **Procedure**: For each tuple in outer $r$, probe an index on the inner $s$.
- **Cost Analysis**:
$$Cost_{B3} = b_r + n_r \cdot (h_i + 1) \quad \text{(primary index, equality join)}$$
- *Step-by-step*:
  1. Scan $r$ once: $b_r$ block transfers + 1 seek.
  2. For each of $n_r$ tuples, traverse index of height $h_i$ and fetch 1 data block: $n_r \cdot (h_i + 1)$ block transfers.
  3. Total: $b_r + n_r \cdot (h_i + 1)$.

#### 2.4.4 B4 – Sort-Merge Join (SMJ)

- **Pre-condition**: Both relations sorted on the join attribute. If not, sort them first.
- **Procedure**: Two-pointer merge like in merge-sort, but emit matching pairs.
- **Cost Analysis (if both pre-sorted)**:
$$Cost_{B4} = b_r + b_s \quad \text{(block transfers + seeks)}$$
- *Cost if sorting is required*:
$$Cost_{B4} = b_r \log_2(b_r) + b_s \log_2(b_s) + b_r + b_s$$
- For $b$ blocks, sorting costs $2b \lceil \log_{M-1}(b) \rceil$ in passes, approximated as $b \log b$ in simplified models.

#### 2.4.5 B5 – Hash Join (HJ)

- **Procedure**:
  1. **Partition phase**: Hash tuples of $r$ and $s$ on the join attribute into $k$ buckets.
  2. **Probe phase**: For each bucket of $r$, probe the corresponding bucket of $s$.
- **Cost Analysis**:
$$Cost_{B5} = 3(b_r + b_s) \quad \text{(assuming recursive partitioning adds 1 extra pass)}$$
- *Step-by-step derivation*:
  1. **Partition** $r$ into $k$ buckets: read $r$ (cost $b_r$), write buckets to disk (cost $2 b_r$). Total = $3 b_r$ if 2 passes are needed; but in simplified model = $b_r + b_r = 2 b_r$ for one pass.
  2. **Partition** $s$ similarly: $2 b_s$.
  3. **Probe**: Read both partitioned relations: $b_r + b_s$.
  4. *Combined textbook simplification*: $3(b_r + b_s)$ accounts for partition + probe.

> [!WARNING]
> **Recursion Cost**: If $k$ is large or memory $M$ is small, partitioning is recursive, adding extra passes. Full model: $2 (b_r + b_s) \lceil \log_{M-1}(b_r) - 1 \rceil + b_r + b_s$.

### 2.5 Evaluation of Expressions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Discuss the two approaches to evaluating relational-algebra expressions."* The two approaches are **Materialization** and **Pipelining**.

#### 2.5.1 Materialization (Eager Evaluation)

- Evaluate each operation in turn, **store the result as a temporary relation on disk**, and then use it as input to the next operation.
- **Cost**: Sum of costs of all individual operations. High I/O because intermediate results are written to disk.
- **Pro**: Simple to implement; allows reuse of intermediate results.
- **Con**: Multiple writes and reads of intermediate results.

#### 2.5.2 Pipelining (Lazy / On-the-fly Evaluation)

- Pass tuples from one operation to the next **without creating a temporary relation**.
- **Demand-driven (pull)**: Top operation requests tuples one at a time from the operation below (e.g., iterator model with `open()`, `next()`, `close()`).
- **Producer-driven (push)**: Bottom operation produces tuples and pushes them to the next operator.
- **Pro**: Avoids writing intermediate results to disk; faster.
- **Con**: Harder to implement; cannot reuse intermediate results.

#### 2.5.3 Equivalence Rules (used in Heuristic Optimization)

- $\sigma_{p_1 \wedge p_2}(E) = \sigma_{p_1}(\sigma_{p_2}(E))$ — *Selection can be broken down*.
- $\sigma_{p}(\Pi_{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n}(E)) = \Pi_{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n}(\sigma_p(E))$ — *Selection commutes with projection if $p$ involves only attributes in the projection list*.
- $\sigma_p(E_1 \times E_2) = \sigma_p(E_1) \times E_2$ — *Push selections below cartesian product* (huge optimization gain).
- $\Pi_{A_1,\ldots,A_n}(E_1 \bowtie E_2) = \Pi_{A_1,\ldots,A_n}( \Pi_{B_1,\ldots,B_m}(E_1) \bowtie \Pi_{C_1,\ldots,C_k}(E_2) )$ — *Push projections below joins*.
- $\bowtie$ and $\times$ are **commutative** and **associative**.
- $\sigma$ and $\Pi$ are **idempotent** and **commutative** (under certain conditions).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following compact table consolidates **every formula you must memorize** for KTU 2024 exam questions on this topic. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Algorithm | Operation | Cost Formula (transfers + seeks) |
|---|---|---|
| Linear Search | $\sigma$ on unsorted $r$ | $b_r$ (or $b_r \mathrel{\big/} 2$ for key equality) |
| Binary Search | $\sigma$ on sorted $r$ (equality) | $\lceil \log_2 b_r \rceil$ |
| Primary Index, key | $\sigma$ equality | $h_i + 1$ |
| Primary Index, non-key | $\sigma$ equality | $h_i + b$ (where $b = n_r \mathrel{\big/} V(r,A)$ blocks) |
| Secondary Index, key | $\sigma$ equality | $h_i + 1$ |
| Secondary Index, non-key | $\sigma$ equality | $h_i + n_r$ (worst) |
| Secondary Index, range | $\sigma$ comparison | $h_i + b$ |
| Nested-Loop Join | $r \bowtie s$ | $n_r \cdot b_s + b_r$ |
| Block Nested-Loop | $r \bowtie s$ | $b_r \cdot b_s + b_r$ |
| Index Nested-Loop | $r \bowtie s$ (index on $s$) | $b_r + n_r \cdot (h_i + 1)$ |
| Sort-Merge Join | $r \bowtie s$ (sorted) | $b_r + b_s$ |
| Sort-Merge Join | $r \bowtie s$ (unsorted) | $b_r \log b_r + b_s \log b_s + b_r + b_s$ |
| Hash Join | $r \bowtie s$ | $3(b_r + b_s)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility**: This cost model is the foundation of every commercial DBMS optimizer (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server). Understanding the cost model helps in writing efficient SQL — e.g., adding indexes, restructuring joins, avoiding `SELECT *`.

### 2.7 Why These Algorithms Matter in Real Engineering

- **Database Tuning**: DBAs use these formulas to decide when to add or drop an index.
- **Schema Design**: Knowing that nested-loop is $O(n_r \cdot n_s)$ drives denormalization decisions.
- **Distributed Databases**: Network cost dominates, leading to semi-join algorithms.
- **Data Warehousing**: Star-schema queries use hash joins heavily; cost analysis predicts query latency.
- **In-memory Databases (Redis, SAP HANA)**: CPU cost overtakes disk cost, requiring parallel versions of these algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Linear Search vs. Binary Search (Selection)

**Given**:
- Relation $r$ has $b_r = 100$ blocks.
- Predicate: $\sigma_{id=42}(r)$, where $id$ is a key.
- Cost model: $t_{transfer} = 1$ ms, $t_{seek} = 4$ ms (normalized to $t_{seek} = 4 \cdot t_{transfer}$).

**Step 1 — Apply Linear Search Cost Formula**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{A1} &= \bigl\lfloor b_r \mathrel{\big/} 2 \bigr\rfloor \cdot t_{transfer} + 1 \cdot t_{seek} \\
&= \bigl\lfloor 100 \mathrel{\big/} 2 \bigr\rfloor \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 4 \\
&= 50 + 4 \\
&= 54 \text{ time units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume $r$ is sorted; Apply Binary Search Cost Formula**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{A2} &= \bigl\lceil \log_2 b_r \bigr\rceil \cdot (t_{transfer} + t_{seek}) \\
&= \bigl\lceil \log_2 100 \bigr\rceil \cdot (1 + 4) \\
&= 7 \cdot 5 \\
&= 35 \text{ time units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Binary search on a sorted file is faster **only** if sorting cost is amortized. Otherwise, sorting first costs extra $b_r \log b_r = 100 \cdot 7 = 700$ time units. KTU questions often ask you to compute this amortization.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cost of Nested-Loop Join vs. Block Nested-Loop Join

**Given**:
- Outer relation $r$: $b_r = 1000$ blocks, $n_r = 100{,}000$ tuples.
- Inner relation $s$: $b_s = 500$ blocks, $n_s = 50{,}000$ tuples.
- Cost model: $t_{transfer} = t_{seek} = 1$.

**Step 1 — Compute Nested-Loop Join Cost**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{B1} &= b_r + n_r \cdot b_s \\
&= 1000 + (100{,}000) \cdot (500) \\
&= 1000 + 50{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 50{,}001{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Swap roles (smaller outer) to get the same result**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost'_{B1} &= b_s + n_s \cdot b_r \\
&= 500 + 50{,}000 \cdot 1000 \\
&= 50{,}000{,}500 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Block Nested-Loop Join Cost**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{B2} &= b_r \cdot b_s + b_r \\
&= 1000 \cdot 500 + 1000 \\
&= 500{,}000 + 1000 \\
&= 501{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute with buffer pool $M = 102$ blocks** ($M - 2 = 100$ blocks for outer relation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{B2}^{optimized} &= \bigl\lceil b_r \mathrel{\big/} (M-2) \bigr\rceil \cdot b_s + b_r \\
&= \bigl\lceil 1000 \mathrel{\big/} 100 \bigr\rceil \cdot 500 + 1000 \\
&= 10 \cdot 500 + 1000 \\
&= 5000 + 1000 \\
&= 6000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight**: Block nested-loop with a 102-block buffer is **almost 8,500×** faster than naïve nested-loop join. This is why KTU emphasizes buffer pool sizing in cost formulas.

### 3.3 Derivation: Sort-Merge Join Cost (Including Sort Phase)

**Given**:
- $b_r = 100$, $b_s = 200$.
- $M = 12$ buffer blocks.

**Step 1 — Compute number of sort passes for $r$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_r &= \bigl\lceil \log_{M-1}(b_r) \bigr\rceil = \bigl\lceil \log_{11}(100) \bigr\rceil \\
&= \bigl\lceil 1.954 \bigr\rceil = 2 \text{ passes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute number of sort passes for $s$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_s &= \bigl\lceil \log_{M-1}(b_s) \bigr\rceil = \bigl\lceil \log_{11}(200) \bigr\rceil \\
&= \bigl\lceil 2.236 \bigr\rceil = 3 \text{ passes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total cost of sort-merge join**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{B4} &= 2 b_r k_r + 2 b_s k_s + (b_r + b_s) \\
&= 2 \cdot 100 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 200 \cdot 3 + (100 + 200) \\
&= 400 + 1200 + 300 \\
&= 1900 \text{ block transfers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Hash Join**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{B5} &= 3 (b_r + b_s) \\
&= 3 \cdot (100 + 200) \\
&= 900 \text{ block transfers}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion for this example**: Hash join is cheaper (900 vs 1900). However, sort-merge handles range predicates and is more memory-efficient for skewed data.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Join Algorithm?

| Scenario | Best Algorithm | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Small outer, large inner, **no index** | Block Nested-Loop | Avoids tuple-level seeks |
| Inner relation has **good index on join attribute** | Index Nested-Loop | $O(n_r \cdot \log n_s)$ per probe |
| Both relations are **already sorted** | Sort-Merge | Linear in combined size |
| Relations are **large, unsorted, equi-join** | Hash Join | $O(b_r + b_s)$ average case |
| **Distributed** databases | Semi-join, Bloom-join | Minimize network cost |
| **Non-equi** join ($<, >, \neq$) | Nested-Loop or Block NLJ | Sort/hash only support equi-joins |
| **Memory-constrained** ($M$ small) | Sort-Merge | Hash join needs $O(\sqrt{b})$ memory |

### 3.5 Full Worked-Out Numerical Example: Cost Comparison

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark question)**:

> Consider relations $R(A, B)$ and $S(B, C)$. $R$ has $b_R = 1000$ blocks, $n_R = 50{,}000$ tuples. $S$ has $b_S = 500$ blocks, $n_S = 100{,}000$ tuples. There is a primary $\text{B}^+$-tree index on $S.B$ with height $h_i = 2$. Compute the cost of $R \bowtie S$ using: (a) Nested-loop, (b) Block nested-loop, (c) Index nested-loop, and (d) Sort-merge join (assuming unsorted relations, $M = 11$).

**Solution**:

**(a) Nested-Loop Join**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{NL} &= b_R + n_R \cdot b_S \\
&= 1000 + 50{,}000 \cdot 500 \\
&= 25{,}001{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Block Nested-Loop Join** (with $M - 2 = 9$ blocks for $R$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{BNL} &= \bigl\lceil b_R \mathrel{\big/} (M-2) \bigr\rceil \cdot b_S + b_R \\
&= \bigl\lceil 1000 \mathrel{\big/} 9 \bigr\rceil \cdot 500 + 1000 \\
&= 112 \cdot 500 + 1000 \\
&= 56{,}000 + 1000 \\
&= 57{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Index Nested-Loop Join**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{INL} &= b_R + n_R \cdot (h_i + 1) \\
&= 1000 + 50{,}000 \cdot 3 \\
&= 1000 + 150{,}000 \\
&= 151{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Sort-Merge Join** (unsorted; $M = 11$, so $M - 1 = 10$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_R &= \bigl\lceil \log_{10}(1000) \bigr\rceil = 3 \text{ passes} \\
k_S &= \bigl\lceil \log_{10}(500) \bigr\rceil = 3 \text{ passes} \\
Cost_{SM} &= 2 b_R k_R + 2 b_S k_S + (b_R + b_S) \\
&= 2 \cdot 1000 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 500 \cdot 3 + (1000 + 500) \\
&= 6000 + 3000 + 1500 \\
&= 10{,}500 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ranking of algorithms for this scenario (cheapest to most expensive)**:
> 1. Sort-Merge: **10,500**
> 2. Block Nested-Loop: **57,000**
> 3. Index Nested-Loop: **151,000**
> 4. Nested-Loop: **25,001,000**
>
> The optimizer will pick **Sort-Merge Join**. KTU expects you to compute all four and conclude which is best.

### 3.6 Step-by-Step Evaluation of a Relational Expression

**Expression**:
$$E = \Pi_{name, dept}(\sigma_{age > 30}(Employee \bowtie Department))$$

**Step 1 — Without Optimization (Naive Evaluation)**:
1. Compute $Employee \times Department$ (Cartesian product): huge intermediate result.
2. Apply $\sigma_{age > 30}$: scan the massive intermediate.
3. Apply $\Pi_{name, dept}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Equivalence Rule to Push Selection**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \Pi_{name, dept}(\sigma_{age > 30}(Employee \bowtie Department)) \\
&= \Pi_{name, dept}( \sigma_{age > 30}(Employee) \bowtie Department )
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Benefit**: $\sigma_{age > 30}$ is applied to `Employee` *before* the join, drastically reducing input size. This is **selection push-down** — a fundamental heuristic optimization.

**Step 3 — Apply Projection Push-Down**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \Pi_{name, dept}( \sigma_{age > 30}(Employee) \bowtie Department ) \\
&= \Pi_{name, dept}\bigl( \Pi_{id, name, dept}(\sigma_{age > 30}(Employee)) \bowtie \Pi_{dept, dname}(Department) \bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The final optimized plan uses an index on `age` to retrieve matching employees, then joins with `Department` using an index on `dept`. Cost is orders of magnitude lower than the naive plan.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: End-to-End Query Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Submits SQL Query] --> B[Parser and Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[Syntax Tree Generation]
    C --> D[Query Translator]
    D --> E[Initial Relational Algebra Expression]
    E --> F[Heuristic Optimizer]
    F --> G[Cost Based Optimizer]
    G --> H[Query Evaluation Plan]
    H --> I[Execution Engine]
    I --> J[Storage Manager]
    J --> K[Buffer Pool and Disk]
    I --> L[Result Set Returned to User]
    F --> F1[Apply Equivalence Rules]
    F --> F2[Push Selections Down]
    F --> F3[Push Projections Down]
    G --> G1[Estimate Cost per Plan]
    G --> G2[Pick Minimum Cost Plan]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree for Selection Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Selection Operation sigma] --> S1{Is the relation sorted?}
    S1 -- No --> S2{Is there an index?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S3{Equality predicate?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Use Binary Search]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Use Binary Search on range]
    S2 -- No --> S6[Use Linear Search A1]
    S2 -- Yes --> S7{Clustered or Unclustered?}
    S7 -- Clustered --> S8{Key attribute?}
    S7 -- Unclustered --> S9{Key attribute?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S10[Algorithm A3 cost hi+1]
    S8 -- No --> S11[Algorithm A4 cost hi+b]
    S9 -- Yes --> S12[Algorithm Secondary on key hi+1]
    S9 -- No --> S13[Algorithm A5 cost hi+nr worst]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Join Algorithm Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J0[Join Operation r bowtie s] --> J1{Is there an index on inner relation?}
    J1 -- Yes --> J2[Use Index Nested Loop B3]
    J1 -- No --> J3{How much buffer memory M?}
    J3 -- Sufficient --> J4{Are relations sorted?}
    J3 -- Limited --> J5[Use Block Nested Loop B2]
    J4 -- Yes --> J6[Use Sort Merge B4 cost br plus bs]
    J4 -- No --> J7{Is it an equi join?}
    J7 -- Yes --> J8[Use Hash Join B5 cost 3 br plus bs]
    J7 -- No --> J9[Use Block Nested Loop B2]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Pipelined vs. Materialized Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Materialization
        M1[Operator 1] --> M2[Temporary Relation on Disk]
        M2 --> M3[Operator 2]
        M3 --> M4[Temporary Relation on Disk]
        M4 --> M5[Operator 3]
        M5 --> M6[Final Result]
    end
    subgraph Pipelining
        P1[Operator 1] -- tuple stream --> P2[Operator 2]
        P2 -- tuple stream --> P3[Operator 3]
        P3 -- tuple stream --> P4[Final Result]
    end
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of a Cost-Based Optimizer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Parsed Query] --> LOG[Logical Plan Generator]
    LOG --> RULES[Equivalence Rule Engine]
    RULES --> CAND[Set of Logical Plan Candidates]
    CAND --> STATS[Statistics Module: nr, br, V r A, hi]
    CAND --> PHYS[Physical Plan Generator]
    STATS --> PHYS
    PHYS --> COST[Cost Estimator]
    COST --> PICK[Pick Plan with Minimum Cost]
    PICK --> EXEC[Execution Engine]
```

### 4.6 Mermaid Diagram: Expression Tree (Optimized Join)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Project name, dept] --> J[Join]
    J --> S[Select age greater than 30]
    S --> E[Scan Employee with Index on age]
    J --> D[Scan Department with Index on dept]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **measure of query cost**. List any four factors considered while computing the cost of a query.
>
> **Model Answer**:
> The measure of query cost is an estimate of the resources (time, memory, I/O) required by the DBMS to execute a query and produce its result. It is used by the query optimizer to compare alternative evaluation plans and choose the most efficient one.
>
> **Four factors**:
> 1. **Number of block transfers** (reads/writes) between disk and memory.
> 2. **Number of seeks** (random positioning of the disk arm).
> 3. **CPU processing time** for in-memory operations.
> 4. **Memory usage** (size of buffer pool required).
>
> *(Communication cost in distributed DBMSs can be a fifth factor.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between **materialized evaluation** and **pipelined evaluation** of relational-algebra expressions.
>
> **Model Answer**:
>
> | Aspect | Materialization | Pipelining |
> |---|---|---|
> | Intermediate results | Stored as temporary relations on disk | Passed directly between operators in main memory |
> | Cost | High (extra I/O for writing & reading temporaries) | Low (no extra disk I/O) |
> | Implementation | Easy, each operator runs to completion | Complex, uses iterator model with `open`, `next`, `close` |
> | Reusability | Temporaries can be reused | Cannot reuse intermediate results |
> | Latency | Higher (waits for full result) | Lower (streams results as produced) |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

> ### **Question A [14 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
>
> *(a)* [7 Marks] Explain the **sort-merge join** algorithm with a neat diagram. Derive its cost formula and list **two advantages** and **two disadvantages**.
>
> *(b)* [7 Marks] Consider relations $R$ and $S$ with the following parameters:
> - $R$: $b_R = 800$ blocks, $n_R = 40{,}000$ tuples, $V(R, A) = 100$ distinct values.
> - $S$: $b_S = 1200$ blocks, $n_S = 60{,}000$ tuples.
> - A **primary index** exists on $R.A$ with $h_i = 3$.
>
> Compute the cost of $\sigma_{A = v}(R)$ using: (i) Linear search, (ii) Primary index on key, (iii) Primary index on non-key. Assume $v$ matches **100 tuples**.

> **Model Solution to Question A**:
>
> **(a) Sort-Merge Join Algorithm**:
> **Step 1 — Sort phase**: If not already sorted, sort both $r$ and $s$ on the join attribute using external merge sort.
> **Step 2 — Merge phase**: Use two pointers; advance the pointer with the smaller value, emit $(t_r, t_s)$ pairs when values are equal.
>
> **Cost Formula** (if both sorted): $Cost = b_r + b_s$.
> If sorting is required: $Cost = b_r \log_2 b_r + b_s \log_2 b_s + b_r + b_s$.
>
> **Diagram**:
> ```
> r (sorted) → [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]   -- pointer i
> s (sorted) → [2, 3, 5, 8, 9]   -- pointer j
>                    ↑ ↑     ↑ ↑
>                matched pairs: (3,3), (5,5), (9,9)
> ```
>
> **Advantages**: (1) Linear cost on pre-sorted data; (2) Handles equi-joins efficiently even on large data.
> **Disadvantages**: (1) High sort cost if data unsorted; (2) Cannot handle non-equi joins.
>
> **(b) Cost Computations**:
>
> *(i) Linear Search* (no sort, no index used):
> $$Cost = b_R = 800 \text{ units}$$
>
> *(ii) Primary Index on Key* (only one tuple matches, but $V(R,A) = 100$ means *non-key*; we interpret as key by assuming $v$ matches 1 tuple):
> $$Cost = h_i + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ units}$$
>
> *(iii) Primary Index on Non-Key* (100 matches):
> $$b = \bigl\lceil n_R \mathrel{\big/} V(R,A) \bigr\rceil = \bigl\lceil 40{,}000 \mathrel{\big/} 100 \bigr\rceil = 400 \text{ blocks}$$
> $$Cost = h_i + b = 3 + 400 = 403 \text{ units}$$

> ### **Question B [14 Marks]** (Alternative Choice)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary 2024]**
>
> *(a)* [7 Marks] Explain **nested-loop join** and **block nested-loop join** algorithms. Compare their cost formulas and explain under what conditions block nested-loop is preferred.
>
> *(b)* [7 Marks] Given $R(A, B)$ with $b_R = 500$, $n_R = 10{,}000$ and $S(B, C)$ with $b_S = 300$, $n_S = 15{,}000$. A **secondary $\text{B}^+$-tree index** on $S.B$ has $h_i = 2$. Compute the cost of $R \bowtie S$ using: (i) Nested-loop join, (ii) Block nested-loop with $M = 12$, (iii) Index nested-loop join, (iv) Hash join.

> **Model Solution to Question B**:
>
> **(a) Nested-Loop vs. Block Nested-Loop**:
> - **Nested-Loop**: For every tuple in outer $r$, scan the entire inner $s$. Cost = $b_r + n_r \cdot b_s$. Disadvantage: excessive seeks.
> - **Block Nested-Loop**: For every **block** of $r$ (not tuple), scan $s$ once. Cost = $b_r \cdot b_s + b_r$ (or optimized as $\lceil b_r \mathrel{\big/} (M-2) \rceil \cdot b_s + b_r$ with buffer).
> - **When BNLJ is preferred**: When memory is sufficient to hold multiple blocks of $r$ and one block of $s$, drastically reducing the number of $s$-scans.
>
> **(b) Cost Computations**:
>
> *(i) Nested-Loop Join*:
> $$Cost = b_R + n_R \cdot b_S = 500 + 10{,}000 \cdot 300 = 3{,}000{,}500 \text{ units}$$
>
> *(ii) Block Nested-Loop Join* ($M - 2 = 10$):
> $$Cost = \bigl\lceil b_R \mathrel{\big/} (M-2) \bigr\rceil \cdot b_S + b_R = \bigl\lceil 500 \mathrel{\big/} 10 \bigr\rceil \cdot 300 + 500 = 50 \cdot 300 + 500 = 15{,}500 \text{ units}$$
>
> *(iii) Index Nested-Loop Join* (secondary index):
> $$Cost = b_R + n_R \cdot (h_i + 1) = 500 + 10{,}000 \cdot 3 = 30{,}500 \text{ units}$$
>
> *(iv) Hash Join*:
> $$Cost = 3 (b_R + b_S) = 3 \cdot (500 + 300) = 2400 \text{ units}$$
>
> **Best algorithm**: Hash Join (2400 units) ≪ Block NLJ (15,500) ≪ Index NLJ (30,500) ≪ Nested-Loop (3,000,500).

> [!WARNING]
> ### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Cost formula must be quoted correctly**. A common mistake is to write $b_r \cdot b_s$ for nested-loop join — the correct formula is $n_r \cdot b_s + b_r$. Losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Buffer pool parameter $M$ is mandatory** in BNLJ cost. Many students forget to subtract 2 (one for inner, one for output). Losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Sort-Merge cost has two cases**: sorted and unsorted. Always specify which. Losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Hash Join cost is $3(b_r + b_s)$** in the simplified model, not $2(b_r + b_s)$. The factor 3 accounts for partition + probe passes.
> 5. **In index nested-loop, the index attribute is on the *inner* relation**. The question may be sneaky and put the index on the outer — read carefully.
> 6. **Always conclude with a ranking** ("Therefore, algorithm X is optimal because…"). This earns 1–2 bonus marks for "engineering judgment".

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> ### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam

- ✅ **Query Processing** has 3 phases: *Translation → Optimization → Execution*.
- ✅ **Measure of Query Cost** = block transfers + seeks (+ CPU + memory in advanced models).
- ✅ **Linear Search cost** = $b_r$ (or $b_r \mathrel{\big/} 2$ for key equality).
- ✅ **Binary Search cost** = $\lceil \log_2 b_r \rceil$ — only on **sorted** files.
- ✅ **Primary index cost (key, equality)** = $h_i + 1$.
- ✅ **Primary index cost (non-key)** = $h_i + b$ where $b = n_r \mathrel{\big/} V(r, A)$.
- ✅ **Secondary index cost (non-key)** = $h_i + n_r$ in the worst case.
- ✅ **Nested-Loop Join cost** = $n_r \cdot b_s + b_r$; always make the **smaller relation the outer**.
- ✅ **Block Nested-Loop cost** = $b_r \cdot b_s + b_r$; optimized with buffer: $\lceil b_r \mathrel{\big/} (M-2) \rceil \cdot b_s + b_r$.
- ✅ **Index Nested-Loop cost** = $b_r + n_r \cdot (h_i + 1)$ (best when index exists on inner).
- ✅ **Sort-Merge cost (sorted)** = $b_r + b_s$; **(unsorted)** = $b_r \log b_r + b_s \log b_s + b_r + b_s$.
- ✅ **Hash Join cost** = $3(b_r + b_s)$ — only for **equi-joins**.
- ✅ **Materialization** writes intermediate results to disk; **Pipelining** streams them.
- ✅ **Heuristic rules**: Push selections *down* (before joins); push projections *down* (before joins).
- ✅ **Joins are commutative and associative** — reorder them for efficiency.
- ✅ Always **state assumptions** (sorted/unsorted, indexed/not, $M$ value) before computing cost.
- ✅ Always **conclude with a comparison** and pick the cheapest algorithm.

> **Mnemonic for Selection Algorithms**: **"L**inear is **L**azy, **B**inary needs **B**ooks (sorted), **P**rimary index is **P**recise, **S**econdary is **S**lower." 
> **Mnemonic for Join Algorithms**: **"N**aïve is **N**otoriously slow, **B**lock **B**eats it, **I**ndex **I**s ideal, **S**ort-**M**erge is **S**mooth, **H**ash is **H**ot for equi-joins." 
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heuristics in Query Optimization: Optimization of Relational Algebra Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST634 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heuristics in Query Optimization: Optimization of Relational Algebra Expressions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Heuristic Query Optimization** is a rule-based approach to query optimization that transforms a relational algebra expression into an equivalent, semantically identical expression that is computationally more efficient, by applying a fixed set of transformation rules (equivalence rules) without estimating actual execution costs.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, heuristic optimization uses **algebraic equivalence rules** to systematically rearrange the query tree, primarily focusing on:
- Pushing **selection operations (σ)** as far down the tree as possible.
- Replacing **Cartesian products** combined with selections by **join operations**.
- Pushing **projection operations (π)** down, while eliminating unwanted attributes early.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** Heuristic optimization is the process of converting the initial query tree (canonical form) into an optimized query tree by applying relational algebra equivalence transformations that reduce the size of intermediate relations and the cost of execution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are preparing a dish using a recipe that calls for ingredients A, B, C, D, and E. The **canonical (unoptimized) recipe** says:
1. Chop all ingredients (A, B, C, D, E).
2. Mix everything together.
3. Cook the whole mixture.
4. Then finally remove the unwanted parts (say, peel pieces).

Now an **optimized chef** (the heuristic optimizer) would re-order this:
1. **Peel and chop only A and C** (because the others B, D, E are already perfect).
2. Remove unwanted pieces *immediately*.
3. Mix the smaller, pre-cleaned set.
4. Cook.

The output dish is the same, but the chef works on a *smaller quantity* of items at every stage, finishing much faster. **Pushing selections early = peeling early**, **Pushing projections = chopping to only needed sizes**, and **Joining before Cartesian product = mixing intelligently instead of blind mixing**.

## 1.3 Canonical vs Optimized Query Tree

| Aspect | Canonical Query Tree | Optimized Query Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Order of Operations | From leaves (tables) to root (output) | Same structure, but reordered for efficiency |
| Selections | Applied at the top | Pushed down close to base relations |
| Projections | At the top only | Pushed down with attribute dependencies |
| Joins | Often Cartesian products with selections | Direct theta/equi-joins |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Heuristic optimization does NOT guarantee the absolute best plan, but it provides a *very good* plan using polynomial-time transformations, whereas cost-based optimization explores an exponential search space.

## 1.4 Why Heuristic Optimization is Critical

- **Reduces I/O cost**: Smaller intermediate relations mean fewer disk pages read/written.
- **Reduces CPU cost**: Fewer tuples to process, compare, and join.
- **Reduces communication cost** (distributed DBs): Smaller data transmitted over network.
- **Polynomial time**: O(n) to O(n²) rules applied, vs exponential cost-based search.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Query Optimization

1. **Heuristic (Logical) Optimization** — Uses equivalence rules (algebraic transformations).
2. **Cost-Based (Physical) Optimization** — Uses statistical estimates of size and cost.

> Heuristics are applied *first* to reduce the search space; cost-based then picks the best among the surviving candidates.

## 2.2 Equivalence Rules of Relational Algebra (KTU High-Yield Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following rules form the **backbone of KTU exam questions**. Memorize these symbol-by-symbol.

### Rule Group A — Selection Equivalences

| # | Rule | Name |
|---|------|------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{c_1 \wedge c_2}(R) \equiv \sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R))$ | Cascade of σ |
| 2 | $\sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R)) \equiv \sigma_{c_2}(\sigma_{c_1}(R))$ | Commutativity of σ |
| 3 | $\sigma_c(R \times S) \equiv R \bowtie_c S$ | Recognition of θ-join |
| 4 | $\sigma_{c_1 \wedge c_2}(R \bowtie S) \equiv \sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R \bowtie S))$ | Cascade in join |

### Rule Group B — Projection Equivalences

| # | Rule | Name |
|---|------|------|
| 5 | $\pi_{L_1}(\pi_{L_2}(R)) \equiv \pi_{L_1}(R)$ where $L_1 \subseteq L_2$ | Cascade of π |
| 6 | $\pi_L(\sigma_c(R)) \equiv \pi_L(\sigma_c(\pi_{L \cup \{A_i\}}(R)))$ where $A_i \in c$ | Push π past σ |

### Rule Group C — Commutativity & Associativity of Joins

| # | Rule | Name |
|---|------|------|
| 7 | $R \bowtie_c S \equiv S \bowtie_c R$ | Commutativity |
| 8 | $(R \bowtie S) \bowtie T \equiv R \bowtie (S \bowtie T)$ | Associativity |

### Rule Group D — Distributive Laws

| # | Rule | Name |
|---|------|------|
| 9 | $\sigma_c(R \bowtie S) \equiv (\sigma_c(R)) \bowtie S$ if $c$ involves only $R$ | Push σ past join |
| 10 | $\pi_L(R \cup S) \equiv \pi_L(R) \cup \pi_L(S)$ | Distribute π over ∪ |
| 11 | $\sigma_c(R \cup S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) \cup \sigma_c(S)$ | Distribute σ over ∪ |
| 12 | $\sigma_c(R - S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) - \sigma_c(S)$ | Distribute σ over − |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Heuristic Algorithm

The standard KTU algorithm proceeds in the following sequence:

1. **Decompose** any selection condition with AND (∧) into a cascade of single-condition selections (Rule 1).
2. **Push selections** down the query tree as far as possible, using Rules 3 and 9 (selections move toward base relations).
3. **Recognize and convert** any remaining Cartesian product + selection combinations into θ-joins (Rule 3).
4. **Reorder joins** so that the most restrictive join (smallest intermediate result) is performed first (Rules 7, 8).
5. **Combine projections** with selections (Rule 6) and push projections down using Rule 5.
6. **Eliminate redundant attributes** introduced by intermediate operations.
7. **Identify subexpressions** that appear multiple times for shared computation (advanced heuristic).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Engines (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle):** Heuristic rules form the first stage of the planner.
- **Big Data (Spark SQL, Hive):** Catalyst optimizer uses similar rule-based transformations.
- **Distributed Systems (Hadoop, NoSQL):** Reducing intermediate data sizes is critical for network efficiency.
- **OLAP Data Warehouses:** Star-schema queries heavily depend on join reordering heuristics.

## 2.5 Push-Down Principle — The Golden Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Heuristic Optimization:** Always perform **selection (σ)** operations *before* **join (⋈)** operations, and **projection (π)** operations *before* **selection**, where possible. This minimizes the cardinality of intermediate relations, which is the single biggest cost driver in relational query processing.

### Cost Justification

The cost of a join is roughly proportional to the product of operand sizes:

$$C_{\text{join}}(R, S) \approx \mid R \mid \times \mid S \mid$$

Reducing $\mid R \mid$ or $\mid S \mid$ via an early $\sigma$ dramatically lowers this cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Example: Full Heuristic Optimization

### Given Schema

- **EMP**(<u>ENO</u>, ENAME, SALARY, DNO)
- **DEPT**(<u>DNO</u>, DNAME, DLOCATION)
- **PROJECT**(<u>PNO</u>, PNAME, DNO, BUDGET)

### Original Query (Q1)

> *"Retrieve the names and salaries of all employees working on project 'DBMS' whose salary is greater than 50,000."*

### Step 1 — Express in SQL

```sql
SELECT E.ENAME, E.SALARY
FROM EMP E, DEPT D, PROJECT P
WHERE E.DNO = D.DNO
  AND D.DNO = P.DNO
  AND P.PNAME = 'DBMS'
  AND E.SALARY > 50000;
```

### Step 2 — Initial Canonical Relational Algebra Expression

$$
\pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY}} \Big( \sigma_{(\text{E.DNO} = \text{D.DNO}) \wedge (\text{D.DNO} = \text{P.DNO}) \wedge (\text{P.PNAME} = \text{DBMS'}) \wedge (\text{E.SALARY} > 50000)} \big( \text{EMP} \times \text{DEPT} \times \text{PROJECT} \big) \Big)
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Rule 1 (Cascade of σ)

Break the conjunctive condition into a cascade:

$$
\pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY}} \Big( \sigma_{\text{E.DNO} = \text{D.DNO}} \big( \sigma_{\text{D.DNO} = \text{P.DNO}} \big( \sigma_{\text{P.PNAME} = \text{'DBMS'}} \big( \sigma_{\text{E.SALARY} > 50000} \big( \text{EMP} \times \text{DEPT} \times \text{PROJECT} \big) \big) \big) \big) \Big)
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Rule 9 (Push σ past Cartesian product)

The condition `E.SALARY > 50000` involves only EMP, so push it down:

$$
\pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY}} \Big( \sigma_{\text{E.DNO} = \text{D.DNO}} \big( \sigma_{\text{D.DNO} = \text{P.DNO}} \big( \sigma_{\text{P.PNAME} = \text{'DBMS'}} \big( \big( \sigma_{\text{E.SALARY} > 50000}(\text{EMP}) \big) \times \text{DEPT} \times \text{PROJECT} \big) \big) \big) \Big)
$$

### Step 5 — Push `PNAME = 'DBMS'` down to PROJECT

$$
\pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY}} \Big( \sigma_{\text{E.DNO} = \text{D.DNO}} \big( \sigma_{\text{D.DNO} = \text{P.DNO}} \big( \big( \sigma_{\text{E.SALARY} > 50000}(\text{EMP}) \big) \times \text{DEPT} \times \big( \sigma_{\text{P.PNAME} = \text{'DBMS'}}(\text{PROJECT}) \big) \big) \big) \Big)
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Rule 3 (Recognize θ-joins)

Replace $\sigma_{\text{E.DNO} = \text{D.DNO}}( \text{EMP} \times \text{DEPT} )$ with $\text{EMP} \bowtie_{\text{E.DNO} = \text{D.DNO}} \text{DEPT}$:

$$
\pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY}} \Big( \sigma_{\text{D.DNO} = \text{P.DNO}} \Big( \big( \big( \sigma_{\text{E.SALARY} > 50000}(\text{EMP}) \bowtie_{\text{E.DNO} = \text{D.DNO}} \text{DEPT} \big) \big) \times \big( \sigma_{\text{P.PNAME} = \text{'DBMS'}}(\text{PROJECT}) \big) \Big) \Big)
$$

### Step 7 — Combine the second join

$$
\pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY}} \Big( \big( \big( \sigma_{\text{E.SALARY} > 50000}(\text{EMP}) \bowtie_{\text{E.DNO} = \text{D.DNO}} \text{DEPT} \big) \bowtie_{\text{D.DNO} = \text{P.DNO}} \big( \sigma_{\text{P.PNAME} = \text{'DBMS'}}(\text{PROJECT}) \big) \Big) \Big)
$$

### Step 8 — Apply Rule 6 (Push π past selections where possible)

Since the output only needs `ENAME` and `SALARY`, the intermediate projection after the join should retain `ENAME, SALARY, DNO` (DNO is needed for the join):

$$
\pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY}} \Big( \pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY, DNO}} \big( \sigma_{\text{E.SALARY} > 50000}(\text{EMP}) \bowtie_{\text{E.DNO} = \text{D.DNO}} \text{DEPT} \big) \bowtie_{\text{D.DNO} = \text{P.DNO}} \big( \sigma_{\text{P.PNAME} = \text{'DBMS'}}(\text{PROJECT}) \big) \Big)
$$

### Final Optimized Expression

$$
\boxed{
\pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY}} \Big( \pi_{\text{ENAME, SALARY, DNO}} \big( \sigma_{\text{E.SALARY} > 50000}(\text{EMP}) \bowtie_{\text{E.DNO} = \text{D.DNO}} \pi_{\text{DNO}}(\text{DEPT}) \big) \bowtie_{\text{DNO}} \pi_{\text{DNO}}(\sigma_{\text{P.PNAME} = \text{'DBMS'}}(\text{PROJECT})) \Big)
}
$$

## 3.2 Quantitative Cost Comparison

| Step | Operation | Input Cardinality | Output Cardinality |
|------|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Unoptimized: `EMP × DEPT × PROJECT` | Cartesian Product | $100 \times 20 \times 50$ | $100{,}000$ tuples |
| Optimized: `σ(E.SALARY>50000)(EMP)` | Selection | $100$ | $30$ |
| Optimized: `σ(PNAME='DBMS')(PROJECT)` | Selection | $50$ | $5$ |
| Optimized: `EMP ⋈ DEPT ⋈ PROJECT` | Joins | $30 \times 20 \times 5$ | $\approx 3000$ tuples |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Reduction:** The optimized plan processes **~30× fewer tuples** in the final join, which translates to a proportional reduction in disk I/O and CPU consumption.

## 3.3 Verification of Equivalence (Proof Sketch)

To prove Rule 9: $\sigma_c(R \times S) \equiv (\sigma_c(R)) \times S$ when $c$ references only $R$'s attributes.

Let $t \in \sigma_c(R \times S)$. Then:
- $t = \langle r, s \rangle$ where $r \in R$, $s \in S$.
- $c(r)$ evaluates to TRUE.

Conversely, for $t = \langle r, s \rangle$ where $r \in \sigma_c(R)$ and $s \in S$:
- Since $c$ depends only on attributes of $r$, $c(r)$ = TRUE.
- So $t \in R \times S$ and $c$ holds, meaning $t \in \sigma_c(R \times S)$.

Therefore, the two sets are equal by mutual containment.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Heuristics Fail

| Scenario | Heuristic Behaviour | Limitation |
|----------|---------------------|------------|
| Low-selectivity σ (returns 90% of tuples) | Pushes σ down | Wasted work — σ should be done later |
| Indexed attribute access | May not utilize index | Cost-based optimizer would prefer index scan |
| Many joins (>10 tables) | Greedy join reordering | May miss optimal join order |
| Skewed data distribution | Assumes uniform selectivity | Heuristic ignores data distribution |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical vs Optimized Query Tree Transformation

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph CANONICAL["CANONICAL QUERY TREE"]
        A1[pi ENAME SALARY] --> A2[sigma E.DNO=D.DNO AND D.DNO=P.DNO AND PNAME=DBMS AND SALARY gt 50000]
        A2 --> A3[X Cartesian Product]
        A3 --> A4[EMP]
        A3 --> A5[DEPT]
        A3 --> A6[PROJECT]
    end
```

## 4.2 Optimized Query Tree (Step-by-Step Result)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph OPTIMIZED["OPTIMIZED QUERY TREE"]
        B1[pi ENAME SALARY] --> B2[pi ENAME SALARY DNO]
        B2 --> B3[bowtie DNO equi join]
        B3 --> B4[bowtie E.DNO equals D.DNO]
        B4 --> B5[sigma E.SALARY gt 50000]
        B5 --> B6[EMP]
        B4 --> B7[pi DNO]
        B7 --> B8[DEPT]
        B3 --> B9[pi DNO]
        B9 --> B10[sigma PNAME equals DBMS]
        B10 --> B11[PROJECT]
    end
```

## 4.3 Heuristic Optimization Workflow (Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start: Parse SQL Query]) --> PARSE[Generate Canonical RA Expression]
    PARSE --> S1[Step 1: Decompose conjunctive selections into cascade]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Push selections down using commutativity and distributive laws]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Recognize Cartesian product plus selection as theta joins]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Reorder joins most restrictive first]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Push projections down retaining only needed attributes]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Eliminate duplicate attributes and subexpressions]
    S6 --> DONE([Optimized RA Expression Ready for Execution])
```

## 4.4 Equivalence Rule Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LEVEL1["SELECTION RULES"]
        L1A[Rule 1 Cascade of sigma]
        L1B[Rule 2 Commutativity of sigma]
        L1C[Rule 9 Push sigma past join]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL2["PROJECTION RULES"]
        L2A[Rule 5 Cascade of pi]
        L2B[Rule 6 Push pi past sigma]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL3["JOIN RULES"]
        L3A[Rule 3 Recognize theta join]
        L3B[Rule 7 Commutativity of join]
        L3C[Rule 8 Associativity of join]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL4["SET OPERATION RULES"]
        L4A[Rule 11 Push sigma past union]
        L4B[Rule 12 Push sigma past difference]
    end
    LEVEL1 --> LEVEL3
    LEVEL2 --> LEVEL3
    LEVEL3 --> LEVEL4
```

## 4.5 Trade-Off Matrix: Heuristic vs Cost-Based Optimization

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HEURISTIC["HEURISTIC OPTIMIZER"]
        H1[Input: RA Expression]
        H2[Apply Algebraic Rules]
        H3[Output: One Good Plan]
        H1 --> H2 --> H3
    end
    subgraph COSTBASED["COST-BASED OPTIMIZER"]
        C1[Input: Multiple Logical Plans]
        C2[Estimate Cost Using Statistics]
        C3[Output: Best Plan by Cost]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3
    end
    HEURISTIC -. Faster but may be suboptimal .-> COSTBASED
```
