# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define heuristic optimization in query processing. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Heuristic optimization in query processing is a rule-based technique used by the query optimizer to transform a relational algebra expression into an equivalent but more efficient form. It uses a fixed set of algebraic equivalence rules to push selections down the query tree, replace Cartesian products with joins, and project only the required attributes early. The primary goals are to reduce the size of intermediate relations and the number of tuples processed during execution. Unlike cost-based optimization, heuristic optimization does not rely on statistical data and runs in polynomial time, providing a fast but not always optimal solution.

---

### Question 2: State any three equivalence rules used in heuristic query optimization. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cascade of Selection (σ):**
   $\sigma_{c_1 \wedge c_2}(R) \equiv \sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R))$
   A conjunctive selection can be broken into a sequence of individual selections.

2. **Commutativity of Selection (σ):**
   $\sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R)) \equiv \sigma_{c_2}(\sigma_{c_1}(R))$
   Selections can be applied in any order.

3. **Recognition of θ-Join:**
   $\sigma_c(R \times S) \equiv R \bowtie_c S$
   A Cartesian product followed by a selection is equivalent to a theta join.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question Choice A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of heuristic optimization of relational algebra expressions with the help of a suitable example. Discuss the role of equivalence rules. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The heuristic optimization of relational algebra expressions involves the systematic application of algebraic equivalence rules to transform a given query into a more efficient equivalent form. The procedure involves the following steps:

**Step 1 — Decomposition of Selections:**
Any selection condition with conjunctive (AND) predicates is decomposed into a cascade of individual selections using the cascade rule:
$\sigma_{c_1 \wedge c_2}(R) \equiv \sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R))$

**Step 2 — Push Selections Down:**
Using commutativity and distributive laws, selections are pushed down the query tree toward the base relations. This is justified because the cost of selections is much lower when applied to smaller input sets.

**Step 3 — Recognition of Joins:**
Any remaining Cartesian product combined with a selection is converted into a theta join, which is significantly more efficient.

**Step 4 — Reordering of Joins:**
Joins are reordered using commutativity and associativity rules so that the most selective (smallest result) joins are executed first, minimizing intermediate relation sizes.

**Step 5 — Push Projections Down:**
Projection operations are pushed down the tree, retaining only the attributes needed downstream, thereby reducing tuple width and I/O.

**Step 6 — Elimination of Duplicates:**
Cascade projection rules are applied to remove duplicate attribute computations.

**Example:** Consider the query *"Find names of employees in department 'CSE' earning more than 60,000."*

Initial expression:
$\pi_{\text{ENAME}}(\sigma_{\text{DNAME} = \text{'CSE'} \wedge \text{SALARY} > 60000}(\text{EMP} \bowtie \text{DEPT}))$

After applying the push-down rules:
$\pi_{\text{ENAME}}( (\sigma_{\text{SALARY} > 60000}(\text{EMP})) \bowtie (\sigma_{\text{DNAME} = \text{'CSE'}}(\text{DEPT})) )$

This optimized form performs selections on smaller individual relations before the expensive join operation, drastically reducing the tuples that participate in the join.

**Role of Equivalence Rules:** Equivalence rules provide the formal mathematical foundation to guarantee that the transformations preserve the semantics of the original query. They ensure correctness while enabling optimization.

---

**(b)** With suitable equivalence rules, show how selection operations are pushed down through join operations. Apply these rules to optimize the following query: *"Find the names of all employees working on the project 'AI' belonging to the 'Research' department."* Given: **EMP(<u>ENO</u>, ENAME, DNO, SALARY)**, **DEPT(<u>DNO</u>, DNAME)**, **PROJECT(<u>PNO</u>, PNAME, DNO)**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Equivalence Rules Used:**

1. $\sigma_c(R \bowtie S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) \bowtie S$ — when $c$ involves only $R$ (Rule 9)
2. $\sigma_{c_1 \wedge c_2}(R \bowtie S) \equiv \sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R \bowtie S))$ — Cascade rule (Rule 1)
3. $\sigma_c(R \times S) \equiv R \bowtie_c S$ — Recognition of join (Rule 3)
4. $R \bowtie S \equiv S \bowtie R$ — Commutativity (Rule 7)

**SQL Form of the Query:**
```sql
SELECT E.ENAME
FROM EMP E, DEPT D, PROJECT P
WHERE E.DNO = D.DNO
  AND D.DNO = P.DNO
  AND P.PNAME = 'AI'
  AND D.DNAME = 'Research';
```

**Initial Canonical Expression:**
$\pi_{\text{ENAME}}\big( \sigma_{(\text{E.DNO}=\text{D.DNO}) \wedge (\text{D.DNO}=\text{P.DNO}) \wedge (\text{P.PNAME}=\text{'AI'}) \wedge (\text{D.DNAME}=\text{'Research'})} (\text{EMP} \times \text{DEPT} \times \text{PROJECT}) \big)$

**Optimization Steps:**

Step 1 — Decompose conjunctive selection into a cascade:
$\pi_{\text{ENAME}}\big( \sigma_{\text{E.DNO}=\text{D.DNO}}(\sigma_{\text{D.DNO}=\text{P.DNO}}(\sigma_{\text{P.PNAME}=\text{'AI'}}(\sigma_{\text{D.DNAME}=\text{'Research'}}(\text{EMP} \times \text{DEPT} \times \text{PROJECT})))) \big)$

Step 2 — Push `PNAME = 'AI'` down (involves only PROJECT):
$\pi_{\text{ENAME}}\big( \sigma_{\text{E.DNO}=\text{D.DNO}}(\sigma_{\text{D.DNO}=\text{P.DNO}}(\sigma_{\text{D.DNAME}=\text{'Research'}}(\text{EMP} \times \text{DEPT} \times \sigma_{\text{PNAME}=\text{'AI'}}(\text{PROJECT}))))) \big)$

Step 3 — Push `DNAME = 'Research'` down (involves only DEPT):
$\pi_{\text{ENAME}}\big( \sigma_{\text{E.DNO}=\text{D.DNO}}(\sigma_{\text{D.DNO}=\text{P.DNO}}(\text{EMP} \times \sigma_{\text{DNAME}=\text{'Research'}}(\text{DEPT}) \times \sigma_{\text{PNAME}=\text{'AI'}}(\text{PROJECT})))) \big)$

Step 4 — Recognize joins (Rule 3):
$\pi_{\text{ENAME}}\big( (\text{EMP} \bowtie_{\text{E.DNO}=\text{D.DNO}} \sigma_{\text{DNAME}=\text{'Research'}}(\text{DEPT})) \bowtie_{\text{D.DNO}=\text{P.DNO}} \sigma_{\text{PNAME}=\text{'AI'}}(\text{PROJECT}) \big)$

Step 5 — Add final projection push:
$\pi_{\text{ENAME}}\big( \pi_{\text{ENAME, DNO}}(\text{EMP} \bowtie_{\text{E.DNO}=\text{D.DNO}} \pi_{\text{DNO}}(\sigma_{\text{DNAME}=\text{'Research'}}(\text{DEPT}))) \bowtie_{\text{DNO}} \pi_{\text{DNO}}(\sigma_{\text{PNAME}=\text{'AI'}}(\text{PROJECT})) \big)$

The optimized query applies cheap selections first, reducing the size of intermediate relations before any join occurs — yielding significant performance gains.

---

### 📌 Question Choice B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** List and explain the equivalence transformation rules for relational algebra operations with proper examples. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Equivalence rules are identities that hold between two relational algebra expressions, meaning they always produce the same result for any database instance. They are categorized as follows:

**1. Selection Equivalences:**

- **Cascade Rule:** $\sigma_{c_1 \wedge c_2}(R) \equiv \sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R))$
  *Example:* $\sigma_{\text{age}>20 \wedge \text{salary}>50000}(\text{EMP}) \equiv \sigma_{\text{age}>20}(\sigma_{\text{salary}>50000}(\text{EMP}))$

- **Commutativity:** $\sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R)) \equiv \sigma_{c_2}(\sigma_{c_1}(R))$
  *Example:* The order of applying `age>20` and `salary>50000` does not affect the result.

- **Distributivity over Set Operations:** $\sigma_c(R \cup S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) \cup \sigma_c(S)$
  *Example:* Filter a union by filtering each operand separately.

**2. Projection Equivalences:**

- **Cascade Rule:** $\pi_{L_1}(\pi_{L_2}(R)) \equiv \pi_{L_1}(R)$ when $L_1 \subseteq L_2$
  *Example:* Projecting `ENAME` then `ENAME, SALARY` is equivalent to just projecting `ENAME, SALARY`.

- **Distributivity over Union:** $\pi_L(R \cup S) \equiv \pi_L(R) \cup \pi_L(S)$

**3. Join and Cartesian Product Equivalences:**

- **Recognize θ-Join:** $\sigma_c(R \times S) \equiv R \bowtie_c S$
  *Example:* A Cartesian product with an equality filter is the same as an equi-join.

- **Commutativity:** $R \bowtie S \equiv S \bowtie R$
  *Example:* EMP ⋈ DEPT produces the same result as DEPT ⋈ EMP.

- **Associativity:** $(R \bowtie S) \bowtie T \equiv R \bowtie (S \bowtie T)$
  *Example:* The grouping of joins does not affect the final result, allowing reordering.

**4. Selection with Join Distributivity:**

$\sigma_c(R \bowtie S) \equiv (\sigma_c(R)) \bowtie S$ when $c$ involves only attributes of $R$.
*Example:* Filter EMP for `SALARY > 50000` before joining with DEPT.

**5. Projection with Join Distributivity:**

$\pi_L(R \bowtie_c S) \equiv \pi_L\big( (\pi_{L_1}(R)) \bowtie_c (\pi_{L_2}(S)) \big)$ where $L_1, L_2$ include join attributes and attributes needed in $L$.

These rules form the foundation for heuristic query optimization, enabling the optimizer to reorder operations safely while preserving query semantics.

---

**(b)** Given the schemas **STUDENT(<u>ROLL</u>, NAME, AGE, DEPT_ID)** and **DEPARTMENT(<u>DEPT_ID</u>, DEPT_NAME, HOD)**. Optimize the following relational algebra expression using heuristic rules: $\pi_{\text{NAME, DEPT\_NAME}} \big( \sigma_{\text{AGE} > 20 \wedge \text{DEPT\_NAME} = \text{'CSE'}} (\text{STUDENT} \times \text{DEPARTMENT}) \big)$ **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Expression:**
$E = \pi_{\text{NAME, DEPT\_NAME}} \big( \sigma_{\text{AGE} > 20 \wedge \text{DEPT\_NAME} = \text{'CSE'}} (\text{STUDENT} \times \text{DEPARTMENT}) \big)$

**Step 1 — Apply Cascade of σ (Rule 1):**
$E = \pi_{\text{NAME, DEPT\_NAME}} \big( \sigma_{\text{AGE} > 20}( \sigma_{\text{DEPT\_NAME} = \text{'CSE'}} (\text{STUDENT} \times \text{DEPARTMENT}) ) \big)$

**Step 2 — Apply Distributive Rule (σ over × when condition involves only one operand):**
Since `AGE > 20` involves only STUDENT and `DEPT_NAME = 'CSE'` involves only DEPARTMENT:
$E = \pi_{\text{NAME, DEPT\_NAME}} \big( \sigma_{\text{AGE} > 20}(\text{STUDENT}) \times \sigma_{\text{DEPT\_NAME} = \text{'CSE'}}(\text{DEPARTMENT}) \big)$

**Step 3 — Recognize θ-Join (Rule 3):**
$E = \pi_{\text{NAME, DEPT\_NAME}} \big( \sigma_{\text{AGE} > 20}(\text{STUDENT}) \bowtie \sigma_{\text{DEPT\_NAME} = \text{'CSE'}}(\text{DEPARTMENT}) \big)$
*Note:* Join condition is `STUDENT.DEPT_ID = DEPARTMENT.DEPT_ID`.

**Step 4 — Apply Projection Cascade Rule (Rule 6):**
Only `NAME`, `DEPT_NAME`, and `DEPT_ID` (for join) are needed:
$E = \pi_{\text{NAME, DEPT\_NAME}} \big( \pi_{\text{NAME, DEPT\_ID}}(\sigma_{\text{AGE} > 20}(\text{STUDENT})) \bowtie \pi_{\text{DEPT\_ID, DEPT\_NAME}}(\sigma_{\text{DEPT\_NAME} = \text{'CSE'}}(\text{DEPARTMENT})) \big)$

**Final Optimized Expression:**
$$
\boxed{E_{\text{opt}} = \pi_{\text{NAME, DEPT\_NAME}} \Big( \pi_{\text{NAME, DEPT\_ID}}\big(\sigma_{\text{AGE} > 20}(\text{STUDENT})\big) \bowtie_{\text{DEPT\_ID}} \pi_{\text{DEPT\_ID, DEPT\_NAME}}\big(\sigma_{\text{DEPT\_NAME} = \text{'CSE'}}(\text{DEPARTMENT})\big) \Big)}
$$

**Optimization Benefits:**
- Selections reduce each operand *before* the costly join.
- Projections eliminate unwanted attributes early.
- The final join operates on much smaller intermediate relations, dramatically reducing I/O and CPU cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** drop join conditions while pushing selections down. Always ensure the join condition is preserved separately.
> 2. **Do NOT** apply π before σ if the projection removes attributes needed in the selection condition.
> 3. **Many students** confuse Rule 3 with Rule 9 — Rule 3 converts × into ⋈, Rule 9 pushes σ past an *existing* ⋈.
> 4. **Order matters in step-by-step answers** — Examiners check the sequence of rule applications.
> 5. **Cascading vs Cascade Confusion** — Cascading σ means applying multiple σ; Cascade rule is the specific transformation.
> 6. **Common Mistake:** Writing `σ(EMP × DEPT)` and then replacing it with `EMP ⋈ DEPT` without preserving the join condition — this loses the predicate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Heuristic optimization** is a rule-based, polynomial-time technique for query optimization.
- The **canonical query tree** has selections and projections at the top, joins (Cartesian products) at the bottom.
- The **optimized query tree** has selections pushed down, projections trimmed, and Cartesian products replaced by joins.
- **Twelve core equivalence rules** govern transformations across σ, π, ⋈, ×, ∪, −.
- **Push selections (σ) early** to reduce tuple count before expensive joins.
- **Push projections (π) carefully** — only after ensuring no attribute dependencies are broken.
- **Replace σ(R × S) with R ⋈_c S** to recognize and exploit joins.
- **Reorder joins** using commutativity and associativity so the most restrictive join executes first.
- **Heuristic ≠ Optimal** — heuristic plans are "very good" but not provably the best.
- **Cost-based optimization** complements heuristics by using statistics on data distribution.
- **Rule application order matters** — push down σ first, then recognize joins, then push down π.
- **Subexpression sharing** is an advanced heuristic that computes common subqueries once.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always write the initial canonical form, then show each transformation with the corresponding **rule number** and **justification**.
- **Watch out for:** Low-selectivity predicates, indexed access paths, and skewed data — these are where heuristics fall short.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Database Design and Tuning: Introduction to Physical Database Design, Overview of Database Tuning, Tuning the Conceptual Schema, Tuning Queries and Views
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST634 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Database Design and Tuning: Introduction to Physical Database Design, Overview of Database Tuning, Tuning the Conceptual Schema, Tuning Queries and Views

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Physical Database Design

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Physical Database Design is the process of translating the logical/ conceptual data model into a physical storage specification that describes the actual storage structures, access paths (indexes), file organizations, record placement, and disk allocation strategies used to store the database on secondary storage devices. It addresses the question: *"How will the data actually be stored?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition Snapshot:**
> Physical Database Design deals with the **storage allocation**, **indexing strategies**, **clustering**, **partitioning**, and **access path selection** that determine how efficiently the database responds to queries and transactions.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are designing a **massive library**:
- The **Conceptual Schema** is the catalogue system (books are categorized by subject, author, ISBN).
- The **Logical Schema** is the decision of *what shelves* to build and *how many floors*.
- The **Physical Schema** is the *exact physical layout* — which aisle, which shelf, which row, which slot, and whether to add a quick-lookup index card for popular books.

Just as a librarian places bestsellers at the front desk and adds a card-index for fast lookup, a physical designer places frequently accessed records together (**clustering**) and builds indexes (**access paths**) to speed up searches.

| Physical Design Activity | Real-World Library Analogy |
|---|---|
| Choice of File Organization | Type of shelf (open, closed, rotating) |
| Index Creation | Adding a card catalog or digital lookup |
| Clustering | Putting related books on the same shelf |
| Partitioning | Splitting books across multiple floors/branches |
| Denormalization | Keeping duplicate copies for quick reference |

## 1.2 Overview of Database Tuning

**Formal Definition:**
Database Tuning is the continuous, iterative activity of adjusting the database configuration, schema, indexes, queries, and system parameters to **minimize response time**, **maximize throughput**, and **optimize resource utilization** (CPU, I/O, memory) without changing the application's external behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Layers of Tuning in KTU 2024:**
> 1. **Conceptual Schema Tuning** – restructure logical tables.
> 2. **Physical Schema Tuning** – adjust indexes, clusters, partitions.
> 3. **Application/Query Tuning** – rewrite queries and views.

### 🎯 Intuition
Think of Database Tuning as **tuning a Formula-1 car during a race**:
- The **engine** = DBMS software.
- The **fuel mix** = indexes and query plans.
- The **tire pressure** = buffer sizes and disk allocation.
- The **driver's skill** = how well queries are written.

A small adjustment in any one place can dramatically change lap times (query response).

## 1.3 Tuning the Conceptual Schema

**Formal Definition:**
Conceptual Schema Tuning (or Logical Schema Tuning) is the process of adjusting the **logical structure** of tables — through selective denormalization, horizontal partitioning, vertical partitioning, attribute splitting, or adding derived attributes — to improve query performance while preserving the integrity of the original information model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tuning Principle:**
> *"Tune the schema, the queries, and the physical storage — in that order — for the greatest performance gain at the lowest cost."*

## 1.4 Tuning Queries and Views

**Formal Definition:**
Query and View Tuning involves modifying the **SQL formulation** of queries, restructuring subqueries, choosing efficient join orders, exploiting indexes, replacing views with materialized views, and avoiding inefficient constructs (such as correlated subqueries or `SELECT *`) to reduce I/O, CPU cost, and network traffic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's Favorite):**
> *"A 10% improvement in query formulation often yields a 90% performance gain — more than any hardware upgrade."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Database Design — Operational Steps

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate the following **structured workflow**:

1. **Analyze the Workload** – Study the query/update mix, frequency, and performance-critical transactions.
2. **Choose File Organization** – Heap, Hash, ISAM, B+Tree, or clustered.
3. **Select Access Paths (Indexes)** – Primary, secondary, clustering, multi-attribute, or bitmap.
4. **Decide on Clustering & Partitioning** – Group related tuples on the same block; split large tables.
5. **Estimate Storage Cost & I/O Cost** – Predict number of disk blocks accessed.
6. **Refine the Design** – Iterate based on cost trade-offs.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Workload Analysis** — Without knowing which queries dominate, you are tuning blind.
- **File Organization** — Determines how records are physically placed and retrieved.
- **Indexing** — Trade-off: faster read vs. slower write (every insert/update must rebuild index).
- **Clustering** — Reduces block I/O for range queries (e.g., `WHERE age BETWEEN 20 AND 30`).
- **Partitioning** — Enables parallel scans and prunes data quickly (partition elimination).

## 2.2 Database Tuning Pyramid (KTU High-Yield Concept)

```
                ┌─────────────────────┐
                │  TUNE THE QUERIES   │  ← Highest ROI, Lowest Cost
                ├─────────────────────┤
                │  TUNE THE VIEWS     │  ← Materialize when profitable
                ├─────────────────────┤
                │  TUNE THE PHYSICAL  │  ← Add/drop indexes
                │       SCHEMA        │
                ├─────────────────────┤
                │  TUNE THE CONCEPTUAL│  ← Denormalize selectively
                │       SCHEMA        │
                ├─────────────────────┤
                │  TUNE THE SYSTEM    │  ← Buffer, disk, CPU
                └─────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tuning Order (Top-Down = Best ROI):**
> Tune **queries first**, then **views**, then **physical schema**, then **conceptual schema**, and **system parameters last**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of Blocks in Heap File | $B = \lceil r / b_{r} \rceil$ | $r$ = records, $b_r$ = block factor (records/block) | blocks |
| 2 | Block Factor | $b_{r} = \lfloor B_{size} / R_{size} \rfloor$ | $B_{size}$ = block size, $R_{size}$ = record size | records/block |
| 3 | Linear Search Cost | $C_{LS} = B$ (average $B/2$) | Sequential scan over all blocks | block I/Os |
| 4 | B+ Tree Index Search | $C_{BT} = h + 1$ | $h$ = height of B+ Tree | block I/Os |
| 5 | Hash Index Search | $C_{H} = 1$ to $2$ | Equality lookup | block I/Os |
| 6 | Clustering Index Range | $C_{CR} = \lceil s / b_{r} \rceil$ | $s$ = number of matching records | block I/Os |
| 7 | Multi-level Index Height | $h = \lceil \log_{fo}(r_{i}) \rceil$ | $f_o$ = fan-out, $r_i$ = index entries | levels |
| 8 | Fan-out of B+ Tree | $f_{o} = \lfloor B_{size} / (K + P) \rfloor$ | $K$ = key size, $P$ = pointer size | pointers/node |
| 9 | Total Cost (Selection) | $C_{total} = C_{access} + C_{transfer}$ | I/O + processing cost | time units |
| 10 | Join Selectivity | $\sigma = \vert R \bowtie S \vert \,/\, (\vert R \vert \times \vert S \vert)$ | Fraction of tuples that join | dimensionless |
| 11 | Estimated Result Size | $\vert \sigma(R) \vert \approx \vert R \vert \times s$ | $s$ = selectivity of predicate | tuples |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipe `|` used in table cells** — replaced with `$\vert$` (LaTeX absolute value) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Conceptual Schema Tuning — Techniques

| Technique | Description | When to Apply |
|---|---|---|
| **Selective Denormalization** | Merge tables or duplicate columns to avoid joins | Frequently joined, rarely updated |
| **Horizontal Partitioning** | Split rows by predicate (e.g., year, region) | Very large tables with skewed access |
| **Vertical Partitioning** | Split columns into hot/cold groups | Tables with rarely accessed wide columns (BLOBs) |
| **Adding Derived Attributes** | Store computed values (e.g., total) | Heavy aggregate workload |
| **Splitting Attributes** | Break multivalued attributes | 1NF violation remediation |
| **Merging Relations** | Combine 1:1 related tables | Reduces I/O for joined reads |

## 2.5 Query and View Tuning — Techniques

| Technique | Benefit |
|---|---|
| Avoid `SELECT *` | Reduces column projection cost |
| Replace `NOT IN` with `NOT EXISTS` | Avoids NULL-related logic issues |
| Use sargable predicates | Allows index usage (`WHERE year = 2024` vs `WHERE YEAR(d) = 2024`) |
| Push down predicates | Filters earlier in execution plan |
| Materialize expensive views | Trades storage for query speed |
| Avoid correlated subqueries | Convert to joins for set-based processing |
| Use `EXISTS` over `COUNT(*)` | Stops at first match |
| Index foreign keys | Speeds up joins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Physical design and tuning decisions impact **OLTP transaction latency** (milliseconds matter in banking), **OLAP query throughput** (data warehouse scans), and **embedded/IoT database footprint** (memory-constrained edge devices).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation: Cost of a Selection Query

**Problem Setup (KTU Typical Exam Style):**
Consider a relation `EMPLOYEE(ENO, ENAME, DEPT, SALARY)` with the following statistics:
- Number of tuples: $r = 10{,}000$
- Block size: $B_{size} = 4096$ bytes
- Record size: $R_{size} = 200$ bytes
- Pointer size: $P = 8$ bytes
- Key size (ENO): $K = 8$ bytes
- Assume a **B+ Tree primary index** on ENO with fan-out $f_o = 100$.

**Query:** `SELECT * FROM EMPLOYEE WHERE ENO = 305;`

### Step 1 — Compute Block Factor
First, calculate how many records fit in one block.

$$
b_{r} = \left\lfloor \frac{B_{size}}{R_{size}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{200} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 20.48 \right\rfloor = 20
$$

**Logic:** Each block of 4096 bytes can hold 20 complete 200-byte records (with the remaining 96 bytes wasted due to fragmentation).

### Step 2 — Total Number of Blocks
Next, compute how many blocks are needed to store the entire relation.

$$
B = \left\lceil \frac{r}{b_{r}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{10{,}000}{20} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 500 \right\rceil = 500
$$

**Logic:** 500 blocks are required to physically store the 10,000 employee records.

### Step 3 — Compute B+ Tree Height
The B+ Tree has leaf nodes and internal nodes. Compute the height using the fan-out.

$$
h = \left\lceil \log_{f_o}(r) \right\rceil = \left\lceil \log_{100}(10{,}000) \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.0 \right\rceil = 2
$$

**Logic:** With a fan-out of 100, we need only 2 levels to reach any one of the 10,000 keys. (Root → Leaf).

### Step 4 — Cost of Equality Search on B+ Tree
The B+ Tree search cost includes traversing the index levels and reading the data block.

$$
C_{BT} = h + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ block I/Os}
$$

**Logic:** We read 2 index blocks (root + intermediate leaf entry) plus 1 data block to fetch the record.

### Step 5 — Compare with Linear Search
For contrast, a linear scan over the heap would require:

$$
C_{LS} = B = 500 \text{ block I/Os (worst case)}
$$

**Logic:** Without an index, every block must be examined.

### Step 6 — Performance Gain

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{C_{LS}}{C_{BT}} = \frac{500}{3} \approx 166.67 \times
$$

**Conclusion:** Indexing provides a **~167× speedup** for point queries — a clear empirical justification for physical design tuning.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Access Method Comparison

Given the same EMPLOYEE relation ($r = 10{,}000$, $B = 500$, $f_o = 100$):

| Access Method | Query Type | Cost Formula | Computed Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Search | Any (no index) | $C_{LS} = B$ | $500$ I/Os | Small tables, low-frequency access |
| B+ Tree (Primary) | Equality on key | $C_{BT} = h + 1$ | $3$ I/Os | OLTP point lookups |
| B+ Tree (Clustering) | Range on key | $C_{CR} = h + \lceil s/b_r \rceil$ | $2 + 50 = 52$ I/Os* | Range scans on sorted attribute |
| Hash Index | Equality on key | $C_H \approx 1$ to $2$ | $1.5$ I/Os avg | Pure equality, no range support |
| Bitmap Index | Low-cardinality | $C_{BM} = \lceil B/8 \rceil$ | $63$ I/Os | Data warehouse `WHERE GENDER = 'F'` |

*\*Assuming 1000 matching records ($s = 1000$), $b_r = 20$.*

## 3.3 Derivation: Selectivity and Result Size Estimation

**Scenario:** `SELECT * FROM EMPLOYEE WHERE DEPT = 'CS';`

Assume DEPT has 4 distinct values uniformly distributed.

### Step 1 — Compute Selectivity

$$
s = \frac{1}{d(D)} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

**Logic:** Each distinct DEPT value matches 1/4 of the records under uniform distribution.

### Step 2 — Estimate Result Cardinality

$$
\vert \sigma(R) \vert = r \times s = 10{,}000 \times 0.25 = 2{,}500 \text{ tuples}
$$

**Logic:** 25% of 10,000 employees work in CS.

### Step 3 — Estimate I/O Cost for Scan

$$
C_{scan} = B = 500 \text{ I/Os}
$$

**Logic:** Without a DEPT index, every block must be read. A bitmap index on DEPT would be ideal here.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (KTU Board Pattern):**
> If the result set is small ($\le 5\%$ of relation), use an **index**. If large, a **full scan** is often cheaper because index random I/O dominates.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: View Materialization Cost-Benefit

**Decision:** Should we materialize view `V = SELECT DEPT, AVG(SALARY) FROM EMPLOYEE GROUP BY DEPT;`?

### Step 1 — Cost of Computing V On-Demand
Let $T_c$ = cost per query of computing the aggregate without materialization.

$$
C_{on\text{-}demand} = Q_v \times T_c
$$

Where $Q_v$ = frequency of queries against $V$.

### Step 2 — Cost of Maintaining Materialized V
Let $T_u$ = cost to update $V$ on each base-table update.

$$
C_{maintain} = U_b \times T_u
$$

Where $U_b$ = frequency of updates to base table.

### Step 3 — Materialization Decision Rule

$$
\text{Materialize} \iff (Q_v \times T_c) > (U_b \times T_u) + S_v
$$

Where $S_v$ = storage cost of the materialized view.

**Logic:** Materialize only if query savings outweigh update + storage costs — a classic KTU 14-mark derivation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Physical Database Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Logical Schema] --> B[Workload Analysis]
    B --> C[Identify Hot Queries and Updates]
    C --> D[Choose File Organization]
    D --> D1[Heap]
    D --> D2[Hash]
    D --> D3[B Plus Tree]
    D1 --> E[Decide on Indexing Strategy]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Primary Index]
    E --> E2[Secondary Index]
    E --> E3[Clustering Index]
    E --> E4[Bitmap Index]
    E1 --> F[Decide on Clustering and Partitioning]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> F1[Horizontal Partition]
    F --> F2[Vertical Partition]
    F1 --> G[Estimate I/O Cost]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H{Cost Acceptable}
    H -- No --> I[Refine Design: Add or Drop Indexes]
    I --> F
    H -- Yes --> J[Final Physical Schema]
    J --> K[Implement and Monitor]
    K --> L[Tuning Phase Begins]
```

## 4.2 Database Tuning Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Layer 1: Tune Queries and Views] --> P2[Layer 2: Tune Physical Schema]
    P2 --> P3[Layer 3: Tune Conceptual Schema]
    P3 --> P4[Layer 4: Tune System Parameters]
    P1 --> N1[Highest ROI Lowest Cost]
    P4 --> N2[Lowest ROI Highest Cost]
    N1 --> R[Start Here]
    N2 --> R
    style P1 fill:#FFD700
    style P2 fill:#FFA500
    style P3 fill:#FF6347
    style P4 fill:#DC143C
    style R fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schema Tuning Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Slow Joins] --> B{Frequently Joined?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Consider Denormalization]
    B -- No --> D[Leave Normalized]
    C --> E{Rarely Updated?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Merge Tables or Duplicate Columns]
    E -- No --> G[Keep Separate Use Cache]
    A --> H[Identify Large Tables]
    H --> I{Skewed Access Pattern?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Horizontal Partition by Predicate]
    I -- No --> K[Vertical Partition by Hot Cold Columns]
    J --> L[Validate Performance]
    K --> L
    F --> L
    G --> L
    D --> L
    L --> M{Improved?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Commit Schema Change]
    M -- No --> O[Revert and Try Another Strategy]
```

## 4.4 Query Tuning Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Developer
    participant O as Optimizer
    participant DB as Database Engine
    D->>O: Submit SQL Query
    O->>O: Parse and Rewrite
    O->>O: Cost Based Optimization
    O->>DB: Generate Execution Plan
    DB->>DB: Execute and Measure
    DB-->>D: Return Result with Statistics
    D->>D: Analyze Slow Plan
    D->>O: Rewrite Query Add Hint or Index
    O->>DB: New Execution Plan
    DB-->>D: Faster Result
    Note over D,DB: Iterate until Performance SLA Met
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Physical Database Design. List any four physical design decisions that affect database performance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Physical Database Design is the process of translating the logical schema into a storage-level specification, defining file organizations, indexing structures, record placement, and access paths to optimize storage and retrieval.

**Four Physical Design Decisions (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Choice of File Organization** — Heap, Hash, Indexed Sequential, or B+ Tree.
2. **Index Selection** — Primary, secondary, clustering, multi-level, or bitmap.
3. **Clustering Strategy** — Grouping related records on the same disk block.
4. **Partitioning Strategy** — Horizontal or vertical splitting of relations.

> [!NOTE]
> A fifth valid option is **record placement and compression** (denormalization) — students mentioning this get bonus credit.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Conceptual Schema Tuning and Physical Schema Tuning with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Conceptual Schema Tuning | Physical Schema Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **Level** | Logical (table structure) | Storage (indexes, blocks) |
| **Operations** | Denormalize, split, merge, partition tables | Add/drop indexes, reorganize clusters |
| **Example** | Merging `STUDENT` and `ENROLLMENT` into one table for faster reads | Adding a B+ Tree index on `ROLL_NO` for fast lookups |
| **Impact Scope** | Affects data model integrity constraints | Affects I/O cost and access paths |
| **Reversibility** | Hard to reverse (data migration) | Easy to reverse (drop index) |

(1 Mark for definition of each, 1 Mark for example comparison)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A — 14 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, Choice A]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **steps involved in Physical Database Design**. Discuss the role of **workload analysis** in determining the optimal physical design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **Database Tuning**. Explain the **four layers of database tuning** with an example for each layer. Justify why **tuning queries first** gives the highest return on investment (ROI). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (a)

**Step 1 – Workload Analysis (2 Marks):**
Workload analysis involves identifying the most frequent and performance-critical queries, update patterns, transaction types, and peak usage periods. It is the **foundation** of physical design — without it, all subsequent decisions are guesswork. Tools like query logs, profiling, and `EXPLAIN PLAN` are used.

**Step 2 – File Organization Selection (1 Mark):**
Choose between **Heap** (best for bulk inserts), **Hash** (best for equality), **B+ Tree** (best for range and ordered access), or **ISAM** (legacy). The choice depends on the dominant access pattern from workload analysis.

**Step 3 – Index Selection (2 Marks):**
Decide which attributes need indexes. **Primary indexes** are built on the primary key automatically. **Secondary indexes** are added on frequently searched non-key attributes. Avoid over-indexing because indexes slow down inserts/updates.

**Step 4 – Clustering and Partitioning (1 Mark):**
Cluster related records together (e.g., all orders for a customer on adjacent blocks) to speed up joins and range queries. Partition very large tables to enable parallel scans.

**Step 5 – Cost Estimation and Refinement (1 Mark):**
Estimate the number of block I/Os for each critical query. If cost exceeds threshold, refine the design (add index, change cluster key, repartition).

**Block Diagram (see Section 4.1):** The five-step cycle from workload analysis → file organization → indexing → clustering → cost estimation → refinement is mandatory for full marks.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (b)

**Definition of Database Tuning (1 Mark):**
Database Tuning is the **iterative process** of adjusting database parameters, schema, queries, and physical structures to achieve optimal performance (minimum response time, maximum throughput).

**Four Layers of Tuning (4 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **Tune the Conceptual Schema** — Example: Denormalize a normalized `ORDERS` and `CUSTOMER` schema into a single `CUST_ORDER` table to avoid an expensive join in a frequently run report.

2. **Tune the Physical Schema** — Example: Add a B+ Tree index on `ORDERS.ORDER_DATE` to speed up date-range queries that previously used a full table scan.

3. **Tune Queries and Views** — Example: Rewrite `SELECT * FROM ORDER WHERE YEAR(ODATE) = 2024` to `SELECT * FROM ORDER WHERE ODATE BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'` so that the index on `ODATE` is utilized (sargable predicate).

4. **Tune System Parameters** — Example: Increase the `DB_CACHE_SIZE` (Oracle) or `innodb_buffer_pool_size` (MySQL) to allow more pages in memory, reducing physical I/O.

**Why Tune Queries First (2 Marks):**
- A single poorly written query can consume 80% of system resources.
- Query tuning is **non-invasive** — no schema change, no restart, no migration.
- Cost is essentially **zero** (developer time only) compared to hardware upgrades.
- Empirically, query tuning yields **10×–100×** improvement, while hardware gives only **1.5×–2×** per dollar spent.

---

### 📌 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Choice B]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **denormalization** as a conceptual schema tuning technique. Discuss **four denormalization strategies** with appropriate scenarios. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **query tuning**? Describe **five techniques** to improve query performance. With an example, show how a **non-sargable predicate** prevents index usage. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (a)

**Denormalization Definition (1 Mark):**
Denormalization is the process of **intentionally introducing redundancy** into a normalized schema — by merging tables, duplicating columns, or adding derived attributes — to **reduce expensive join operations** and improve read performance at the cost of additional storage and update overhead.

**Four Denormalization Strategies (4 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **Merging 1:1 Relations** — Combine `STUDENT` and `STUDENT_PROFILE` into one table when they are always accessed together.
2. **Duplicating Non-Key Columns** — Add `DEPT_NAME` to `EMPLOYEE` table to avoid joining with `DEPARTMENT` for every employee report.
3. **Storing Derived Attributes** — Pre-compute `TOTAL_SALARY = BASIC + HRA + DA` in the `PAYROLL` table to avoid aggregating on every report.
4. **Pre-joining M:N Relations** — Materialize a `STUDENT_COURSE` link table with extra snapshot data (e.g., `ENROLLMENT_DATE`) for fast lookups.

**Scenarios & Trade-offs (2 Marks):**
- Apply denormalization only when: (i) the join is on a **hot path** (high frequency), (ii) the data is **read-mostly** (low update rate), and (iii) the storage cost is **acceptable**.
- Avoid denormalization on frequently updated columns to prevent **update anomalies**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (b)

**Query Tuning Definition (1 Mark):**
Query tuning is the process of **rewriting or restructuring SQL queries** to minimize execution time, reduce I/O, and leverage available indexes effectively.

**Five Query Tuning Techniques (3.5 Marks — 0.7 each):**

1. **Use Sargable Predicates** — Avoid functions on indexed columns in `WHERE` clause.
2. **Avoid `SELECT *`** — Project only required columns.
3. **Replace `NOT IN` with `NOT EXISTS`** or with `LEFT JOIN ... WHERE ... IS NULL`.
4. **Use `UNION ALL` instead of `UNION`** when duplicates are not a concern (skips sort).
5. **Avoid Correlated Subqueries** — Convert to `JOIN` for set-based execution.

**Worked Example — Non-Sargable Predicate (2.5 Marks):**

Consider table `ORDERS(ORDER_ID, ORDER_DATE, TOTAL)` with a B+ Tree index on `ORDER_DATE`.

❌ **Non-Sargable Query:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM ORDERS WHERE YEAR(ORDER_DATE) = 2024;
```

- **Problem:** The function `YEAR()` is applied to the indexed column, so the optimizer **cannot use the index**. A full table scan is forced.
- **Cost:** Suppose $B = 1000$ blocks → $1000$ I/Os.

✅ **Sargable Rewrite:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM ORDERS 
WHERE ORDER_DATE >= '2024-01-01' 
  AND ORDER_DATE <  '2025-01-01';
```

- **Benefit:** The predicate is now a **direct comparison** on `ORDER_DATE`, allowing the B+ Tree to perform a range scan.
- **Cost:** Approximately $h + \lceil s/b_r \rceil \approx 3 + 5 = 8$ I/Os — a **125× speedup**.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** say "add an index" as the first step for query tuning. **Always rewrite the query first.**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *sargable* (Search ARGument ABLE) with *non-sargable*. The key is whether the predicate allows the optimizer to seek into the index.
> - **Do NOT** denormalize without first trying query rewrite + index addition — denormalization is a **last resort** in conceptual schema tuning.
> - In 14-mark answers, students often **skip the numerical example**. Always include at least one I/O cost computation to earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 1):**

- ✅ **Physical Design** = "How to store" — file organization, indexes, clustering, partitioning.
- ✅ **Database Tuning** is **iterative**, **continuous**, and **multi-layered** (4 layers).
- ✅ **Tuning Order = Top-Down**: Queries → Views → Physical → Conceptual → System.
- ✅ **Workload Analysis** is the **first and most critical** step in physical design.
- ✅ **B+ Tree Cost** = $h + 1$ for equality; $h + \lceil s/b_r \rceil$ for range.
- ✅ **Hash Index Cost** = 1 to 2 I/Os (equality only — no range support).
- ✅ **Sargable Predicate** = predicate on a **raw column** (no function wrapping) → index usable.
- ✅ **Denormalization** = intentional redundancy → faster reads, slower updates, more storage.
- ✅ **Horizontal Partition** = split rows; **Vertical Partition** = split columns.
- ✅ **Materialize a view** when $Q_v \times T_c > U_b \times T_u + S_v$.
- ✅ **Avoid `SELECT *`** — project only required columns.
- ✅ **Block Factor** $b_r = \lfloor B_{size} / R_{size} \rfloor$ — know this cold.
- ✅ **Index Trade-off**: Every index speeds up reads but **slows down writes** (insert/update must maintain the index).
- ✅ **Bitmap Index** is best for **low-cardinality** columns in data warehouses.
- ✅ **Clustering Index** physically sorts data — only **one per table**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook:**
> *"Schema tunes the **structure**, queries tune the **sentence**, indexes tune the **speed**, and the system tunes the **substance**."* — Layer this on your exam page for a quick recall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Concurrency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST634 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Impact of Concurrency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Concurrency** in a Database Management System (DBMS) refers to the ability of the database system to allow multiple transactions to access and manipulate the same database simultaneously (at the same time) while preserving data integrity and consistency.

In the context of the **transaction model**, concurrency is the interleaved execution of multiple transactions by an operating system, where each transaction's operations may execute in parallel at the hardware level or be interleaved at the logical level by the DBMS scheduler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Concurrency control is one of the four classical properties of transaction management alongside the **ACID properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability). The DBMS ensures these properties even when transactions execute concurrently.

### The Three Realms of Concurrency

| Realm | Description | KTU Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Logical Concurrency** | Multiple users issuing queries simultaneously | Permitted by default |
| **Physical Concurrency** | Parallel CPU/disk access to data blocks | Handled at OS & DBMS layer |
| **Transaction Concurrency** | Multiple transactions operating on shared data | Requires **concurrency control** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bank Counter System**
> 
> Imagine a small bank branch with **3 service counters** but **20 customers** waiting. The bank manager wants to serve all customers quickly. Two approaches are possible:
> 
> 1. **Sequential Processing (No Concurrency):** Counter-1 serves Customer-A completely (withdraws cash, updates passbook, leaves), then Counter-2 serves Customer-B, and so on. Only **one customer at a time** can be processed. The branch becomes extremely slow.
> 
> 2. **Concurrent Processing (With Concurrency):** All 3 counters work simultaneously, each handling a different customer. **Customer-A withdraws ₹5000** while **Customer-B deposits ₹3000** while **Customer-C inquires about balance**.
> 
> **The Problem:** What if Customer-A and Customer-D **both want to withdraw** from the same joint account which has only ₹8000 balance, and they submit requests to two counters at the same time? Without a controlling mechanism, both might read the balance as ₹8000, both might withdraw ₹5000, and the bank would end up paying ₹10,000 from an account with only ₹8000!
> 
> **The Solution:** A **concurrency controller** (acting like a strict bank manager) ensures that the operations are scheduled in a way that prevents this incorrect outcome. This controller enforces rules such as "only one withdrawal transaction can update the balance field at any given moment."

This analogy directly maps to the **DBMS scenario**: customers = transactions, money = data items, counters = CPUs/processors, and the bank manager = the **DBMS Concurrency Control Subsystem**.

## 1.3 The Fundamental Promise of Concurrency

The DBMS guarantees that the concurrent execution of a set of transactions $\mathbf{T} = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$ produces a result **equivalent** to some serial execution of those transactions. This guarantee is called **Serializability** and is the cornerstone of concurrency theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric — Transaction Throughput:** The standard metric for measuring concurrency benefit is **Throughput** = number of transactions committed per unit time (TPS — Transactions Per Second). The standard baseline comparison metric is **speedup** $\approx \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{concurrent}}}$.

## 1.4 Why Concurrency Is Essential in Modern Systems

Concurrency is not a luxury; it is a fundamental necessity driven by:
1. **Reduced waiting time** for user requests.
2. **Higher CPU and disk utilization** (avoiding idle resources).
3. **Reduced average response time**.
4. **Increased transaction throughput** (system metric, **TPS**).
5. **Better resource sharing** across multiple users.

However, these benefits come at the cost of **potential data inconsistencies** if the DBMS does not enforce proper isolation. The remainder of this study note explores these consequences in detail.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing the Impact of Concurrency

The impact of concurrency is best understood by examining two opposing sides — the **benefits** (positive impact) and the **problems** (negative impact).

### 2.1.1 Positive Impact of Concurrency (Benefits)

When transactions run concurrently (and are correctly scheduled), the following advantages emerge:

1. **Increased Processor Utilization:** While one transaction waits for I/O (disk read/write), another can use the CPU.
2. **Decreased Average Response Time:** Users get faster results as their requests are not queued strictly serially.
3. **Less Waiting Time:** Multiple users can interact with the system without indefinite blocking.
4. **Higher Throughput (TPS):** More transactions complete per unit time.
5. **Better Fairness:** Round-robin or priority scheduling ensures equitable CPU access.

### 2.1.2 Negative Impact of Concurrency (Problems)

When transactions run concurrently **without proper control**, several anomalies can occur. KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **three classic concurrency problems** plus **one scheduling anomaly**:

#### **Problem 1: The Lost Update Problem**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lost Update** occurs when two transactions read the same data item and update it based on the value read, but the second update **overwrites** the first update, causing the first update to be "lost."

**Operational steps:**
- Transaction $T_1$ reads data item $X$ (value = 100).
- Transaction $T_2$ reads data item $X$ (value = 100).
- $T_1$ computes new value = $X + 50 = 150$ and writes $X = 150$.
- $T_2$ computes new value = $X - 30 = 70$ and writes $X = 70$.
- The update by $T_1$ (+50) is **silently lost**; the final value is 70 instead of the correct 120.

#### **Problem 2: The Dirty Read (Uncommitted Dependency) Problem**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dirty Read** occurs when a transaction reads a data value that has been written by another **uncommitted** transaction. If the other transaction later rolls back, the read value becomes invalid.

**Operational steps:**
- $T_1$ updates $X$ from 100 to 200 and **does NOT commit**.
- $T_2$ reads $X = 200$ (the uncommitted value).
- $T_1$ rolls back (or aborts); $X$ reverts to 100.
- $T_2$ has now used a **"dirty" or non-existent value** in its subsequent computations.

#### **Problem 3: The Non-Repeatable Read (Inconsistent Retrieval) Problem**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Repeatable Read** occurs when a transaction reads the same data item **twice** and obtains **different values**, because another transaction modified (or deleted) the item between the two reads.

**Operational steps:**
- $T_1$ reads $X = 100$.
- $T_2$ updates $X = 150$ and commits.
- $T_1$ reads $X$ again — obtains $X = 150$.
- $T_1$'s two reads give different results in the **same** transaction → inconsistency.

#### **Problem 4: The Phantom Read Problem**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phantom Read** occurs when a transaction re-executes a query returning a set of rows that satisfy a search condition and finds that **new rows (phantoms)** have been inserted by another committed transaction, or some rows have been deleted.

**Operational steps:**
- $T_1$ executes `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Accounts WHERE balance > 5000;` → returns 10.
- $T_2$ inserts a new account with balance 7000 and commits.
- $T_1$ re-executes the same `SELECT` → returns 11.
- The extra row is the **phantom**.

## 2.2 The Four Standard Transaction Isolation Levels (SQL Standard)

To control the impact of concurrency, the SQL standard defines **four isolation levels**, each providing a different trade-off between consistency and performance:

| Isolation Level | Dirty Read | Non-Repeatable Read | Phantom Read | Performance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **READ UNCOMMITTED** | Possible | Possible | Possible | Highest |
| **READ COMMITTED** | Prevented | Possible | Possible | High |
| **REPEATABLE READ** | Prevented | Prevented | Possible | Medium |
| **SERIALIZABLE** | Prevented | Prevented | Prevented | Lowest |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Fact:** PostgreSQL's default is **READ COMMITTED**; MySQL InnoDB's default is **REPEATABLE READ**; Oracle's default is **READ COMMITTED**. The strictest level (**SERIALIZABLE**) emulates a serial execution and provides complete isolation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for KTU ESE preparation. These formulas/definitions are tested in both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Throughput (TPS)** | $TPS = \frac{N_{\text{committed}}}{t}$ transactions per second | Quantitative Part A question |
| 2 | **Speedup** | $S = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{concurrent}}} \geq 1$ | Concurrency benefit proof |
| 3 | **Schedule Length** | $SL = \max_{T_i} \sum_{op \in T_i} \text{duration}(op)$ | Scheduler analysis |
| 4 | **Average Response Time** | $ART = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\text{finish}_i - \text{start}_i)$ | Performance comparison |
| 5 | **CPU Utilization** | $U_{CPU} = 1 - P_{\text{idle}}$ | Concurrency efficiency metric |
| 6 | **Lost Update** | $W_1(A); R_2(A); W_2(A); \ldots$ | 14-mark Part B |
| 7 | **Dirty Read** | $W_1(A); R_2(A); \text{(abort}_1)$ | 14-mark Part B |
| 8 | **Non-Repeatable Read** | $R_1(A); W_2(A); \text{commit}_2; R_1(A)$ | 14-mark Part B |
| 9 | **Phantom Read** | $R_1(P); W_2(\text{insert to } P); \text{commit}_2; R_1(P)$ | 14-mark Part B |
| 10 | **Serializability Condition** | A schedule $S$ is serializable if $\equiv$ to some serial schedule $S'$ | Schedule classification |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Lost Update Problem

This is a classic **KTU 14-mark question pattern**. We will solve it with **exhaustive step-by-step notation**, showing every algebraic transition.

> **Problem Statement:**
> A bank account $A$ has balance $= \mathbf{1000}$. Two concurrent transactions execute as follows:
> - **Transaction $T_1$**: Deposit ₹500 → new balance should be 1500.
> - **Transaction $T_2$**: Withdraw ₹300 → new balance should be 700.
> 
> Assuming both transactions begin by reading the current balance, derive the sequence of operations that causes a **lost update**, and compute the final incorrect balance.

### **Step 1 — Initial State (Time $t_0$)**

$$
\text{Balance}(A) = 1000
$$

Both transactions read this initial value.

### **Step 2 — Transaction $T_1$ Reads the Balance**

$$
T_1: \quad \text{READ}(A) \longrightarrow \text{Local}_1 = 1000
$$

The variable $\text{Local}_1$ is the **local copy** of the balance inside transaction $T_1$.

### **Step 3 — Transaction $T_2$ Reads the Balance**

$$
T_2: \quad \text{READ}(A) \longrightarrow \text{Local}_2 = 1000
$$

The variable $\text{Local}_2$ is the **local copy** inside transaction $T_2$.

> **Critical observation:** Both $T_1$ and $T_2$ have an **identical local snapshot** of $A$, even though the real-world state of $A$ is about to be modified.

### **Step 4 — Transaction $T_1$ Computes Its New Value**

$$
T_1: \quad \text{New}_1 = \text{Local}_1 + 500 = 1000 + 500 = 1500
$$

This is the **intended** new balance after the deposit.

### **Step 5 — Transaction $T_2$ Computes Its New Value**

$$
T_2: \quad \text{New}_2 = \text{Local}_2 - 300 = 1000 - 300 = 700
$$

This is the **intended** new balance after the withdrawal.

### **Step 6 — Transaction $T_1$ Writes Its Value to the Database**

$$
T_1: \quad \text{WRITE}(A) \longrightarrow \text{Balance}(A) = 1500
$$

At this moment, the database reflects $A = 1500$.

### **Step 7 — Transaction $T_2$ Writes Its Value to the Database (OVERWRITING $T_1$)**

$$
T_2: \quad \text{WRITE}(A) \longrightarrow \text{Balance}(A) = 700
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Lost Update:** The value $1500$ (written by $T_1$ in Step 6) has been **clobbered** by $T_2$'s write of $700$. The $+500$ deposit is now **completely lost** from the database.

### **Step 8 — Compare with the Correct Serial Execution**

If the transactions had run serially in the order $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$:

$$
\text{Balance}_{T_1 \rightarrow T_2} = (1000 + 500) - 300 = 1200
$$

If the transactions had run serially in the order $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$:

$$
\text{Balance}_{T_2 \rightarrow T_1} = (1000 - 300) + 500 = 1200
$$

In **both** serial orderings, the final balance is the correct $\mathbf{1200}$. The concurrent execution produced the **wrong** value of $700$ — a **discrepancy of ₹500** (the lost deposit).

### **Step 9 — Compute the Error Magnitude**

$$
\text{Error} = \vert \text{Balance}_{\text{actual}} - \text{Balance}_{\text{expected}} \vert
$$

$$
\text{Error} = \vert 700 - 1200 \vert = 500
$$

This 500-unit error represents the lost deposit.

### **Step 10 — Conclusion of the Derivation**

The lost update problem causes the database to enter an **inconsistent state**. The formal schedule that causes this is:

$$
S_{\text{bad}} = R_1(A), R_2(A), W_1(A), W_2(A)
$$

Such a non-serializable schedule must be **prevented** by the DBMS using a concurrency control protocol such as **2-Phase Locking (2PL)** or **Timestamp Ordering (TSO)**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of All Four Problems

The following matrix provides a unified analytical framework for all four classic concurrency problems. KTU 2024 examiners frequently test students on identifying **which problem** a given schedule exhibits.

| Property | Lost Update | Dirty Read | Non-Repeatable Read | Phantom Read |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operations involved** | $R, W$ on single item | $W, R$ across items | $R, W, R$ on same item | $R, W_{\text{insert}}, R$ on set |
| **Number of data items** | 1 | $\geq 1$ | 1 | $\geq 1$ (set/relation) |
| **Affected transaction count** | 2 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| **Aborts involved?** | No | Yes (writer aborts) | No | No |
| **Commit order dependency?** | Write-Write conflict | Write-Read conflict | Read-Write conflict | Predicate Read-Write conflict |
| **Standard conflict type** | W-W | W-R | R-W | Predicate R-W |
| **Minimum schedule length** | 4 operations | 3 operations | 4 operations | 4 operations |
| **Prevalence in KTU exams** | Very High | Very High | High | Medium |
| **Example domain** | Account balance | Booking systems | Report generation | Analytics queries |

### **3.2.1 Canonical Schedule for Each Problem**

**Lost Update (W-W Conflict):**
$$
S_{\text{LU}} = R_1(X), R_2(X), W_1(X), W_2(X)
$$

**Dirty Read (W-R Conflict with abort):**
$$
S_{\text{DR}} = W_1(X), R_2(X), \text{ABORT}_1
$$

**Non-Repeatable Read (R-W Conflict):**
$$
S_{\text{NRR}} = R_1(X), W_2(X), \text{COMMIT}_2, R_1(X)
$$

**Phantom Read (Predicate conflict):**
$$
S_{\text{PR}} = R_1(\sigma_{\text{cond}}), \text{INSERT}_2(\text{matching row}), \text{COMMIT}_2, R_1(\sigma_{\text{cond}})
$$

### **3.2.2 Formal Conflict-Serializability Check Using Precedence Graph**

For each schedule, we construct a **Precedence Graph** $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$ (transactions as vertices)
- $E$ contains edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ if $T_i$ must precede $T_j$ in any equivalent serial schedule.

**Theorem (KTU 2024 High-Yield):**
A schedule $S$ is **conflict-serializable** $\iff$ its precedence graph is **acyclic**.

Let us verify this for the Lost Update schedule:
- $R_1(X)$ before $W_2(X)$ on same item $X$ → edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$.
- $R_2(X)$ before $W_1(X)$ on same item $X$ → edge $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$.
- The precedence graph contains a **cycle** $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$.
- Therefore, the schedule is **NOT conflict-serializable** → it must be rejected by the DBMS.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Quantitative Speedup Calculation

> **Problem:** A serial DBMS processes transactions at an average rate of **50 TPS** (transactions per second). With concurrency control, the same hardware processes **180 TPS**. Compute the **speedup factor** and the **percentage improvement** in throughput.

### **Step 1 — Identify the Two Throughput Values**

$$
TPS_{\text{serial}} = 50
$$

$$
TPS_{\text{concurrent}} = 180
$$

### **Step 2 — Apply the Speedup Formula**

$$
S = \frac{TPS_{\text{concurrent}}}{TPS_{\text{serial}}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{180}{50} = 3.6
$$

### **Step 3 — Apply the Percentage Improvement Formula**

$$
\%\text{Improvement} = \left( \frac{TPS_{\text{concurrent}} - TPS_{\text{serial}}}{TPS_{\text{serial}}} \right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
\%\text{Improvement} = \left( \frac{180 - 50}{50} \right) \times 100\% = \left( \frac{130}{50} \right) \times 100\% = 260\%
$$

### **Step 4 — Interpretation**

The concurrent DBMS is **3.6$\times$ faster** than the serial one, representing a **260% improvement** in throughput. This dramatic improvement is the **primary positive impact** of concurrency — but it must be balanced against the cost of preventing anomalies.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always show the units in your final answer. Write "180 TPS" not just "180", and "3.6$\times$ speedup" with the dimensionless ratio.

        L3 --> L4["T2 writes X minus delta"]
        L4 --> L5["Final result: T1 update LOST"]
    end
