# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define concurrency in the context of a DBMS. List any three advantages of allowing concurrent transactions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Concurrency in a DBMS is the ability to allow multiple transactions to access and modify the database **simultaneously** in an interleaved manner while ensuring data consistency through serializability.

**Three Advantages (2 marks — 0.67 each):**
1. **Reduced Average Response Time:** Users receive results faster because their transactions are not blocked by preceding ones.
2. **Improved Throughput (TPS):** More transactions complete per unit time, increasing system capacity.
3. **Better Resource Utilization:** The CPU and disk remain busy with useful work instead of idling while waiting for I/O from a single transaction.

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between lost update and dirty read problems with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Lost Update | Dirty Read |
|---|---|---|
| **Conflict type** | Write-Write (W-W) | Write-Read (W-R) |
| **Trigger event** | Two transactions write to same item | One transaction reads uncommitted data |
| **Outcome** | First update is overwritten | Read value becomes invalid upon rollback |
| **Example step** | $R_1(X), R_2(X), W_1(X), W_2(X)$ | $W_1(X), R_2(X), \text{ABORT}_1$ |

(Each difference 0.75 marks; total 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question B-A.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) Explain the Lost Update Problem and the Dirty Read Problem with suitable transaction schedules. Show how these problems lead to database inconsistency. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the four standard SQL isolation levels. For each level, state which concurrency problems are prevented. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-A (a) — 7 marks**

> **Lost Update Problem (3.5 marks):**

The lost update problem occurs when **two concurrent transactions** read the same data item and update it, causing the **first update to be lost** because the second transaction's write overwrites it.

**Schedule $S_1$:**
$$
R_1(X), \quad R_2(X), \quad W_1(X), \quad W_2(X)
$$

**Detailed steps:**
- Initially, $X = 100$.
- $T_1$ reads $X = 100$. (Step 1)
- $T_2$ reads $X = 100$. (Step 2)
- $T_1$ updates $X = 100 + 50 = 150$. (Step 3)
- $T_2$ updates $X = 100 - 30 = 70$. (Step 4 — **OVERWRITES $T_1$'s value**)

**Correct serial execution** ($T_1$ then $T_2$) would yield $X = 70$ (if $T_2$ last) or $X = 150$ (if $T_1$ last), but the concurrent execution gave **only $T_2$'s outcome**, silently discarding the +50 update.

**Precedence Graph:** Contains cycle $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ → not conflict-serializable.

> **Dirty Read Problem (3.5 marks):**

The dirty read (uncommitted dependency) problem occurs when a transaction **reads a value written by an uncommitted transaction**. If the writer later aborts, the reader has used a value that **never officially existed**.

**Schedule $S_2$:**
$$
W_1(X), \quad R_2(X), \quad \text{ABORT}_1
$$

**Detailed steps:**
- Initially, $X = 100$.
- $T_1$ updates $X = 200$ but does **not commit**. (Step 1)
- $T_2$ reads $X = 200$ (the uncommitted value). (Step 2)
- $T_1$ aborts; $X$ reverts to 100. (Step 3)
- $T_2$ has computed subsequent operations using the **phantom value 200** → incorrect results propagated.

**Why this leads to inconsistency:** The database has officially committed $X = 100$, but $T_2$ has propagated computations using $X = 200$ into other parts of the system, causing cascading inconsistency that cannot be easily undone.

---

#### **Model Solution for B-A (b) — 7 marks**

The **SQL Standard (ANSI/ISO)** defines **four isolation levels** to control the impact of concurrency:

**Level 1 — READ UNCOMMITTED (1.5 marks):**
- Allows transactions to read data modified by **other uncommitted transactions**.
- **All three problems can occur** (dirty read, non-repeatable read, phantom read).
- Provides highest performance, lowest consistency.

**Level 2 — READ COMMITTED (1.5 marks):**
- A transaction sees **only committed data** at the time of its read.
- **Dirty Read is prevented.**
- Non-repeatable read and phantom read are still possible.

**Level 3 — REPEATABLE READ (2 marks):**
- Guarantees that within a transaction, **repeated reads of the same data item return the same value**.
- **Dirty Read and Non-Repeatable Read are prevented.**
- Phantom reads are still possible.

**Level 4 — SERIALIZABLE (2 marks):**
- The strictest level. Transactions execute as if they were running one after another.
- **All four concurrency problems are prevented** (including phantom reads).
- Provides lowest performance, highest consistency.

**Summary Table (bonus, 0.5 mark):**

| Level | Dirty Read | Non-Repeatable Read | Phantom Read |
|---|---|---|---|
| READ UNCOMMITTED | Allowed | Allowed | Allowed |
| READ COMMITTED | Prevented | Allowed | Allowed |
| REPEATABLE READ | Prevented | Prevented | Allowed |
| SERIALIZABLE | Prevented | Prevented | Prevented |

---

### **Question B-B (Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) With a clear schedule diagram, explain the Non-Repeatable Read and Phantom Read problems. How do they differ? (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the positive and negative impacts of allowing concurrent transactions in a DBMS. Justify why concurrency control is necessary with real-world examples. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-B (a) — 7 marks**

> **Non-Repeatable Read (3.5 marks):**

Occurs when a transaction **reads the same data item twice** within its execution and obtains **two different values** because another committed transaction modified the item in between.

**Schedule $S_3$:**
$$
R_1(X), \quad W_2(X), \quad \text{COMMIT}_2, \quad R_1(X)
$$

**Detailed steps:**
- $T_1$ reads $X = 100$ at time $t_1$. (Step 1)
- $T_2$ updates $X = 150$ and commits at time $t_2$. (Step 2)
- $T_1$ reads $X = 150$ at time $t_3 > t_2$. (Step 3)
- $T_1$ sees **two different values** ($100$ and $150$) for the **same read operation** in the same transaction → inconsistency.

**Distinguishing feature:** The conflict is on a **single data item** modified by another transaction.

> **Phantom Read (3.5 marks):**

Occurs when a transaction re-executes a query that returns **a set of rows** matching a predicate, and finds that **new rows (phantoms)** have been inserted (or deleted) by another committed transaction.

**Schedule $S_4$:**
$$
R_1(\sigma_{\text{balance}>5000}), \quad \text{INSERT}_2(A_{\text{new}}, 7000), \quad \text{COMMIT}_2, \quad R_1(\sigma_{\text{balance}>5000})
$$

**Detailed steps:**
- $T_1$ executes `SELECT * FROM Accounts WHERE balance > 5000;` returning **10 rows** at $t_1$. (Step 1)
- $T_2$ inserts a new account with balance 7000 and commits. (Step 2)
- $T_1$ re-executes the same query at $t_3$ and now returns **11 rows**. (Step 3)
- The **11th row is the phantom** — it did not exist in the first read.

**Key difference from Non-Repeatable Read:**
- Non-repeatable read affects a **single item's value**.
- Phantom read affects the **membership of a set of items** (row insertion/deletion).

---

#### **Model Solution for B-B (b) — 7 marks**

> **Positive Impacts of Concurrency (3 marks):**
> 
> 1. **Throughput Improvement:** Multiple transactions execute in parallel, dramatically increasing the number of completed transactions per second. Example: An airline reservation system processing 10,000 bookings/minute vs. 1,000 if serial.
> 2. **Reduced Response Time:** Average user waiting time drops because transactions are not queued indefinitely.
> 3. **Better Resource Utilization:** CPU and disk remain productive — while one transaction waits for disk I/O, another uses the CPU.
> 4. **Shared Data Access:** Multiple users can interact with the same database simultaneously without artificial barriers.

> **Negative Impacts of Concurrency (3 marks):**
> 
> 1. **Lost Update:** Concurrent writes can silently destroy each other's results.
> 2. **Dirty Read:** Reading uncommitted data can cause cascading errors after rollback.
> 3. **Non-Repeatable Read:** Repeated queries within a transaction yield inconsistent results.
> 4. **Phantom Read:** Set-based queries return different result sets within one transaction.
> 5. **Overhead of Locking:** Concurrency control itself consumes CPU and memory.

> **Justification with Real-World Example (1 mark):**
> 
> In an **online banking system**, two simultaneous withdrawals from a joint account must not result in the bank paying out more money than the account holds. Without concurrency control, a customer could "double-spend" their balance. Therefore, concurrency control mechanisms such as **2-Phase Locking (2PL)** are mandatory in real-world financial DBMS deployments.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing conflict types (W-W vs W-R vs R-W):** Many students mix up lost update (W-W) and dirty read (W-R). Use the mnemonic: **Lost = Write-Write**, **Dirty = Write-Read**, **Non-Repeating = Read-Write**. (Loses 1–2 marks per question.)
> 
> 2. **Omitting the abort/commit step:** In a dirty read schedule, students often forget to write `ABORT_1` at the end. Without this, the schedule does not illustrate the problem. (Loses 1 mark.)
> 
> 3. **Writing schedules in plain English:** Always write schedules in the **canonical notation** $R_i(X), W_i(X)$ with subscripts. Plain English descriptions earn partial credit at best. (Loses 0.5–1 mark.)
> 
> 4. **Missing the precedence graph:** When asked to "show why the schedule is incorrect," students frequently describe the problem verbally but skip drawing the **precedence graph** with the cycle. KTU examiners specifically expect this diagram. (Loses 2 marks.)
> 
> 5. **Confusing isolation levels with consistency models:** SQL isolation levels are **transaction-level concepts**, distinct from eventual consistency in distributed systems. Don't mix these. (Loses 1 mark.)
> 
> 6. **Forgetting units:** Writing "throughput = 180" without specifying "transactions per second (TPS)" loses the unit-marker mark. Always include units in numerical answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Impact of Concurrency**
> 
> - **Concurrency = simultaneous access** by multiple transactions; managed by the **Concurrency Control Subsystem** of the DBMS.
> - **Four key benefits:** improved throughput, reduced response time, better resource utilization, shared data access.
> - **Four classic problems to memorize:**
>   - **Lost Update** (W-W conflict): $R_1, R_2, W_1, W_2$ — first write is silently overwritten.
>   - **Dirty Read** (W-R conflict with abort): $W_1, R_2, \text{ABORT}_1$ — reader uses phantom value.
>   - **Non-Repeatable Read** (R-W conflict): $R_1, W_2, \text{COMMIT}_2, R_1$ — same item, two values.
>   - **Phantom Read** (Predicate R-W): insert/delete causes row-set to change.
> - **Four SQL Isolation Levels** (from weakest to strongest): **READ UNCOMMITTED → READ COMMITTED → REPEATABLE READ → SERIALIZABLE**. Higher level = more consistency, less performance.
> - **Serializability Theorem:** A schedule is correct iff it is equivalent to some serial schedule. **Conflict-serializability** is checked via the precedence graph: **acyclic ⟹ serializable**.
> - **Real-world analogy:** Bank counter manager preventing double-withdrawal from the same joint account.
> - **Key metrics:** Throughput (TPS), Speedup, Average Response Time.
> - **Standard textbook terminologies (KTU-favored):** Schedule notation uses $R_i(X)$ for read, $W_i(X)$ for write, with subscripts $i$ for transaction IDs.
> - **Concurrency control protocols** (next module topics): **2-Phase Locking (2PL), Timestamp Ordering (TSO), Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC), Multiversion Concurrency Control (MVCC).**
> - **ACID connection:** Concurrency issues threaten the **Isolation (I)** property of ACID transactions.
> - **Final tip:** Always draw the precedence graph and check for cycles in any 14-mark Part B question asking whether a schedule is conflict-serializable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed Databases: Distributed Systems, Introduction, Architecture, Distributed Database Concepts, Distributed Data Storage, Distributed Transactions, Commit Protocols, Concurrency Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST634 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Distributed Databases: Distributed Systems, Introduction, Architecture, Distributed Database Concepts, Distributed Data Storage, Distributed Transactions, Commit Protocols, Concurrency Control # 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Distributed Database System (DDBS) — Formal Definition A **Distributed Database** is a collection of multiple, logically interrelated databases that are physically distributed over a computer network. From the user's perspective, the system appears as a **single, unified database**, even though data resides at multiple geographically separated sites. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus Context):** > A *distributed database (DDB)* is a unified database built on top of a network of geographically distributed, autonomous local databases. The collection of local databases forms a **single logical database**, controlled by a *Distributed Database Management System (DDBMS)*. A **Distributed Database Management System (DDBMS)** is the software system that manages the distributed database transparently and provides a unified view to users while coordinating access, updates, and transactions across multiple sites. --- ## 1.2 🌍 Real-World Analogy — "The National Bank" Imagine a bank with branches in **Kerala, Delhi, and Mumbai**. - Each branch has its own local database of customers (physically separate). - Yet, when you walk into the Kerala branch and ask for your account balance, the system instantly fetches and updates your data — whether the record lives in Delhi or Mumbai. - You (the user) **never need to know** where your data physically resides. **This is the essence of a distributed database.** | Bank Analogy | Distributed Database | |---|---| | Bank Branch | Site / Node | | Branch's Local Records | Local Database | | Head Office Coordinator | Distributed DBMS | | Customer | User / Application | | You walking into any branch | *Location Transparency* | --- ## 1.3 Distributed Database — Core Properties (KTU High-Yield) A distributed database must satisfy **four defining properties**: > [!NOTE] > **The 4 Pillars of a Distributed Database (Must Remember for KTU)** 1. **Logical Integration** — Data is logically unified as a single database. 2. **Physical Distribution** — Data is physically stored across multiple sites. 3. **Heterogeneity Tolerance** — Sites may use different hardware/DBMS. 4. **Autonomy** — Each site can operate independently (controlled autonomy). --- ## 1.4 🔑 Key Characteristics of Distributed DBMS - **Location Transparency:** Users query without knowing *where* data is stored. - **Replication Transparency:** Users don't know if data is duplicated. - **Fragmentation Transparency:** Users don't know if data is partitioned. - **Failure Independence:** A failure at one site does not halt the entire system. - **Distributed Query Processing:** Queries are optimized and executed across sites. - **Distributed Transaction Management:** Ensures ACID properties across sites. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Centralized vs Distributed — Quick Reference:** > - Centralized DB → One site, one DBMS > - Distributed DB → Many sites, one logical DBMS (DDBMS), with autonomy + transparency --- # 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of Distributed Database Systems The architecture of a DDBMS can be classified along **three orthogonal dimensions** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus: ### A. Autonomy Dimension (Degree of Independence) | Level | Description | KTU Term | |---|---|---| | **Tight Integration** | One master DBMS controls others | Federated (Tightly Coupled) | | **Loose Integration** | Sites share a global schema but remain autonomous | Federated (Loosely Coupled) | | **No Integration** | Independent DBMSs communicate via middleware | Multi-Database | ### B. Heterogeneity Dimension - **Homogeneous:** Same DBMS at every site (e.g., all Oracle). - **Heterogeneous:** Different DBMSs coexist (e.g., Oracle + MySQL + PostgreSQL). ### C. Distribution Dimension - **Client/Server:** Clients at sites, single/multiple server(s). - **Peer-to-Peer (P2P):** Every site is both client and server — **no central server**, all sites are *equal peers*. This is the most flexible model. --- ## 2.2 Distributed Data Storage — Fragmentation & Replication > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 expects students to know the three fundamental storage strategies:** ### 1️⃣ Fragmentation Splitting a relation into smaller pieces. - **Horizontal Fragmentation:** Tuples (rows) distributed across sites. - $R_i = \sigma_{p_i}(R)$ - Union rule: $R = R_1 \cup R_2 \cup \dots \cup R_n$ - **Vertical Fragmentation:** Attributes (columns) distributed; primary key must be replicated. - $R_i = \pi_{A_i}(R)$ ### 2️⃣ Replication Storing copies of data at multiple sites. - **Full Replication:** Every site has a complete copy. - **Partial Replication:** Only some sites have copies. - **No Replication:** Each fragment stored at exactly one site. ### 3️⃣ Allocation Mapping fragments to sites — the core of distributed storage design. --- ## 2.3 🧮 Distributed Transactions A **distributed transaction** is one that accesses or updates data at multiple sites. It must preserve **ACID** properties *across the network*. ### ACID in Distributed Context | Property | Distributed Implication | |---|---| | **Atomicity** | All sites commit, or all rollback (→ **2PC / 3PC**) | | **Consistency** | Global consistency rules must hold across all sites | | **Isolation** | Concurrency control must work across sites | | **Durability** | Local DBMS handles; logs replicated to backup sites | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!NOTE] > These are the **must-know formulas** for solving KTU numerical/derivation problems on distributed databases. $$ N_R = \frac{\text{Number of sites holding replicated copies}}{\text{Total number of sites}} $$ $$ \text{Replication Factor (RF)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i $$ Where $C_i = 1$ if a copy of data exists at site $i$, else $0$. $$ \text{Read Quorum } (R) + \text{Write Quorum } (W) > N $$ Where $N$ = total number of replicas. This is the **Quorum Consensus Condition** for consistency. $$ \text{Majority Quorum} = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ For Read operations: $R = \lceil N/2 \rceil$ For Write operations: $W = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ $$ \text{Fragmentation Completeness} \Rightarrow R = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i $$ $$ \text{Fragmentation Disjointness} \Rightarrow R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset \; (i \neq j) \quad \text{(for horizontal)} $$ | Formula / Term | Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Use | |---|---|---|---| | Replication Factor | $N_R$ | Sites with copy / total sites | Ratio (0 to 1) | | Read Quorum | $R$ | Minimum replicas to read | Integer | | Write Quorum | $W$ | Minimum replicas to write | Integer | | Total Copies | $N$ | Total replicas of a fragment | Integer | | Quorum Condition | $R + W > N$ | Guarantees consistency | Boolean | | Majority Quorum | $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ | Standard quorum size | Integer | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Use:** > - These quorum equations power **Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra, Riak**, and **Google Spanner**'s replica coordination. > - KTU 2024 frequently asks to derive these — you must commit them to memory. --- ## 2.5 Concurrency Control in Distributed Databases Concurrency control ensures **serializability** of concurrent transactions across sites. ### Three main approaches: 1. **Locking-Based (Pessimistic):** 2PL, Distributed 2PL, Centralized 2PL. 2. **Timestamp-Based (Optimistic-Order):** Logical/Physical timestamps per site. 3. **Replication-Based (Quorum):** Read/Write quorum consensus. ### Deadlock Handling (Distributed) - **Centralized Detection:** One site is the global deadlock detector. - **Hierarchical Detection:** Tree of sites, each managing its subtree. - **Distributed Detection:** Sites exchange local wait-for graphs via **Chandy–Misra–Haas algorithm**. --- # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Quorum Sizes from First Principles We need to derive the minimum read and write quorums that guarantee **no two conflicting operations can proceed simultaneously**. ### 🎯 Assumption Let $N$ = total number of replicas of a data item $x$. We want the system to ensure: - A read always returns the **most recent write** (or at least, no stale value). - A write overlaps with another write (so only one succeeds). - A read overlaps with every write that is still in progress (so we read the latest). ### Step 1: Enforce Read-Write Overlap For a read to see the latest write, the read set must intersect the most recent write set: $$ R + W > N $$ **Reasoning:** If $R + W \le N$, it is possible that the read quorum and the latest write quorum do **not overlap**, allowing a read to fetch a stale copy. ### Step 2: Enforce Write-Write Overlap Two simultaneous writes must conflict so that they don't both succeed independently: $$ W + W > N \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2W > N $$ Solving for minimum integer $W$: $$ W = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ ### Step 3: Solve for Read Quorum $R$ Substitute $W$ back into $R + W > N$: $$ R + \left( \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 \right) > N $$ $$ R > N - \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor - 1 $$ Since $N - \lfloor N/2 \rfloor = \lceil N/2 \rceil$: $$ R > \left\lceil \frac{N}{2} \right\rceil - 1 $$ Therefore minimum integer: $$ R = \left\lceil \frac{N}{2} \right\rceil $$ ### Step 4: Final Compact Rule $$ R = \left\lceil \frac{N}{2} \right\rceil \quad , \quad W = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ ### ✅ Worked Numerical Example Suppose $N = 5$ replicas of a data item. Compute Write Quorum: $$ W = \left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 $$ Compute Read Quorum: $$ R = \left\lceil \frac{5}{2} \right\rceil = 3 $$ Verify: $$ R + W = 3 + 3 = 6 > 5 \quad ✅ $$ $$ W + W = 3 + 3 = 6 > 5 \quad ✅ $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** With 5 replicas, both reads and writes need 3 successful contacts. This ensures strong consistency — the trade-off being slightly higher latency. --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step: 2-Phase Commit Protocol (2PC) The **Two-Phase Commit (2PC)** protocol ensures atomic commitment of distributed transactions. It has a **Coordinator** and multiple **Cohorts (participants)**. ### Phase 1 — Voting (PREPARE) ``` Coordinator Cohort (Site) | ---- PREPARE ------------------> | | | (forces log, locks resource) | <---- VOTE YES / NO ------------- | ``` The cohort writes a `prepare` log record and replies: - **YES:** Ready to commit - **NO:** Cannot commit ### Phase 2 — Decision (COMMIT / ABORT) If **all** votes are YES → Coordinator sends `GLOBAL_COMMIT`. If **any** vote is NO (or timeout) → Coordinator sends `GLOBAL_ABORT`. ``` Coordinator Cohort | ---- GLOBAL_COMMIT / ABORT ----> | | | (writes commit/abort log) | <---- ACK ---------------------- | ``` ### Failure Handling in 2PC | Site Failure | Recovery | |---|---| | Cohort fails before vote | Coordinator aborts on timeout | | Cohort fails after YES, before commit | On recovery, cohort asks coordinator | | Coordinator fails after sending PREPARE | Cohorts must wait (→ **blocking protocol**) | > [!WARNING] > **2PC is BLOCKING.** If the coordinator fails after sending PREPARE but before sending the decision, cohorts must hold locks indefinitely — a classic KTU question. --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: 3-Phase Commit Protocol (3PC) 3PC fixes the blocking problem of 2PC by adding a **Pre-Commit phase**. ### Phase 1: CAN-COMMIT Coordinator asks participants if they can commit. Cohort writes `ready` log. ### Phase 2: PRE-COMMIT Coordinator sends `PRE-COMMIT` (only if all voted YES). Cohorts acknowledge. ### Phase 3: DO-COMMIT Coordinator sends `DO-COMMIT`. Cohorts commit and release locks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key 3PC property:** The *Pre-Commit* phase guarantees that **at least one quorum** of participants is aware of the decision, so a new coordinator can safely resume — 3PC is **non-blocking** (under reasonable failure assumptions). ### Trade-off Matrix: 2PC vs 3PC | Property | 2PC | 3PC | |---|---|---| | Phases | 2 | 3 | | Blocking on Coord. failure | **Yes** | **No** | | Message Complexity | $4N$ | $5N$ | | Tolerance to network partition | Low | Higher | | Use in Practice | XA, Java EE, Oracle | Spanner-inspired (modified) | --- ## 3.4 Distributed Concurrency Control — Locking Strategies ### A. Centralized 2PL - One site is the **Lock Manager**. - All lock requests funnel to it. - Pros: Simple. Cons: Single point of failure; bottleneck. ### B. Primary Copy 2PL - Each data item has a **primary site** owning its lock manager. - Pros: Distributed load. Cons: Primary site is critical for that data item. ### C. Distributed 2PL - **Lock manager exists at every site.** - A transaction that wants to lock $x$ at Site A sends a request to Site A's local lock manager. - Pros: No bottleneck. Cons: Deadlock detection is hard — distributed wait-for graph needed. --- ## 3.5 Distributed Deadlock Detection — Chandy–Misra–Haas Algorithm (Conceptual) The algorithm uses **probe messages** sent along the edges of the distributed wait-for graph. ``` Site i waits on Site j → Site i sends a probe (i, j, k) to Site j, where k is the initiator. ``` If the probe returns to initiator $k$, a deadlock cycle involving $k$ is detected. --- # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Three-Tier Distributed Database Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Application Layer
Clients and Apps] B[Global Schema Layer
Logical Unified View] C[Local Schema Layer
Fragmentation Mapping] D[Local DBMS Layer
Site 1 Oracle] E[Local DBMS Layer
Site 2 MySQL] F[Local DBMS Layer
Site 3 PostgreSQL] G[Physical Data Storage
Site 1 Disks] H[Physical Data Storage
Site 2 Disks] I[Physical Data Storage
Site 3 Disks] A --> B B --> C C --> D C --> E C --> F D --> G E --> H F --> I ``` ## 4.2 Two-Phase Commit Protocol Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Coord as Coordinator participant S1 as Cohort Site 1 participant S2 as Cohort Site 2 participant S3 as Cohort Site 3 Coord->>S1: PREPARE Coord->>S2: PREPARE Coord->>S3: PREPARE S1-->>Coord: VOTE YES S2-->>Coord: VOTE YES S3-->>Coord: VOTE YES Coord->>S1: GLOBAL COMMIT Coord->>S2: GLOBAL COMMIT Coord->>S3: GLOBAL COMMIT S1-->>Coord: ACK S2-->>Coord: ACK S3-->>Coord: ACK ``` ## 4.3 Three-Phase Commit Protocol Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Coord as Coordinator participant S1 as Cohort 1 participant S2 as Cohort 2 participant S3 as Cohort 3 Coord->>S1: CAN COMMIT Coord->>S2: CAN COMMIT Coord->>S3: CAN COMMIT S1-->>Coord: VOTE YES S2-->>Coord: VOTE YES S3-->>Coord: VOTE YES Coord->>S1: PRE COMMIT Coord->>S2: PRE COMMIT Coord->>S3: PRE COMMIT S1-->>Coord: ACK PRECOMMIT S2-->>Coord: ACK PRECOMMIT S3-->>Coord: ACK PRECOMMIT Coord->>S1: DO COMMIT Coord->>S2: DO COMMIT Coord->>S3: DO COMMIT S1-->>Coord: ACK COMMIT S2-->>Coord: ACK COMMIT S3-->>Coord: ACK COMMIT ``` ## 4.4 Distributed Fragmentation and Replication Strategy ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Global Relation R
Student Name Dept] B[Horizontal Fragment
R1 Kerala Students] C[Horizontal Fragment
R2 Delhi Students] D[Horizontal Fragment
R3 Mumbai Students] E[Replicated at Site A] F[Replicated at Site B] G[Replicated at Site C] A --> B A --> C A --> D B --> E C --> F D --> G B -.replicated.-> F C -.replicated.-> G ``` ## 4.5 Distributed Concurrency Control — Centralized vs Distributed 2PL ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CENTRALIZED[Centralized 2PL] T1C[Transaction T1] --> LM[Central Lock Manager] T2C[Transaction T2] --> LM T3C[Transaction T3] --> LM LM --> DB1[(Database Site)] end subgraph DISTRIBUTED[Distributed 2PL] T1D[Transaction T1] --> LM1[Lock Manager Site 1] T2D[Transaction T2] --> LM2[Lock Manager Site 2] T3D[Transaction T3] --> LM3[Lock Manager Site 3] LM1 --> DB1A[(Site 1 Data)] LM2 --> DB2A[(Site 2 Data)] LM3 --> DB3A[(Site 3 Data)] end ``` --- # 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Define a distributed database. List its four defining properties.** **Model Answer:** A **distributed database** is a collection of logically interrelated databases distributed over a computer network, managed by a DDBMS such that the system appears as a single unified database to the user. **Four Defining Properties:** 1. **Logical Integration** — data is a single logical whole. 2. **Physical Distribution** — data physically resides at multiple sites. 3. **Heterogeneity Tolerance** — different sites can use different DBMSs. 4. **Autonomy** — sites retain controlled independence. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between 2PC and 3PC.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | 2PC | 3PC | |---|---|---| | Phases | 2 (Prepare, Commit) | 3 (CanCommit, PreCommit, DoCommit) | | Blocking | Yes — blocking on coordinator failure | No — non-blocking | | Message Cost | Lower | Higher | | Tolerance | Low | Higher | --- ## 📙 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the architecture of a distributed database system in detail, with reference to the three dimensions: autonomy, heterogeneity, and distribution. *(7 marks)* **(b)** What are the different strategies for distributed data storage? Discuss fragmentation, replication, and allocation with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* #### ✅ Model Solution (a) The architecture of a Distributed Database Management System (DDBMS) can be described along **three orthogonal dimensions**: 1. **Autonomy Dimension** — Refers to the degree of independence of each DBMS site. - **Tight Integration (Federated Tightly Coupled):** A single global schema controls all sites; minimal autonomy. - **Loose Integration (Federated Loosely Coupled):** Each site retains significant autonomy; shares a common global schema. - **No Integration (Multi-Database):** Independent DBMSs, no global schema; communication via middleware. 2. **Heterogeneity Dimension** — Refers to differences in hardware, DBMS software, or data models. - **Homogeneous:** Same DBMS everywhere. - **Heterogeneous:** Mixed DBMSs, schemas, and platforms. 3. **Distribution Dimension** — Refers to how clients and servers are organized. - **Client/Server:** Centralized server(s) serving distributed clients. - **Peer-to-Peer:** No central server; every site acts as both client and server. These three dimensions together define any DDBMS architecture (e.g., *tightly-coupled, homogeneous, client-server* = traditional distributed DB). #### ✅ Model Solution (b) Distributed data storage strategies: 1. **Fragmentation:** Breaking a relation into smaller logical pieces. - *Horizontal Fragmentation:* Distributes rows based on a predicate. Example: `Student` relation split by state — `Student_Kerala`, `Student_Delhi`. - *Vertical Fragmentation:* Distributes columns; primary key replicated. Example: `Student(name, id, dept, marks)` split into two with `id` duplicated. 2. **Replication:** Maintaining multiple copies of fragments at different sites. - **Full Replication:** Every site has full data (high availability, high update cost). - **Partial Replication:** Some fragments replicated selectively. - **No Replication:** Each fragment stored at exactly one site. 3. **Allocation:** Assigning fragments to physical sites. - Optimal allocation balances **query cost**, **update cost**, **storage**, and **availability**. **Example:** A bank's `Customer` relation horizontally fragmented by region; the `Kerala_Fragment` stored at Site 1 with a backup copy at Site 3 for fault tolerance. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]** **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Two-Phase Commit (2PC)** protocol. Discuss how it handles different failure scenarios. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the **Quorum Consensus** method for distributed concurrency control. Derive the conditions for read and write quorums. *(7 marks)* #### ✅ Model Solution (a) The **Two-Phase Commit (2PC)** protocol guarantees atomicity of distributed transactions through a **coordinator** and **cohorts (participants)**. **Phase 1 — Voting (PREPARE):** - Coordinator sends `PREPARE` to all cohorts. - Each cohort writes a `prepare` log record, acquires locks, and replies `VOTE YES` or `VOTE NO`. **Phase 2 — Decision (COMMIT/ABORT):** - If all votes are YES → Coordinator writes `commit` log → sends `GLOBAL_COMMIT`. - If any vote is NO or timeout → Coordinator writes `abort` log → sends `GLOBAL_ABORT`. - Cohorts acknowledge (ACK) after applying. **Failure Handling:** - **Cohort fails before voting:** Coordinator times out and aborts. - **Cohort fails after YES but before COMMIT:** On recovery, cohort asks coordinator for the decision. - **Coordinator fails after sending PREPARE:** Cohorts wait in an *uncertain* state — **2PC is blocking**. - **Coordinator fails after decision logged:** A new coordinator can resume from log. (See Section 4.3 for the Mermaid sequence diagram.) #### ✅ Model Solution (b) **Quorum Consensus Method:** Let $N$ be the total number of replicas of a data item. The **Read Quorum** $R$ and **Write Quorum** $W$ are the minimum number of replicas that must respond to a read/write respectively. **Conditions to guarantee consistency:** 1. **Read–Write overlap:** $R + W > N$ 2. **Write–Write overlap:** $W + W > N \Rightarrow 2W > N$ From these: $$ W = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ $$ R = \left\lceil \frac{N}{2} \right\rceil $$ **Example:** For $N = 7$ replicas: - $W = 4$, $R = 4$. - $R + W = 8 > 7$ ✅ - $2W = 8 > 7$ ✅ This ensures every read sees the latest write and no two writes succeed independently. (See derivation in Section 3.1.) --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers** > - **Don't confuse** "Horizontal" with "Vertical" fragmentation. Horizontal = rows, Vertical = columns (with replicated PK). > - **2PC is BLOCKING** — if you write "2PC is non-blocking," you lose 2 marks instantly. > - **Quorum formula** is often asked for derivation — skipping the algebraic steps = −3 marks. > - Always mention **location/replication/fragmentation transparency** in DDBMS definitions. > - Drawing 2PC *without* distinguishing the **two phases** loses easy marks. > - In Primary Copy 2PL, never claim the *primary* is the lock for *all* data — it's per data item. --- ## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🚀 Final Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2** - ✅ Distributed DB = logically unified, physically distributed, governed by DDBMS. - ✅ Four defining properties: Integration, Distribution, Heterogeneity, Autonomy. - ✅ Three architectural dimensions: **Autonomy, Heterogeneity, Distribution**. - ✅ Fragmentation: **Horizontal** (rows), **Vertical** (columns), **Mixed**. - ✅ Replication: **Full / Partial / None** with **Primary Copy** technique. - ✅ 12 Rules of Codd's Distributed DB extend to the 12+1=12 rules. - ✅ **2PC** = 2 phases (Prepare, Commit); blocking protocol. - ✅ **3PC** = 3 phases (CanCommit, PreCommit, DoCommit); non-blocking. - ✅ ACID properties must hold **across sites** for distributed transactions. - ✅ Quorum Condition: $R + W > N$; $2W > N$. - ✅ Majority quorum: $W = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$, $R = \lceil N/2 \rceil$. - ✅ Distributed concurrency control: **Centralized 2PL, Primary Copy 2PL, Distributed 2PL**. - ✅ Deadlock detection: **Chandy–Misra–Haas algorithm** with probe messages. - ✅ **Real-world examples:** Google Spanner (3PC variants), Amazon DynamoDB (quorum), Cassandra (tunable consistency). ---

topics.title: Query Processing and Decomposition: Query Processing Objectives, Characterization of Query Processors, Layers of Query Processing, Query Decomposition, Localization of Distributed Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST634 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Query Processing and Decomposition: Query Processing Objectives, Characterization of Query Processors, Layers of Query Processing, Query Decomposition, Localization of Distributed Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Query Processing in a Distributed Database System?

**Query Processing** is the sequence of activities involved in retrieving data from a distributed database (DDB) in response to a user query. It encompasses the translation of a high-level declarative query (typically expressed in SQL or relational calculus) into a low-level, optimized execution strategy that operates across multiple, geographically dispersed, autonomous processing nodes connected by a network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
> Query Processing is a multi-stage transformation pipeline that converts a non-procedural query into a procedural query execution plan. In a distributed context, it additionally determines the *best* sites for processing sub-queries, the *order* of data movement, and the *medium* (network or disk) for intermediate result transmission — all while minimizing a combined objective function of **response time**, **total cost**, and **network bandwidth**.

### 1.2 The Core Objectives of Distributed Query Processing

The primary objectives that drive every optimization decision in distributed query processing are:

| # | Objective | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Minimize Response Time** | The wall-clock elapsed time between query submission and the last tuple delivered to the user. Treated as the **primary** objective in interactive (OLTP/OLAP) workloads. |
| 2 | **Minimize Total Cost** | Sum of local processing cost (CPU + I/O) and communication cost (network bytes × latency). |
| 3 | **Maximize Throughput** | Number of queries processed per unit time, relevant in multi-user environments. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently ask for the *tri-objective function*. Memorize the trio: **Response Time + Total Cost + Network Bandwidth Utilization**. The fundamental difference between centralized and distributed query processing is that distributed QP must manage the **cost of data transfer** across sites, which is often the dominant expense.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Multi-Branch Restaurant Chain

Imagine a customer in **Kochi** who wants a meal consisting of *Appam* (from the **Chennai** branch), *Fish Curry* (from the **Mangalore** branch), and a local drink. The **Waiter (Query Processor)** must:

1. **Understand the order** (Query Decomposition — what was actually asked?)
2. **Check which branch has fresh ingredients** (Localization of Distributed Data).
3. **Decide the preparation order** (Optimization — e.g., do we cook locally or fly fish from Mangalore?).
4. **Coordinate simultaneous cooking and final plating** (Execution — parallel, distributed, networked).

A *good* waiter minimizes total delivery time (response time), total food cost + courier cost (total cost), and uses courier services efficiently (bandwidth). A *bad* waiter flies every ingredient from Mangalore to Kochi before cooking — exactly what a naive distributed query processor would do without optimization.

### 1.4 The Distributed Query Processing Pipeline — At a Glance

A high-level look at the journey of a query:

```
USER (SQL) 
   ↓
[Query Decomposition]      ← Calculus → Algebra → Normalized Form
   ↓
[Data Localization]        ← Map fragments to sites, reduce using semi-joins
   ↓
[Global Query Optimization]← Choose best join order, sites, parallel strategy
   ↓
[Local Query Optimization] ← Per-site DBMS internal optimization
   ↓
[Distributed Execution]    ← Parallel operators + ship intermediates
   ↓
RESULT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this module critical for KTU?** Module 2 of PECST634 essentially *defines* the engineering heart of a Distributed DBMS. Without it, fragmentation (Module 1) is just a file-splitting exercise. The query processor is the *brain* that gives fragmentation its performance purpose.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Characterization of Query Processors

A query processor in a distributed environment is *characterized* along **three orthogonal axes** — these are direct, high-yield KTU questions.

### Axis 1: Layer of Optimization Decision

| Type | Layer of Operation | Granularity | Typical Use |
|------|--------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **One-shot / Monolithic** | Global optimizer handles the entire query tree as one unit | Coarse | Simple DDBs |
| **Iterative / Modular** | Decomposition → Localization → Optimization → Execution as separate layers | Fine-grained | Modern DDBMS (Standard) |

### Axis 2: Cost of Optimization vs. Cost of Execution

- **Static (Compile-time) Optimization** — Build one plan, execute many times. Best for repetitive queries. **Lower runtime overhead**, higher compile cost.
- **Dynamic (Run-time) Optimization** — Re-optimize during execution. Adapts to actual cardinalities. **Higher runtime overhead**, robust against estimation errors.
- **Hybrid** — Most modern systems.

### Axis 3: Site Topology & Heterogeneity

| Type | Description | KTU Distinguishing Feature |
|------|-------------|----------------------------|
| **Homogeneous DDBMS** | All sites run the same DBMS, same schema view, identical capabilities. | Simplifies optimization; uses **system catalog** uniformly. |
| **Heterogeneous DDBMS** | Sites have different DBMSs (Oracle + PostgreSQL + MySQL), different schemas. | Requires **mediator/wrapper architecture**, schema translation, capability negotiation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Past-Year Pattern:** "Differentiate homogeneous and heterogeneous distributed DBMS with respect to query processing." This is a 7-mark favourite. The *key discriminator* is whether the query processor can assume a **single global catalog** (homogeneous) or must use a **mediator + wrappers** (heterogeneous).

### 2.2 Layers of Query Processing — The Canonical Four-Layer Architecture

The four canonical layers, top to bottom, are:

#### Layer 1: Query Decomposition
Produces a **standard relational algebra expression** that is syntactically and semantically correct. Output is the **Normalized Query** (also called the *canonical form*).

**Internal steps (5):**
1. **Lexical & Syntactic Analysis** — Tokenize and parse the SQL.
2. **Query Normalization** — Apply equivalence rules to flatten nested sub-queries and AND/OR predicates into conjunctive/disjunctive normal form.
3. **Semantic Analysis** — Verify relation/attribute names, types, integrity constraints via the global schema.
4. **Simplification / Reduction** — Remove redundant predicates (e.g., `age > 10 AND age > 20` → `age > 20`).
5. **Calculus → Algebra Translation** — Convert tuple/domain relational calculus into a relational algebra tree using the safe-expression transformation.

#### Layer 2: Data Localization
The input normalized algebra tree references *global relations*. The output is a **fragment query** — an algebra expression that references *physical fragments* at specific sites. This is where the global schema → fragmentation schema mapping is applied.

#### Layer 3: Global Query Optimization
Finds the **near-optimal distributed execution plan** — which site hosts which operator, in what order joins occur, whether semi-joins are used, and which intermediate results are materialized.

#### Layer 4: Local Query Optimization
Delegated to the local DBMS at each site. Optimizes access paths (indexes, join methods) for the sub-plan assigned to that site.

### 2.3 Query Decomposition — Detailed Mechanics

**Goal:** Given an SQL query, produce an equivalent normalized relational algebra expression.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Parsing**: An SQL string is fed to a parser that builds a parse tree using the grammar of the language.
2. **Normalization**: A query in conjunctive normal form (CNF) is a conjunction of disjunctions:
$$Q \equiv (P_1 \vee P_2) \wedge (P_3 \vee P_4) \wedge \dots$$
This step eliminates nesting and quantifiers.
3. **Analysis**: Cross-checks every relation, attribute, and type against the *global conceptual schema* and the access rights in the system catalog.
4. **Elimination of Redundancy**: Apply idempotence, absorption, and contradiction laws to remove useless predicates.
5. **Rewriting**: Convert the normalized calculus query into a **canonical relational algebra expression** using the following equivalences (representative subset):

| Calculus Construct | Algebra Equivalent |
|--------------------|---------------------|
| $\forall x \in R,\ P(x)$ | $\neg(\exists x \in R,\ \neg P(x))$ |
| $\exists x \in R,\ P(x)$ | $\pi(\sigma_P(R))$ |
| $\forall$ over join | Join followed by projection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Term:** The output of decomposition is called the **Normalized Query (NQ)**. It is *correct*, *minimal*, and *complete* — but it is **NOT** yet execution-ready because it still references *global* relations rather than physical fragments.

### 2.4 Localization of Distributed Data — The Core Reduction Problem

Given a query `Q` referencing global relations `R, S, T`, and a fragmentation schema `{R_1, R_2, R_3, S_1, S_2, T_1}`, the localization problem is to **rewrite Q as Q'** that references only fragments, *without losing or duplicating tuples*.

#### Two Reduction Phases (4 sub-steps total):

**Phase 2.1: Primary Horizontal Reduction**
For a query `Q` containing a selection on a *horizontally* fragmented relation `R`:
$$Q \rightarrow \pi_{attrs}(\sigma_{P}(R))$$

Since $R = R_1 \cup R_2 \cup R_3$ (disjoint union), the rule is:
$$\sigma_P(R) = \sigma_P(R_1) \cup \sigma_P(R_2) \cup \sigma_P(R_3)$$

But for any fragment $R_i$ where $R_i$'s *minterm predicate* $m_i$ makes $P \wedge m_i$ **unsatisfiable** (always false), the fragment is *eliminated* from the query. This is the **primary reduction rule**.

> **Example:** Fragmentation by city (Trivandrum, Kochi, Kozhikode). Query: `WHERE city = 'Kochi'`. The Trivandrum and Kozhikode fragments are *eliminated* because their minterms are mutually exclusive with the selection.

**Phase 2.2: Derived Horizontal Reduction (Join Reduction)**
For a query with a **join** between a horizontally fragmented relation `R` and `S`, the standard union is:
$$\sigma_P(R \bowtie S) = \bigcup_{i,j} \sigma_P(R_i \bowtie S_j)$$

The *derived reduction* applies the rule: a fragment $R_i$ *only needs to join with* those fragments of $S$ whose minterm $m_j$ is **compatible** with $m_i$ under the join predicate. This is the foundation of **semi-join-based optimization**.

**Phase 2.3: Vertical Reduction**
For a *vertically* fragmented relation $R = R_1 \bowtie R_2 \bowtie R_3$ (joined on the primary key), the query projection on attributes $A$ is:
$$\pi_A(R) = \pi_A(R_1 \bowtie R_2 \bowtie R_3)$$
A fragment $R_i$ that contains *none* of the projected attributes (other than the join key) can sometimes be excluded — but typically all vertical fragments are still required for the reconstruction.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Cost Functions for Distributed Query Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** The vertical pipe `|` is never used in the table below. All absolute values use $\vert$ to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Cost Component | Symbolic Form | Definition / When Used |
|---|----------------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Total Cost of a Plan** | $C_{total} = C_{CPU} + C_{I/O} + C_{MSG}$ | $C_{MSG}$ is unique to distributed QP. |
| 2 | **Communication Cost (Size of Relation Transfer)** | $C_{MSG}(R) = C_0 + size(R) \cdot C_1$ | $C_0$ = fixed startup cost per message (latency, in seconds), $C_1$ = per-byte transfer cost. |
| 3 | **Time to Send Relation R** | $T_{msg}(R) = C_0 + \frac{size(R)}{bandwidth}$ | $size(R) = card(R) \times len(R)$ where $card$ is cardinality and $len$ is tuple length. |
| 4 | **Response Time (Sequential)** | $T_{resp} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{stage_i}$ | Sum of all sequential stage latencies. |
| 5 | **Response Time (Parallel with n sites)** | $T_{resp} = \max_{i} T_{stage_i} + \sum \text{sequential}$ | Max of parallel branch times + sequential orchestration. |
| 6 | **Semi-Join Cost (R ⋉ S)** | $C_{SJ} = 2 \cdot C_{MSG} + C_{local}(\pi_{join}(S))$ | $C_{MSG}$ for sending projection + response. |
| 7 | **Join Cost (Direct, no semi-join)** | $C_{join} = 3 \cdot C_{MSG} + C_{local}(R \bowtie S)$ | Ship both R and S to a third site. |
| 8 | **Cost Reduction Condition for Semi-Join** | $C_{SJ} < C_{join}$ ⇔ $size(\pi_{join}(S)) \ll size(S)$ | Decision rule: use semi-join iff projected join attribute is much smaller. |
| 9 | **Cardinality of Join** | $card(R \bowtie S) = \frac{card(R) \cdot card(S)}{\max(V(R.A), V(S.A))}$ | $V$ = number of distinct values of join attribute $A$. |
| 10 | **Size of Selection** | $size(\sigma_P(R)) = size(R) \cdot sel(P)$ | $sel(P)$ = selectivity of predicate $P$ (fraction of tuples satisfying $P$). |
| 11 | **Size of Projection** | $size(\pi_A(R)) = card(R) \cdot \sum_{a \in A} len(a)$ | With duplicate elimination: multiplied by a dedup factor. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These cost functions are the **workhorses** of the **Apache Calcite** optimizer (used in Apache Flink, Hive, Drill) and the **Volcano/Cascades** framework (used in PostgreSQL, SQL Server, MonDB). Modern optimizers also incorporate **histogram-based cardinality estimation** and **adaptive re-optimization** (e.g., Google's Mesa, Snowflake's auto-clustering).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — Query Decomposition of a Nested SQL Query

**Given Query:** Find the names of all suppliers in Kerala who supply at least one red part.

**SQL Form:**
```sql
SELECT S.SNAME
FROM   S
WHERE  S.SCITY = 'Kerala'
  AND  S.S# IN ( SELECT SP.S#
                 FROM   SP
                 WHERE  SP.P# IN ( SELECT P.P#
                                   FROM   P
                                   WHERE  P.COLOR = 'RED' ) );
```

### Step 1: Lexical & Syntactic Analysis
The parser tokenizes the SQL into a parse tree. The result is a tree with the outer `SELECT` as the root and two nested `IN` sub-queries as subtrees.

### Step 2: Normalization
The nested `IN` sub-queries are converted into join-based equivalents.

**IN Subquery 1 (innermost):**
$$Q_1: \{P \vert P.COLOR = 'RED'\} \equiv \sigma_{P.COLOR = 'RED'}(P)$$

**IN Subquery 2:**
$$Q_2: \{SP \vert SP.P\# \in Q_1\} \equiv \pi_{S\#}(\sigma_{P\# = P\#}(SP \bowtie Q_1)) \equiv \pi_{S\#}(SP \bowtie Q_1)$$

**Outer Query:**
$$Q_{out}: \{S \vert S.SCITY = 'Kerala' \land S.S\# \in Q_2\}$$
$$\equiv \pi_{SNAME}(\sigma_{SCITY='Kerala' \land S\# = S\#}(S \bowtie Q_2))$$

### Step 3: Semantic Analysis
- `S`, `SP`, `P` exist in global schema ✓
- `SNAME`, `SCITY` are attributes of `S` ✓
- `S#` is the join key between `S` and `SP` ✓
- `P#` is the join key between `SP` and `P` ✓

### Step 4: Simplification
- `SCITY = 'Kerala' AND SCITY = 'Kerala'` → `SCITY = 'Kerala'` (idempotence, no redundancy in this case)
- Cross-product with explicit predicate `S.S# = SP.S#` is collapsed to a join.

### Step 5: Calculus → Algebra Translation (Canonical Form)

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{canonical} &= \pi_{SNAME}\Big( \sigma_{SCITY='Kerala'}\big( S \bowtie_{S\#} (\ \pi_{S\#}(\ SP \bowtie_{P\#} \sigma_{COLOR='RED'}(P)\ )\ )\big)\Big) \\
&= \pi_{SNAME}\Big( \sigma_{SCITY='Kerala'}\big( S \bowtie_{S\#} \pi_{S\#}\big( SP \bowtie_{P\#} \sigma_{COLOR='RED'}(P) \big) \big) \Big)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Normalized Query (NQ)**. It is semantically equivalent to the original nested SQL, but is now in a form that can be further optimized and localized.

### Step 3.2 — Optimization Equivalences Applied

Using the **commutativity of join with selection** and the **pushing selections down**:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{opt} &= \pi_{SNAME}\Big( \sigma_{SCITY='Kerala'}(S) \bowtie_{S\#} \pi_{S\#}\big( SP \bowtie_{P\#} \sigma_{COLOR='RED'}(P) \big) \Big) \\
&= \pi_{SNAME}\Big( \sigma_{SCITY='Kerala'}(S) \bowtie_{S\#} \pi_{S\#}\big( \sigma_{COLOR='RED'}(P) \bowtie_{P\#} SP \big) \Big) \quad \text{(join commutativity)} \\
&= \pi_{SNAME}\Big( \sigma_{SCITY='Kerala'}(S) \bowtie_{S\#} \pi_{S\#}\big( \sigma_{COLOR='RED'}(P) \bowtie_{P\#} SP \big) \Big) \quad \text{(push \sigma through joins)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This pushed-down form is *vastly* cheaper to execute because $\sigma_{SCITY='Kerala'}(S)$ and $\sigma_{COLOR='RED'}(P)$ produce tiny intermediate relations, drastically reducing the join input sizes. This is a direct application of the **principle of selection push-down** — universally cited in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough — Localization using Primary and Derived Reduction

**Scenario:** Relation `EMP(ENO, ENAME, DEPTNO, SAL)` is **horizontally fragmented** by department:
- $E_1$ = employees in D1 (minterm $m_1: DEPTNO = 'D1'$)
- $E_2$ = employees in D2 (minterm $m_2: DEPTNO = 'D2'$)
- $E_3$ = employees in D3 (minterm $m_3: DEPTNO = 'D3'$)

Relation `DEPT(DNO, DNAME, LOC)` is **horizontally fragmented** by location:
- $D_1$ = departments in India (minterm $m_1': LOC = 'India'$)
- $D_2$ = departments in UAE (minterm $m_2': LOC = 'UAE'$)

**Query:** Find the names of all employees in India working on a project.

```sql
SELECT E.ENAME
FROM   EMP E JOIN DEPT D ON E.DEPTNO = D.DNO
WHERE  D.LOC = 'India';
```

### Step 1: Normalized Algebra

$$Q = \pi_{ENAME}\big( EMP \bowtie_{DEPTNO = DNO} \sigma_{LOC='India'}(DEPT) \big)$$

### Step 2: Apply Primary Horizontal Reduction on DEPT

The predicate $LOC = 'India'$ is applied to DEPT. Using the disjoint union $DEPT = D_1 \cup D_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{LOC='India'}(DEPT) &= \sigma_{LOC='India'}(D_1 \cup D_2) \\
&= \sigma_{LOC='India'}(D_1) \cup \sigma_{LOC='India'}(D_2) \\
&= D_1 \cup \emptyset \quad \text{(since } D_2 \text{ has } LOC = 'UAE' \text{, the predicate fails)} \\
&= D_1
\end{aligned}$$

So the localized query becomes:

$$Q_{loc} = \pi_{ENAME}\big( EMP \bowtie_{DEPTNO = DNO} D_1 \big)$$

### Step 3: Apply Derived Horizontal Reduction on EMP (Join Reduction)

Since EMP is now joined only with $D_1$ (Indian departments), we determine which EMP fragments need to participate. The rule: a fragment $E_i$ joins with $D_1$ **only if** there exist tuples in $E_i$ with a $DEPTNO$ value that also appears in $D_1$'s $DNO$ column.

For employees in **department D1** (Indian), this requires accessing the $DNO$ values present in $D_1$. If $D_1 = \{(D1, Sales, India), (D2, Marketing, India)\}$, then $E_1$ (D1) and $E_2$ (D2) must participate, but $E_3$ (D3) might still be needed if D3 is also Indian — assuming the minterm is by DEPTNO, not LOC.

For a clean case, suppose $E_1$ contains employees in DEPTNO = 'D1', $E_2$ in 'D2', $E_3$ in 'D3', and only D1 and D2 are Indian. Then:

$$Q_{loc} = \pi_{ENAME}\big( (E_1 \cup E_2) \bowtie_{DEPTNO = DNO} D_1 \big)$$

$E_3$ is **eliminated** by derived reduction — saving one full fragment scan and one join input.

### Step 4: Cost Comparison

| Strategy | Number of EMP fragments joined | Join input size | Approx. cost (C) |
|----------|-------------------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Naive (no reduction)** | 3 ($E_1, E_2, E_3$) | $3 \cdot size(E) / 3 = size(E)$ | $3 \cdot C_{join}$ |
| **Primary + Derived Reduction** | 2 ($E_1, E_2$) | $\frac{2}{3} \cdot size(E)$ | $\frac{2}{3} \cdot C_{join}$ |
| **Savings** | — | **~33% reduction** in join work | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Always show the **disjoint union decomposition step** explicitly. Many students skip $\sigma_{P}(R_1 \cup R_2) = \sigma_{P}(R_1) \cup \sigma_{P}(R_2)$ and lose marks for missing the distributive law application.

## 3.4 Semi-Join Reduction — Algebraic Derivation

**Setup:** Relations $R$ at Site 1 and $S$ at Site 2. Compute $R \bowtie_{R.A = S.B} S$ at Site 3.

**Naive Cost:**
$$C_{naive} = size(R) + size(S)$$
(Ship both relations to Site 3, then join.)

**Semi-Join Plan:**
1. At Site 2, project: $S' = \pi_B(S)$. Cost: $size(S')$.
2. Ship $S'$ to Site 1. Cost: $size(S')$.
3. At Site 1, semi-join: $R' = R \bowtie S' = R \ltimes S$. Cost: local.
4. Ship $R'$ to Site 3. Cost: $size(R')$.
5. At Site 3, final join: $R' \bowtie S$. Cost: $size(R') + size(S)$.

**Total Cost with Semi-Join:**
$$C_{semi} = size(S') + size(R') + size(R') + size(S)$$

**Condition for Benefit:**
$$C_{semi} < C_{naive}$$
$$\iff size(S') + 2 \cdot size(R') + size(S) < size(R) + size(S)$$
$$\iff size(S') + 2 \cdot size(R') < size(R)$$

This holds when $R'$ (the semi-joined result on $R$) is *much smaller* than $R$ — i.e., the join is *highly selective* on $R$. This is the foundational cost-based rule for semi-join program generation in the **AHY (Apers, Hevner, Yao) algorithm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often quote "$C_{semi} < C_{naive}$" *without deriving* the conditions. For a 7-mark KTU question, you must show the algebraic manipulation. The expression $size(S') + 2 \cdot size(R') < size(R)$ is the key derived result.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**"State and explain the three main objectives of distributed query processing."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The three main objectives of distributed query processing are:

1. **Minimize Response Time** — Reduce the wall-clock time elapsed between query submission and the delivery of the last result tuple to the user. This is the primary metric for interactive workloads (OLAP, OLTP) and is the *most frequently weighted* objective in real systems.

2. **Minimize Total Cost** — Reduce the combined consumption of local computing resources (CPU cycles, disk I/O) and communication resources (network bandwidth, message latency). Total cost is essential in batch processing and shared-resource environments.

3. **Maximize Throughput** — Maximize the number of queries processed per unit time. In multi-user settings, the optimizer may trade a slight increase in individual query time for higher aggregate query throughput.

The **fundamental difference** from centralized query processing is that distributed query processing must additionally manage **data communication cost**, which is often the dominant component. The three objectives are interrelated: minimizing one does *not* always minimize the others, making multi-objective optimization a non-trivial research and engineering problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** 1 mark each for stating the three objectives + 0 mark for difference, OR 1 mark each + bonus 0.5 for the *distributed-specific* note. A complete answer should mention **all three explicitly**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**"Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous distributed database systems with respect to query processing."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Homogeneous DDBMS | Heterogeneous DDBMS |
|--------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Schema** | Single global conceptual schema | Multiple local schemas requiring translation |
| **DBMS Software** | Same DBMS at all sites | Different DBMSs (e.g., Oracle + MySQL + PostgreSQL) |
| **Query Processing** | Uses a *single* system catalog; unified optimizer | Uses a **mediator/wrapper** architecture; per-site capability negotiation |
| **Cost Model** | Uniform across sites | Must account for site-specific cost functions and translation overhead |
| **Implementation** | Tighter, faster | Looser, requires schema integration and wrapper development |
| **Example** | Distributed Oracle across data centers | Federated DB integrating MySQL, Oracle, and MongoDB |

In a **homogeneous** system, the query processor can assume uniform SQL dialects, data types, and a single global catalog, leading to simpler and more efficient optimization. In a **heterogeneous** system, the query processor must use a **mediator** layer that translates queries into local dialects, plus **wrappers** that adapt each local DBMS's interface. This adds latency, complexity, and uncertainty to the cost model.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** ESE Module Internal Choice — Answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Explain the **four layers of distributed query processing** with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Distributed query processing is structured as a four-layer pipeline. Each layer transforms the query and passes a progressively more executable form to the next.

**Layer 1: Query Decomposition**
- **Input:** User's high-level query (typically SQL or relational calculus).
- **Activities:** Lexical/syntactic analysis → normalization into CNF/DNF → semantic analysis against global schema → simplification (redundancy elimination) → translation from calculus to relational algebra.
- **Output:** A *correct, minimal, and complete* canonical relational algebra expression — the **Normalized Query (NQ)**.
- **Key Feature:** Operates *only* on global relations; ignores distribution, fragmentation, and site allocation.

**Layer 2: Data Localization**
- **Input:** The NQ referencing global relations.
- **Activities:** Apply the fragmentation schema to map global relations to physical fragments. Execute **primary horizontal reduction** (eliminate fragments where selection is unsatisfiable), **derived horizontal reduction** (eliminate unnecessary joins using minterm compatibility), and **vertical reduction** checks.
- **Output:** A **fragment query** — an algebra tree referencing physical fragments at specific sites.
- **Key Feature:** This is the layer that *introduces site-awareness* into the query plan.

**Layer 3: Global Query Optimization**
- **Input:** The fragment query.
- **Activities:** Enumerate candidate distributed execution plans (different join orders, different result-materialization sites, different semi-join strategies). Estimate cost using the cost formulas. Select the **near-optimal plan** that minimizes the weighted objective function.
- **Output:** An **optimal distributed execution plan** with assigned operators, sites, and data-flow directions.
- **Key Feature:** The most computationally intensive layer; uses algorithms like AHY, SDD-1, or Iterative Improvement.

**Layer 4: Local Query Optimization**
- **Input:** A sub-plan assigned to a specific site.
- **Activities:** Delegate to the local DBMS's internal optimizer. Choose access paths (indexes, full scans), local join methods (nested-loop, sort-merge, hash join), and local operator order.
- **Output:** A fully executable local plan per site.
- **Key Feature:** Each site independently optimizes its sub-plan; the global plan orchestrates them.

> **Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 (Mermaid diagram) of these notes for the four-layer architecture.

#### **(b)** With a suitable example, explain **primary and derived horizontal fragmentation reduction** in data localization. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Primary Horizontal Reduction** handles the case where a query contains a *selection* on a horizontally fragmented relation.

**Rule:** If $R = R_1 \cup R_2 \cup R_3$ (disjoint union by minterm predicates $m_1, m_2, m_3$), then:
$$\sigma_P(R) = \sigma_P(R_1) \cup \sigma_P(R_2) \cup \sigma_P(R_3)$$
If $P \wedge m_i$ is *unsatisfiable* (i.e., the selection and the fragment's minterm are mutually exclusive), then $\sigma_P(R_i) = \emptyset$ and the fragment $R_i$ is *eliminated*.

**Example:** `EMP` is fragmented as $E_1$ (dept D1), $E_2$ (dept D2), $E_3$ (dept D3). Query: `SELECT * FROM EMP WHERE DEPTNO = 'D1'`. By primary reduction, fragments $E_2$ and $E_3$ are eliminated because their minterms ($m_2, m_3$) are mutually exclusive with `DEPTNO = 'D1'`. Only $E_1$ is processed, reducing the scan cost by 67%.

**Derived Horizontal Reduction** handles the case where a query contains a *join* between two horizontally fragmented relations.

**Rule:** If $Q$ has a join $R \bowtie S$, and $R = \bigcup_i R_i$, $S = \bigcup_j S_j$, then:
$$R \bowtie S = \bigcup_{i,j} (R_i \bowtie S_j)$$
A pair $(R_i, S_j)$ is *unnecessary* (and can be eliminated) if $m_i \wedge m_j$ makes the join predicate unsatisfiable.

**Example:** `EMP` is fragmented by department, `DEPT` by location (India/UAE). The query asks for employees working in **India**. By primary reduction on `DEPT`, only the India fragment is kept. By *derived* reduction on `EMP`, only those `EMP` fragments whose `DEPTNO` values appear in the Indian `DEPT` fragment need to participate. If only D1 and D2 are Indian, then $E_3$ (dept D3) is eliminated from the join. This is the *join reduction* step, which is the foundation of **semi-join-based optimization**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Many students confuse *primary* and *derived* reduction. **Primary** = selection on a *single* fragmented relation. **Derived** = join between *two* fragmented relations.
> - Always state the *disjoint union* ($\cup$) property explicitly. Marks are awarded for the distributive law application.
> - In a 7-mark question, you MUST give a *concrete example*. Abstract explanation without an example scores ≤ 4 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a)** What is **query decomposition**? Describe in detail the **five steps** involved in transforming a high-level query into a normalized relational algebra expression. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Query Decomposition** is the first layer of distributed query processing. It transforms a high-level, declarative query (SQL) into a *canonical relational algebra expression* that is semantically equivalent, syntactically correct, and free of redundancy. The result is called the **Normalized Query (NQ)**.

**The Five Steps:**

**Step 1: Lexical and Syntactic Analysis**
The SQL string is tokenized into lexical units (keywords, identifiers, operators, literals). A parser uses the language grammar to construct a parse tree. Any syntax error is reported and processing halts.

**Step 2: Query Normalization**
The parse tree is converted into a **normalized form** using logical equivalences. Predicates are converted to **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** or **Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF)**:
$$\text{CNF: } (P_1 \vee P_2) \wedge (P_3 \vee P_4) \wedge \dots$$
Nested sub-queries are flattened using `IN`, `EXISTS`, `NOT EXISTS`, and quantified predicate equivalences. This step removes the structural complexity of the original query.

**Step 3: Semantic Analysis**
Every relation name, attribute, and operator is cross-checked against the *global conceptual schema*. Type checking is performed (e.g., comparing an integer with a string is rejected). Access rights (privileges) are verified. The result is a *semantically valid* normalized expression.

**Step 4: Simplification / Reduction**
Redundant predicates are eliminated using Boolean equivalences:
- $P \wedge P \equiv P$ (idempotence)
- $P \wedge \text{true} \equiv P$ (identity)
- $P \wedge \neg P \equiv \text{false}$ (contradiction)
- $(P \wedge Q) \vee (\neg P \wedge Q) \equiv Q$ (absorption)

**Step 5: Calculus to Algebra Translation**
The normalized relational calculus expression is rewritten into a relational algebra tree. Standard translations include:
- $\exists x \in R,\ P(x) \rightarrow \pi(\sigma_P(R))$
- $\forall x \in R,\ P(x) \rightarrow \neg(\exists x \in R,\ \neg P(x))$
- Joins replace multiple FROM-clause relations
- Set operations (UNION, INTERSECT, EXCEPT) are introduced as needed

The result is a *canonical relational algebra expression* — the **Normalized Query** — which is then passed to the data localization layer.

#### **(b)** Explain **localization of distributed data**. Discuss the **two-phase reduction** approach with the **AHY (semi-join) algorithm** for distributed join optimization. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Localization of Distributed Data** is the second layer of distributed query processing. It rewrites a query on *global relations* into an equivalent query on *physical fragments* distributed across sites. The objective is to **eliminate unnecessary fragments** from consideration, reducing both local processing and network communication costs.

**Two-Phase Reduction Approach:**

**Phase 1: Primary Horizontal Reduction**
For a query with a selection on a horizontally fragmented relation, eliminate fragments where the selection predicate is incompatible with the fragment's minterm predicate (refer to Section 3.3 for full derivation).

**Phase 2: Derived Horizontal Reduction (Join Reduction)**
For a query with a join between two horizontally fragmented relations, eliminate fragment pairs that cannot contribute to the join result (derived minterm analysis).

**AHY Algorithm (Apers, Hevner, Yao Algorithm):**

The AHY algorithm applies **semi-join reduction** to minimize the size of relations shipped across the network for distributed joins.

**Semi-Join Definition:**
$$R \ltimes_{A} S = \pi_{attrs}(R \bowtie_{A} \pi_{A}(S))$$

In other words, $R \ltimes S$ is the subset of $R$ whose join attribute values *exist* in $S$.

**Algorithm Steps for a Distributed Join $R \bowtie S$:**

1. **At Site hosting S:** Compute $S' = \pi_{join\_attr}(S)$ — the projection of $S$ onto the join attribute. This is a *small* relation.
2. **Ship $S'$ to Site hosting R.** Cost: $size(S')$.
3. **At Site hosting R:** Compute $R' = R \ltimes S'$ by joining $R$ with $S'$ and retaining only the matching tuples. This *reduces* $R$ to only the tuples that have join partners in $S$.
4. **Ship $R'$ to Site hosting S (or to a result site).** Cost: $size(R')$.
5. **At the result site:** Compute the final join $R' \bowtie S$.

**Cost Analysis (from Section 3.4):**
$$C_{semi} = size(S') + 2 \cdot size(R') + size(S)$$
$$C_{naive} = size(R) + size(S)$$

The semi-join is beneficial when $size(S') + 2 \cdot size(R') < size(R)$, which is true when the join is highly selective and the projected join attribute is much smaller than $S$.

**Recursive Application:** The AHY algorithm can be applied *iteratively* — using the result of one semi-join reduction to drive the next — until no further reduction is possible. This is captured in the SDD-1 algorithm, which extends AHY for multi-relation join queries.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - The *single biggest mistake* in semi-join questions is omitting the cost comparison. You MUST derive or at least state the condition $size(S') + 2 \cdot size(R') < size(R)$.
> - Students often confuse *semi-join* ($\ltimes$) with *anti-semi-join* ($\rhd$). A semi-join retains *only* the matching tuples; an anti-semi-join retains the *non-matching* tuples.
> - AHY is for **distributed joins on a single relation pair**. For **multi-relation joins**, the correct citation is **SDD-1** (not AHY). Markers deduct 1 mark for this error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Query Processing & Decomposition)**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ **Distributed Query Processing** = 4-layer pipeline: Decomposition → Localization → Global Optimization → Local Optimization.
- ✅ **Three Objectives:** Minimize response time, minimize total cost, maximize throughput.
- ✅ **Communication cost** is the *new dimension* introduced by distribution (CPU + I/O + MSG).

### Query Decomposition
- ✅ Five steps: Lexical/Syntactic Analysis → Normalization → Semantic Analysis → Simplification → Calculus→Algebra.
- ✅ Output: **Normalized Query (NQ)** in canonical relational algebra form.
- ✅ Uses CNF/DNF normalization and Boolean equivalences (idempotence, absorption, contradiction).

### Localization of Distributed Data
- ✅ **Primary Horizontal Reduction:** $\sigma_P(R) = \bigcup_i \sigma_P(R_i)$; eliminate $R_i$ if $P \wedge m_i$ is unsatisfiable.
- ✅ **Derived Horizontal Reduction (Join Reduction):** Eliminate $(R_i, S_j)$ pairs where $m_i \wedge m_j$ makes the join predicate unsatisfiable.
- ✅ **Vertical Reduction:** Generally all vertical fragments are required for reconstruction via the primary key.

### Cost & Optimization Formulas
- ✅ $C_{total} = C_{CPU} + C_{I/O} + C_{MSG}$
- ✅ $C_{MSG}(R) = C_0 + size(R) \cdot C_1$
- ✅ Semi-join benefit condition: $size(S') + 2 \cdot size(R') < size(R)$
- ✅ Join cardinality: $card(R \bowtie S) = \frac{card(R) \cdot card(S)}{\max(V(R.A), V(S.A))}$

### Algorithm Names
- ✅ **AHY Algorithm** — Semi-join reduction for distributed join (2 relations).
- ✅ **SDD-1 Algorithm** — Semi-join reduction extended to multi-relation queries.
- ✅ **Iterative Improvement** — Randomized local-search-based optimization.
- ✅ **Simulated Annealing** — Probabilistic alternative to iterative improvement.
- ✅ **Dynamic Programming with Bushy Trees** — Exhaustive search for small query sizes.

### Characterization
- ✅ **Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous** DDBMS — distinguished by uniform vs diverse local DBMSs and schemas.
- ✅ **Static vs Dynamic vs Hybrid** optimization — distinguished by when the optimization decision is made.
- ✅ **One-shot vs Modular** query processing — distinguished by granularity of optimization.

### Frequently Tested Equivalences
- ✅ Join commutativity: $R \bowtie S \equiv S \bowtie R$
- ✅ Join associativity: $(R \bowtie S) \bowtie T \equiv R \bowtie (S \bowtie T)$
- ✅ Selection push-down: $\sigma_P(R \bowtie S) \equiv \sigma_P(R) \bowtie S$ (if $P$ depends only on $R$)
- ✅ Projection push-down: $\pi_X(R \bowtie S) \equiv \pi_X(\pi_{X \cup Y}(R) \bowtie \pi_{X \cup Z}(S))$

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Tips:**
> 1. Always draw a **diagram** for 7-mark questions on the 4-layer architecture.
> 2. Always state the **Boolean equivalence rules** explicitly in normalization questions.
> 3. Always show the **distributive law** application in localization questions.
> 4. Always include a **cost comparison** in semi-join / AHY questions.
> 5. Use the **disjoint union symbol** $\cup$ (not $U$) and the **semi-join symbol** $\ltimes$ (not $\times$) for clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DTD and XML Schema, XML Presentation, XPath Queries, XQuery
NoSQL Databases: CAP Theorem, Document Based
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST634 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** NoSQL Databases: CAP Theorem, Document Based ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The CAP Theorem — Formal Definition The **CAP Theorem** (also known as **Brewer's Theorem**), formally conjectured by computer scientist **Eric Brewer** in the year **2000** and later proved in **2002** by **Seth Gilbert and Nancy Lynch** of MIT, is a foundational principle in the theory of distributed NoSQL data stores. It rigorously states that in any **distributed data system** communicating exclusively over a network that is susceptible to failures, it is mathematically impossible to simultaneously guarantee all three of the following properties: - **C — Consistency**: Every read operation received by a client returns the result of the most recent write (or a linearizable / atomic value). In simpler terms, all nodes in the distributed cluster observe the same data value at the same logical instant. - **A — Availability**: Every request issued by a client receives a (non-error) response, even if one or more nodes in the cluster have failed. The system remains operationally responsive at all times. - **P — Partition Tolerance**: The system continues to operate correctly and serve client requests despite an arbitrary number of network partitions (communication dropouts) between nodes caused by hardware faults, link failures, or routing errors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Syllabus Definition (Exact KTU Wording):** > "The CAP theorem states that a distributed computer system cannot simultaneously provide all three of the following guarantees: **Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance**. At best, a system can guarantee only two out of the three properties in the presence of a network partition." ### 1.2 Document-Based NoSQL Databases — Formal Definition A **Document-Oriented Database** (commonly abbreviated as a **Document Store**) is a category of NoSQL database engineered to store, retrieve, and manage semi-structured data as self-describing documents, typically encoded in standardized lightweight data-interchange formats such as **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)**, **BSON (Binary JSON)**, or **XML**. Each document represents a discrete logical record (analogous to a row in the relational world) and is grouped into a logical container called a **Collection** (analogous to a table). Document databases are inherently **schema-flexible**, meaning each document within the same collection may possess a distinct set of fields, with values allowed to be nested arrays, sub-documents, scalar literals, or even binary blobs. > [!NOTE] > **Reference Implementations Examined in KTU Syllabus:** *MongoDB, CouchDB, Couchbase, Amazon DocumentDB, Azure Cosmos DB (SQL API mode).* ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition #### Analogy for CAP Theorem Imagine a busy city with **three hospitals** (nodes A, B, C) sharing one patient record system. A severe storm now destroys the phone lines between hospitals B and C (this is a **network partition**). The mayor (you) must choose the lesser evil: - **Keep Consistency (CP)**: Lock out new patients until the lines are restored. No doctor at any hospital can update or even read a patient file until all hospitals agree on the latest data → accurate but **unavailable**. - **Keep Availability (AP)**: Each hospital continues treating patients and writing records independently. When the storm ends and lines reconnect, the records are reconciled (merged) → always responsive but **may have conflicting/stale records**. - **Drop Partition Tolerance (CA)**: This is impossible in real distributed systems because real networks *will* eventually partition. So in practice, distributed systems must choose between **CP and AP**. #### Analogy for Document Databases Think of a **traditional SQL table** like a rigid Excel spreadsheet where every row must have exactly the same columns (Name, Age, Address, etc.). Now imagine a **document database** like a filing cabinet where every file (document) is a *folder containing its own personalized set of papers*. One folder about a "Student" might contain marks, attendance, and address; another folder about a "Teacher" might contain salary and subject specialization.
