They live in the same cabinet (collection) but carry entirely different papers (fields). You can throw any folder in, and later you can add or remove papers freely — without redesigning the cabinet. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 CAP Theorem — The Three Properties Deep Dive #### 2.1.1 Consistency (Strong / Linearizable Consistency) - A read is guaranteed to return the value of the **most recent successful write**. - Equivalent to **linearizability** in concurrent systems theory. - Implemented typically via **quorum-based replication** (waiting for majority of replicas to acknowledge a write before returning success). - Penalizes latency because writes must propagate to multiple nodes before reads can be served. #### 2.1.2 Availability - Defined formally by Gilbert & Lynch as: "every request received by a non-failing node in the system must result in a non-error response." - The system does *not* guarantee that the response contains the most recent write. - Implemented via asynchronous replication and local reads/writes at the nearest replica. #### 2.1.3 Partition Tolerance - The defining assumption: **network partitions will occur**. In any realistically large distributed system, hardware will fail, links will drop, switches will reboot. Partition tolerance is therefore *non-negotiable* in true distributed systems. - Implication: When a partition occurs, the system must make a binary choice between Consistency (CP) and Availability (AP). ### 2.2 The Three Practical System Classes | Class | Sacrifices | Real-World Examples | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **CP Systems** | Availability during partition | **MongoDB (default), Redis, HBase, Apache ZooKeeper** | Banking ledgers, atomic counters, distributed locking | | **AP Systems** | Strong consistency during partition | **Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB, Riak, Amazon Dynamo** | Shopping carts, social feeds, session caches | | **CA Systems** *(theoretical only)* | Partition tolerance | Traditional single-node RDBMS (PostgreSQL, MySQL) running on a single server | Single-site monolithic deployments | > [!IMPORTANT] > **PACELC Extension (Post-CAP refinement by Daniel Abadi, 2010):** > Even when there is **no partition** (Else clause), the system must still trade off between **Latency (L)** and **Consistency (C)**. This refines CAP into a more nuanced model: e.g., **PA/EL** = sacrifice consistency during partitions **and** favor low latency normally — characteristic of DynamoDB. ### 2.3 Mathematical Representation of Quorum (Used in CAP-aware replication) For any replicated data store with **N total replicas**, define: - **W** = number of replicas that must acknowledge a write for it to be considered durable. - **R** = number of replicas that must respond to a read for it to return successfully. The system guarantees **strong consistency** (linearizable reads) if and only if: $$R + W > N$$ This condition ensures that the read set and write set of any operation overlap on at least one node, guaranteeing that the most recent write is observed. ### 2.4 Document Database Deep Architecture #### 2.4.1 Data Model Hierarchy ``` Database └── Collection (logical grouping, schema-less) └── Document (atomic unit of storage & retrieval) └── Field : Value ├── Scalar (string, int, bool, double, date) ├── Array [...] ├── Embedded Sub-document { ... } └── Binary blob (GridFS in MongoDB) ``` #### 2.4.2 Key Properties - **Schema-less / Flexible Schema**: No DDL constraints on the structure of documents in a collection. - **Document ID**: Each document possesses a unique primary key `_id` (typically a 12-byte ObjectId in MongoDB, comprising timestamp + machine + process counter). - **Atomic single-document operations**: Updates to a single document are ACID-compliant. Multi-document transactions require explicit transaction blocks. - **Indexing**: Secondary indexes (single-field, compound, multi-key, text, geospatial, hashed) can be created on any field, including nested array elements. - **Aggregation Pipeline**: Supports server-side data transformation through stages like `$match`, `$group`, `$project`, `$sort`, `$lookup` (SQL-like joins), and `$unwind`. #### 2.4.3 BSON vs JSON — The Encoding Choice - **JSON** (textual): Human-readable, lightweight, ~30% slower to parse natively. - **BSON** (binary): Used internally by MongoDB. Supports additional types like `ObjectId`, `Date`, `Binary`, `Decimal128`, and `int64`. Roughly **5× faster** to traverse than JSON due to length-prefixed encoding. ### 2.5 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet | # | Concept / Formula | Symbolic Representation | Boundary / Constraint | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | CAP Theorem (set form) | $C \cap A \cap P = \emptyset$ | Cannot achieve all three simultaneously | | 2 | Quorum Consistency | $R + W > N$ | Required for linearizable reads | | 3 | Read-only Quorum | $R + W \le N$ | Eventual consistency (sloppy quorum) | | 4 | Majority Quorum | $W = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ | Standard for durability | | 5 | PACELC | $(PA \vert PC) \;\&\; (EL \vert EC)$ | Else-branch covers partition-free operation | | 6 | Document Size Limit (MongoDB) | $16 \;\text{MB}$ | Nesting depth limit: 100 levels | | 7 | BSON Encoding Speedup | $\approx 5 \times$ | Relative to textual JSON parsing | | 8 | Storage Overhead (BSON) | $\approx 30\text{–}50\%$ | Compared to equivalent JSON text | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **CAP awareness** drives architects to choose the correct database class for mission-critical workloads: e.g., a payment ledger in **CP-Cassandra with strict quorum** vs. an analytics recommendation engine in **AP-Cassandra with eventual consistency**. - **Document stores** are the *de facto* standard for **microservices persistence**, **content management**, **catalog data**, **IoT telemetry**, and **mobile app backends** — wherever the data shape evolves rapidly. - Used extensively in **MERN / MEAN stacks** (MongoDB + Express + React/Angular + Node.js). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Proof Sketch of the CAP Impossibility We demonstrate why a distributed system cannot simultaneously provide **Consistency**, **Availability**, and **Partition tolerance**. **Setup**: Assume two nodes $G_1$ and $G_2$ that share a single replicated data item $x$ with an initial value $v_0$. **Step 1** — Assume a network partition occurs. The communication link between $G_1$ and $G_2$ is severed. **Step 2** — A client $C_1$ connected to $G_1$ issues a write request $w_1$ that updates $x = v_1$. Because the link is broken, $G_1$ **cannot propagate** this update to $G_2$. Under the **Availability** guarantee, $G_1$ must still acknowledge the write to $C_1$. **Step 3** — Simultaneously, a client $C_2$ connected to $G_2$ issues a read request $r_2$ on $x$. Under the **Availability** guarantee, $G_2$ must respond. But $G_2$ never received $w_1$, so $G_2$ responds with the stale value $v_0$. **Step 4** — This response from $G_2$ violates the **Consistency** requirement, which mandates that every read observe the result of the most recent write $v_1$. **Conclusion**: The three guarantees cannot co-exist when a partition is present. $$ \text{If } P \text{ holds} \implies \lnot(C \land A) \implies (C \oplus A) $$ Hence, the system designer must choose exactly one of the two: $$ \text{CP (consistency-favoring)} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{AP (availability-favoring)} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Quorum Condition **Goal**: Determine the relationship between $R$, $W$, and $N$ that guarantees read-after-write consistency. **Step 1** — The system has $N$ replicas of a value. A write commits only after $W$ replicas acknowledge. **Step 2** — A read is satisfied by querying any $R$ replicas. **Step 3** — For the most recent write to be guaranteed to overlap with the read set, the read quorum must intersect the write quorum by at least one node. **Step 4** — The maximum possible union size of two disjoint sets of sizes $R$ and $W$ is $R + W$. For an intersection of size $\ge 1$, we require $R + W \le N$. **Step 5** — Equivalently, the disjointness condition fails (i.e., overlap is forced) when: $$ R + W > N $$ **Verification** — Common configurations: | N | W | R | R + W > N ? | Property | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 3 | 2 | 2 | $4 > 3$ ✓ | Strong consistency | | 3 | 1 | 1 | $2 > 3$ ✗ | Eventual consistency | | 5 | 3 | 3 | $6 > 5$ ✓ | Strong consistency | | 5 | 2 | 2 | $4 > 5$ ✗ | Sloppy quorum | ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: CP vs AP Systems | Dimension | CP System | AP System | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Behavior under partition** | Refuses writes to maintain consistency | Continues both reads and writes | | **Conflict resolution** | Avoids conflicts by rejecting minority-side writes | Resolves conflicts via last-write-wins (LWW) or vector clocks | | **Latency** | Higher (synchronous replication) | Lower (local writes) | | **Client experience during partition** | Errors/timeouts | Stale but successful responses | | **Recovery post-partition** | Trivial (no divergent states) | Requires anti-entropy / Merkle tree sync | | **Example** | MongoDB (w:majority), HBase | Cassandra (CL:ONE), DynamoDB | | **Suitable for** | Stock trading, leaderboards, booking systems | Social media feeds, shopping carts, IoT | ### 3.4 Document Write Atomicity — Worked Example Consider a MongoDB collection `students`. Insert two documents with divergent schemas into the **same collection**: ```json { "_id": "S001", "name": "Anu", "cgpa": 9.1, "skills": ["Java", "Python"] } { "_id": "S002", "name": "Ravi", "age": 21, "hosteller": true } ``` - Both documents succeed in insertion despite different fields → **schema flexibility demonstrated**. - Each individual write is **atomic at the document level**. - A multi-document transaction (across `S001` and `S002`) requires an explicit `session.startTransaction()` block in modern MongoDB (≥ 4.0). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CAP Theorem Triangle — System Classification View ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef cpNode fill:#f9c0c0,stroke:#a02020,stroke-width:2px,color:#5a0000 classDef apNode fill:#c0e0f9,stroke:#2050a0,stroke-width:2px,color:#001a4a classDef caNode fill:#d8d8d8,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:2px,color:#222222 classDef centralNode fill:#fff4c0,stroke:#a07000,stroke-width:3px,color:#3d2a00 central["CAP TRIANGLE
Any Distributed System
During Network Partition"] central:::centralNode cp["CP CLASS
Consistency + Partition
Sacrifice: Availability
Examples: MongoDB, HBase,
ZooKeeper, etcd"] cp:::cpNode ap["AP CLASS
Availability + Partition
Sacrifice: Consistency
Examples: Cassandra,
CouchDB, DynamoDB, Riak"] ap:::apNode ca["CA CLASS
Consistency + Availability
Sacrifice: Partition Tolerance
Examples: Single-node RDBMS,
Traditional SQL Servers
(Not Truly Distributed)"] ca:::caNode central --> cp central --> ap central --> ca cp -.->|Mutually
Exclusive| ap ap -.->|Mutually
Exclusive| cp ``` ### 4.2 Document Database Architecture — Functional Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef clientNode fill:#dff5d6,stroke:#3a7d20,stroke-width:2px,color:#0e2a00 classDef driverNode fill:#fde2c0,stroke:#a05500,stroke-width:2px,color:#3a1d00 classDef serverNode fill:#c0dff9,stroke:#205080,stroke-width:2px,color:#001a3a classDef storeNode fill:#e0c0f9,stroke:#502080,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a0040 classDef engineNode fill:#f9c0d8,stroke:#a02050,stroke-width:2px,color:#3a0014 cli1[Web App Client]:::clientNode cli2[Mobile App Client]:::clientNode cli3[Analytics Service]:::clientNode drv[MongoDB Driver
BSON Encoder/Decoder]:::driverNode srv[MongoDB Server mongod
Query Parser + Planner]:::serverNode aggr[Aggregation Pipeline
match, group, project, lookup]:::engineNode idx[Index Engine
B-Tree, Text, Geospatial, Hashed]:::engineNode coll1[Collection students]:::storeNode coll2[Collection courses]:::storeNode coll3[Collection orders]:::storeNode fs[(GridFS
Large File Store)]:::storeNode repl[Replication Set
Primary, Secondary, Secondary]:::engineNode cli1 --> drv cli2 --> drv cli3 --> drv drv --> srv srv --> aggr srv --> idx aggr --> coll1 aggr --> coll2 aggr --> coll3 idx --> coll1 idx --> coll2 idx --> coll3 srv --> fs coll1 -.Replicates.-> repl coll2 -.Replicates.-> repl coll3 -.Replicates.-> repl ``` ### 4.3 CAP Trade-off Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef dec fill:#fff4c0,stroke:#a07000,stroke-width:2px,color:#3a2700 classDef branch fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#205080,stroke-width:2px,color:#001a3a classDef outCP fill:#f9c0c0,stroke:#a02020,stroke-width:2px,color:#5a0000 classDef outAP fill:#c0e0c8,stroke:#206030,stroke-width:2px,color:#001a00 q1{Does your application
require zero data loss
and immediate consistency?}:::dec q2{Are network partitions
expected frequently?}:::dec cp[Choose CP System
MongoDB, HBase, ZooKeeper
Strong consistency
Possible unavailability during partition]:::outCP ap[Choose AP System
Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB
Always responsive
Eventual consistency / stale reads]:::outAP single[Consider CA Single-Node
PostgreSQL, MySQL on one host
Not truly distributed]:::branch q1 -- Yes --> q2 q1 -- No --> ap q2 -- Yes --> cp q2 -- No --> single ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *State and explain the CAP theorem. Why is it relevant to distributed NoSQL systems?* **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The **CAP theorem**, formulated by **Eric Brewer (2000)** and proved by **Gilbert and Lynch (2002)**, states that any distributed data store can simultaneously guarantee **only two out of three** of the following properties in the presence of a network partition: - **Consistency (C)**: Every read returns the most recent write. - **Availability (A)**: Every request receives a non-error response. - **Partition Tolerance (P)**: The system continues to operate despite network failures. It is relevant to distributed NoSQL systems because such systems are designed for horizontal scalability across commodity clusters, where **network partitions are inevitable**. Designers must therefore make a conscious trade-off, choosing either **CP** (e.g., MongoDB) or **AP** (e.g., Cassandra) configurations based on application requirements such as financial accuracy vs. continuous user availability. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Equivalent]** *What is a document-oriented database? Mention any two advantages.* **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** A **document-oriented database** is a NoSQL data store that organizes information as **self-describing, semi-structured documents** (commonly encoded in **JSON or BSON**) grouped into **collections**. Each document contains a unique `_id` and a flexible set of key-value fields, with no rigid schema enforced across documents. **Two advantages:** 1. **Schema flexibility**: New fields can be added to individual documents without altering the entire collection, accelerating agile development and evolving data models. 2. **Natural mapping to object-oriented programming**: Documents map directly to objects in languages like JavaScript, Python, and Java, eliminating complex ORM impedance mismatch. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### ✅ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **CAP theorem** in detail. Describe the three system classifications (CP, AP, CA) with one real-world NoSQL example for each. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 Mark):** The CAP theorem, conjectured by Eric Brewer at the ACM Symposium on Principles of Distributed Computing in 2000 and formally proven by Gilbert and Lynch at MIT in 2002, is a cornerstone result in distributed systems theory. It asserts that a distributed data store cannot simultaneously offer all three guarantees — **Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance** — when a network partition occurs. Since partitions are an unavoidable fact of distributed computing, designers must choose between C and A. **CP Systems (2 Marks):** Consistency + Partition tolerance. During a partition, the system refuses writes (and sometimes reads) on the minority side to maintain strict linearizability. Once the partition heals, the cluster resumes normal operation. - *Example*: **MongoDB** (with `w: "majority"` write concern) — primary node accepts writes only after majority replication; secondaries may return errors during partitions. - *Example*: **HBase** — uses ZooKeeper for coordination; minority-side region servers reject writes to preserve strong consistency. **AP Systems (2 Marks):** Availability + Partition tolerance. During a partition, both sides continue serving requests, accepting writes locally. When the partition heals, conflicting writes are reconciled using last-write-wins (LWW), vector clocks, or application-defined merge logic. - *Example*: **Apache Cassandra** — tunable consistency with eventual consistency as the default; both sides accept writes with Hinted Handoff. - *Example*: **Amazon DynamoDB** — uses vector clocks and Sloppy Quorum; supports "always write, never block" semantics ideal for e-commerce carts. **CA Systems (2 Marks):** Consistency + Availability without partition tolerance. This class is theoretically possible only when there are no partitions — meaning the system must run on a single machine or use perfectly reliable synchronous links. Real-world examples are therefore **not truly distributed**. - *Example*: A **single-node PostgreSQL** or **MySQL** deployment with synchronous replication over a perfectly reliable LAN. --- **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a document-oriented database**. Discuss the role of collections, documents, BSON encoding, and the aggregation pipeline. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Architecture Overview (2 Marks):** A document database such as MongoDB is built on a layered architecture: clients connect via a language-specific **driver** (BSON encoder/decoder), which forwards queries to the **mongod server process**. The server parses the query, consults the **query planner**, optionally invokes the **aggregation pipeline**, and resolves the result using the **index engine** (typically B-Tree, with specialized text, geospatial, and hashed variants). Documents are persisted in **collections** within a logical **database**. Replication is achieved through a **replica set** of one primary and multiple secondary nodes. **Collections and Documents (2 Marks):** A *collection* is a schema-less container analogous to a table. A *document* is the atomic unit of storage — a BSON object identified by a unique `_id`. Documents support nested arrays, sub-documents, and diverse scalar types (int, double, decimal128, date, ObjectId). Atomicity is guaranteed at the single-document level. **BSON Encoding (1 Mark):** BSON is a binary-encoded, length-prefixed superset of JSON. It adds support for non-JSON-native types like `ObjectId`, `Date`, and `Binary`, and offers roughly 5× faster traversal than textual JSON due to its binary structure. Each BSON document has a maximum size of **16 MB** in MongoDB. **Aggregation Pipeline (2 Marks):** Server-side data processing occurs through a pipeline of stages. The `$match` stage filters documents; `$group` performs aggregations; `$project` reshapes documents; `$sort` orders results; `$lookup` performs left-outer joins with other collections (introducing SQL-like relational capability); `$unwind` deconstructs array fields. The pipeline processes documents in memory with optional disk spill for large workloads. --- #### ✅ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Supplementary Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Compare **document databases** with **relational databases** across at least five dimensions. Justify where each is preferable with examples. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** | Dimension | Document Database (e.g., MongoDB) | Relational Database (e.g., PostgreSQL) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Data Model** | Semi-structured BSON/JSON documents, nested | Flat rows in tables with rigid schema | | **Schema** | Schema-flexible; each document may differ | Schema-rigid; enforced via DDL | | **Scaling** | Horizontal (sharding native to the engine) | Primarily vertical; sharding is complex | | **Transactions** | Single-document atomic; multi-doc transactions added later | Full ACID across multiple tables | | **Query Language** | MQL (MongoDB Query Language) + aggregation pipeline | SQL (mature, ANSI-standardized) | | **Joins** | `$lookup` (limited, slower for deep joins) | Native, highly optimized via query planner | | **Use Case Fit** | Catalogs, content management, IoT, mobile apps | Banking ledgers, ERP, OLAP reporting | **Justification (2 Marks):** - Choose **document** when the data model evolves rapidly (e.g., a product catalog where each category has different attributes like phones vs. shoes). - Choose **relational** when referential integrity and complex analytical joins dominate (e.g., financial transaction processing with strict ACID requirements across multiple accounts). **Storage example (1 Mark):** In MongoDB a single product document can embed its reviews as a sub-array: ```json { "_id": "P100", "name": "Smartphone X", "price": 49999, "reviews": [ { "user": "Anu", "rating": 5, "comment": "Excellent!" }, { "user": "Ravi", "rating": 4, "comment": "Good value" } ] } ``` This eliminates the need for a separate `reviews` table and a foreign-key join. --- **(b)** Explain the **PACELC extension** of the CAP theorem. Why is it considered a more refined model? Give two example systems and classify them. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **PACELC — Definition (3 Marks):** Proposed by **Daniel Abadi of Yale University in 2010**, PACELC extends CAP by adding an **Else (E)** branch that addresses system behavior *when there is no network partition*. The full acronym decodes as: - **P** — if Partition occurs - **A** — then choose Availability - **C** — then choose Consistency - **E** — Else (no partition) - **L** — favor Latency (low-latency reads/writes) - **C** — favor Consistency (linearizable reads) So a system's classification is a pair: e.g., **PA/EL** means *during partitions favor availability, otherwise favor low latency*. **Why it is more refined (2 Marks):** CAP only specifies behavior during partitions — but in production systems, partitions are rare and transient (most of the time, the network is healthy). PACELC captures the always-on latency-vs-consistency trade-off that exists even in normal operation. This helps designers reason about everyday performance characteristics, not just disaster scenarios. **Example Classifications (2 Marks):** | System | PACELC Class | Justification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Amazon DynamoDB** | **PA/EL** | Sacrifices consistency during partition (uses vector clocks, sloppy quorum); always prioritizes low latency with single-digit-millisecond reads | | **HBase** | **PC/EC** | Refuses writes during partition to maintain consistency; also favors consistency over latency in normal operation due to ZooKeeper coordination | | **MongoDB (with `w:majority`)** | **PC/EL** | Prefers consistency during partition; otherwise optimized for low latency | | **Cassandra (default CL:ONE)** | **PA/EL** | Always-available design; tunable but defaults to latency-favoring | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:** > 1. **Misstating CAP**: Students often write "you can have any 2 of 3 at all times." The correct phrasing is "*during a network partition*, only 2 can coexist." Partition tolerance is non-negotiable in distributed systems, so the practical choice is **CP vs AP**. > 2. **Confusing Consistency models**: Strong (linearizable) consistency ≠ eventual consistency. CAP "C" specifically refers to **linearizable / atomic** consistency. > 3. **MongoDB misclassification**: MongoDB is *configurable*. Default settings lean toward CP (with `w:majority`), but with `w:1` it behaves like an AP system. Always specify the configuration. > 4. **Forgetting `PACELC`**: KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly includes PACELC in the syllabus. Writing only CAP without mentioning PACELC will cost 2–3 marks. > 5. **Mixing document and columnar databases**: Document stores (MongoDB) ≠ Columnar stores (HBase, Cassandra). Do not interchange examples in answers. > 6. **Schemaless ≠ Schema-less thinking**: Although collections are schema-flexible, applications should still enforce validation through JSON Schema validators to maintain data quality. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ The **CAP theorem** was proposed by **Eric Brewer (2000)** and proved by **Gilbert & Lynch (2002)**. - ✅ The three properties are **Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance** — only **two can coexist during a partition**. - ✅ **CP examples**: MongoDB (with majority writes), HBase, ZooKeeper, etcd, Redis Cluster. - ✅ **AP examples**: Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB, Riak, Voldemort. - ✅ **CA** is theoretical — only achievable in single-node systems, not truly distributed. - ✅ **PACELC** (Abadi, 2010) extends CAP by addressing behavior in the **Else (no partition)** branch, capturing the latency-vs-consistency trade-off. - ✅ The **quorum formula** for strong consistency is **R + W > N**. - ✅ **Document databases** store data as self-describing JSON/BSON documents grouped in collections. - ✅ **MongoDB** is the canonical document store — uses BSON, supports secondary indexes, aggregation pipelines, GridFS for large files. - ✅ Document size limit in MongoDB: **16 MB**; maximum nesting depth: **100**. - ✅ Documents support **nested arrays**, **sub-documents**, and **diverse BSON types** including ObjectId and Date. - ✅ Single-document operations are ACID; multi-document transactions require explicit session blocks (MongoDB ≥ 4.0). - ✅ **Schema flexibility** accelerates agile development but should be governed by application-level JSON Schema validation. - ✅ Document databases map naturally to **object-oriented languages**, eliminating ORM impedance. - ✅ The **aggregation pipeline** enables server-side data transformation with `$match`, `$group`, `$project`, `$lookup`, `$unwind`, `$sort`. - ✅ Document databases are widely used in **microservices**, **mobile backends**, **content management**, **IoT telemetry**, and **MERN/MEAN stacks**. - ✅ Trade-off awareness: CP for financial/banking accuracy; AP for social feeds, shopping carts, IoT, and analytics workloads.

topics.title: MongoDB Operation: Insert, Update, Delete, Query, Indexing, Application, Replication, Sharding, Deployment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST634 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** MongoDB Operation: Insert, Update, Delete, Query, Indexing, Application, Replication, Sharding, Deployment ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is MongoDB? **MongoDB** is an open-source, document-oriented **NoSQL (Not Only SQL) database** designed for high availability, scalability, and performance. It stores data in flexible, **JSON-like documents** called **BSON (Binary JSON)**, where each document can have a different structure within the same collection. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** > MongoDB is a cross-platform, document-oriented NoSQL database that uses dynamic schemas, stores data in BSON format, and provides high performance, high availability, and easy scalability. It is classified as a *non-relational* database belonging to the **document store** family under the NoSQL paradigm. ### 1.2 Key Terminology Mapping (RDBMS ↔ MongoDB) | RDBMS Concept | MongoDB Equivalent | Description | |---|---|---| | Database | Database | Physical container of collections/tables | | Table | **Collection** | Group of MongoDB documents | | Row / Record | **Document** | A single BSON record | | Column | **Field** | A key-value pair in a document | | Primary Key | **_id** field | Unique document identifier (auto-generated ObjectId) | | JOIN | **Embedded documents / $lookup** | No native joins, uses denormalization | | Schema | **Dynamic Schema** | No fixed structure enforced | > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Distinction:** Unlike SQL databases which enforce a rigid schema, MongoDB allows **schema-less** storage. A collection can contain documents with completely different fields — this is called **polymorphic schema** behavior. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Magic Filing Cabinet" Imagine a traditional office: - **SQL Database** = A rigid spreadsheet with fixed columns. To add a new data type, you must redesign the whole table. - **MongoDB** = A **flexible folder system** where each folder (document) can contain any combination of papers (fields). One folder might have 3 papers, another might have 15, and a third might have entirely different papers — all stored together in the same drawer (collection). This is why MongoDB is favored for: - **Big Data** applications - **Real-time analytics** - **Content management systems** - **IoT** and **mobile** backends - **Social networks** (variable user attributes) > [!TIP] > **Engineering Context:** Companies like Uber, Facebook (Meta), eBay, and Adobe use MongoDB to handle millions of writes per second with horizontal scaling via **sharding**. ### 1.4 The BSON Document Format A MongoDB document is encoded as **BSON (Binary JSON)** — a binary representation of JSON-like structures. BSON extends JSON with additional data types such as `int`, `long`, `double`, `Date`, `ObjectId`, and `Binary`. **Example BSON Document:** ```json { "_id": ObjectId("507f1f77bcf86cd799439011"), "student_name": "Anjali Krishnan", "register_no": "KTU2024CS142", "cgpa": 8.7, "skills": ["MongoDB", "Python", "React"], "address": { "district": "Thrissur", "pincode": 680001 }, "enrolled_on": ISODate("2024-08-15T00:00:00Z") } ``` > [!WARNING] > **Maximum BSON document size = 16 MB**. This is a hard limit enforced by MongoDB to prevent excessive memory use and ensure single-document atomicity. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 MongoDB CRUD Operations — The Core **CRUD** stands for **Create, Read, Update, Delete** — the four fundamental persistent storage operations. ### 2.2 INSERT Operation The `insert` operation adds new documents to a collection. **Why it matters:** Unlike SQL where `INSERT` strictly enforces schema, MongoDB's insert is **schema-flexible**. #### 2.2.1 Methods of Insertion | Method | Syntax Pattern | Use Case | |---|---|---| | `insertOne()` | `db.collection.insertOne(document)` | Insert a single document | | `insertMany()` | `db.collection.insertMany([doc1, doc2, ...])` | Bulk insert in single call | | `insert()` (legacy) | `db.collection.insert()` | Deprecated; avoid in new code | #### 2.2.2 Insert Behavior — Ordered vs Unordered - **Ordered (default = true):** Stops on first error. Atomicity maintained per document. - **Unordered (`ordered: false`):** Continues inserting remaining documents even after an error. $$ \text{Behavior} = \begin{cases} \text{Halt on error} & \text{if } ordered = true \\ \text{Skip error doc, continue} & \text{if } ordered = false \end{cases} $$ #### 2.2.3 The `_id` Field If `_id` is not provided, MongoDB auto-generates a **12-byte ObjectId**: $$ \text{ObjectId} = \underbrace{4\text{B Timestamp}}_{\text{seconds since epoch}} + \underbrace{5\text{B Random Value}}_{\text{machine/process}} + \underbrace{3\text{B Counter}_{\text{incrementing}}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **12 bytes = 96 bits.** The ObjectId is unique across distributed systems, making it ideal for sharded environments. ### 2.3 READ / QUERY Operation #### 2.3.1 Query Methods | Method | Returns | Description | |---|---|---| | `find()` | Cursor | Returns matching documents | | `findOne()` | Document | Returns first match only | | `find().pretty()` | Cursor | Formats output for readability | #### 2.3.2 Query Selectors (Operators) | Operator | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | `$eq` | Equal to | `{cgpa: {$eq: 8.5}}` | | `$ne` | Not equal | `{district: {$ne: "Kochi"}}` | | `$gt`, `$gte` | Greater than [or equal] | `{age: {$gt: 20}}` | | `$lt`, `$lte` | Less than [or equal] | `{age: {$lte: 30}}` | | `$in` | Matches any value in array | `{branch: {$in: ["CS","IT"]}}` | | `$nin` | Not in array | — | | `$and`, `$or`, `$not`, `$nor` | Logical | `{$or: [...]}` | | `$exists` | Field existence check | `{phone: {$exists: true}}` | | `$regex` | Pattern matching | `{name: {$regex: "^Ktu"}}` | | `$all` | All array elements match | `{skills: {$all: ["MongoDB"]}}` | | `$elemMatch` | Match in array of subdocs | `{grades: {$elemMatch: {score: {$gt: 80}}}}` | #### 2.3.3 Projection Used to limit fields returned: ``` db.students.find({cgpa: {$gt: 8.0}}, {name: 1, cgpa: 1, _id: 0}) ``` #### 2.3.4 Cursor Methods (Chaining) | Method | Purpose | |---|---| | `.sort({field: 1})` | Sort: `1` = ascending, `-1` = descending | | `.limit(n)` | Restrict to first n documents | | `.skip(n)` | Skip first n documents | | `.count()` | Count matches (deprecated in favor of `countDocuments`) | | `.explain("executionStats")` | Analyze query plan | ### 2.4 UPDATE Operation #### 2.4.1 Update Methods | Method | Use Case | |---|---| | `updateOne()` | Modify first matching document | | `updateMany()` | Modify all matching documents | | `replaceOne()` | Replace entire document (except `_id`) | | `findOneAndUpdate()` | Atomic find + update, returns document | | `save()` (legacy) | Upsert based on `_id` | #### 2.4.2 Update Operators | Operator | Function | Example | |---|---|---| | `$set` | Set field value | `{$set: {cgpa: 9.0}}` | | `$unset` | Remove a field | `{$unset: {phone: ""}}` | | `$inc` | Increment numeric value | `{$inc: {attempts: 1}}` | | `$mul` | Multiply value | `{$mul: {price: 1.05}}` | | `$rename` | Rename a field | `{$rename: {"name": "full_name"}}` | | `$push` | Append to array | `{$push: {skills: "Docker"}}` | | `$pull` | Remove from array | `{$pull: {skills: "Java"}}` | | `$addToSet` | Add if not present | `{$addToSet: {tags: "urgent"}}` | | `$pop` | Remove first/last array | `{$pop: {queue: 1}}` | #### 2.4.3 Upsert Option `upsert: true` → Inserts a new document if no match is found. $$ \text{Update} = \begin{cases} \text{Modify existing} & \text{if match found} \\ \text{Insert new document} & \text{if no match} \end{cases} \quad \text{(when } upsert=true\text{)} $$ ### 2.5 DELETE Operation | Method | Description | |---|---| | `deleteOne()` | Removes first matching document | | `deleteMany()` | Removes all matching documents | | `findOneAndDelete()` | Atomic find + delete, returns deleted doc | | `remove()` (legacy) | Deprecated | > [!WARNING] > In MongoDB, **delete operations are not transactional across multiple documents** (without explicit transactions). Always back up data before bulk deletes. ### 2.6 INDEXING Indexes are special data structures (B-Tree by default) that store a small portion of the collection's data in an easy-to-traverse form. #### 2.6.1 Why Index? Without an index, MongoDB performs a **collection scan** — checks every document. With an index, it uses a **bounded scan** on a sorted structure. #### 2.6.2 Index Types | Type | Description | |---|---| | Single Field | Index on one field | | Compound | Multi-field index: `{name:1, age:-1}` | | Multikey | Index on array fields | | Text | Full-text search | | Geospatial (2dsphere / 2d) | Location queries | | Hashed | Hash-based (used in sharding) | | TTL | Auto-expire documents after time | | Unique | Enforce uniqueness | | Sparse | Only documents containing the field | | Partial | Index only documents matching filter | #### 2.6.3 Indexing Formula For a query on field `f`, the time complexity reduces from: $$ T_{no\_index} = O(n) \quad \text{(linear collection scan)} $$ $$ T_{with\_index} = O(\log n) \quad \text{(B-Tree traversal)} $$ > [!NOTE] > Indexes **speed up reads** but **slow down writes** because every index must be updated. Rule of thumb: index selectively. ### 2.7 High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---| | ObjectId size | 12 bytes (96 bits) | 4B + 5B + 3B | | Max document size | **16 MB** | Hard limit | | Max BSON nesting | 100 levels | BSON depth limit | | Index complexity | $O(\log n)$ | B-Tree | | Collection scan | $O(n)$ | No index | | Shard key | Must be indexed field | Required for sharding | | Replica set | Min **3** members | For fault tolerance | | WriteConcern majority | $w = \text{"majority"}$ | Acknowledgment rule | | TTL purge | Background thread every **60s** | Expired doc removal | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What? | Requirement | Use | |---|---| | Add a single record | `insertOne()` | | Add 10,000 records efficiently | `insertMany()` with `ordered: false` | | Find one user by email | `findOne({email: "x@y.com"})` | | Range search on age | `find({age: {$gte: 20, $lte: 30}})` | | Increment a counter | `updateOne({...}, {$inc: {count: 1}})` | | Atomic find-and-modify | `findOneAndUpdate()` | | Delete with confirmation | `findOneAndDelete()` | | Speed up frequent search | Create index on the field | | Distribute load across servers | Enable sharding | | High availability | Configure replica set | ### 3.2 Replication — Detailed Walkthrough **Replication** = Maintaining multiple copies of data across servers for redundancy. #### 3.2.1 Replica Set Architecture A **replica set** is a group of `mongod` instances that maintain the same dataset. **Components:** - **Primary (P):** Receives all write operations. Only one per replica set. - **Secondary (S):** Replicates the primary's oplog. Can serve reads (with read preference). - **Arbiter (A):** Participates in elections but holds no data. #### 3.2.2 Replication Process (Step-by-Step Derivation) 1. Client sends write to **Primary**. 2. Primary writes to its local oplog (operations log). 3. Secondaries continuously **poll** the primary's oplog. 4. Each Secondary applies the operations to its own data set. 5. On Primary failure → **Election** triggered. 6. Secondary with highest priority + freshest oplog becomes new Primary. **Election Algorithm:** $$ \text{New Primary} = \arg\max_{i \in S} \left( \text{Priority}_i + \text{Optime Freshness}_i \right) $$ #### 3.2.3 Automatic Failover Conditions - Heartbeat failure for **10 seconds** (default). - Requires majority of voting members to elect new primary. - **Quorum rule:** $$ \text{Election Possible} \iff \text{Available Members} \geq \frac{N+1}{2} $$ where $N$ = total voting members. **Example derivation:** For $N = 3$ (1 Primary + 2 Secondaries): $$ \text{Quorum} = \lceil 3/2 \rceil = 2 \text{ members needed} $$ If 1 Secondary fails, **2 remain** → Election can proceed. If both Secondaries fail, **only 1 remains** → **No majority** → No election. Read-only mode. ### 3.3 Sharding — Detailed Walkthrough **Sharding** = Horizontal partitioning of data across multiple machines. #### 3.3.1 Why Sharding? $$ \text{Single Server Limit} \approx \text{RAM} \propto \text{Working Set Size} $$ When dataset exceeds RAM, disk I/O becomes the bottleneck. Sharding distributes: - **Read load** across multiple shards - **Write load** across multiple shards - **Storage** across multiple shards #### 3.3.2 Sharded Cluster Components | Component | Role | |---|---| | **Shard** | Stores subset of data (can be a replica set itself) | | **Config Server** | Stores cluster metadata and chunk mappings | | **mongos (Query Router)** | Routes client requests to correct shard(s) | #### 3.3.3 Shard Key Selection The **shard key** is an indexed field (or compound) used to partition data. **Three partitioning strategies:** 1. **Range-based sharding:** $$ \text{Chunk} = [\text{min}, \text{max}) \text{ on shard key} $$ Best for range queries. Risk: **hotspots** if data is skewed. 2. **Hash-based sharding:** $$ h = \text{Hash}(\text{shard key}) \mod N_{shards} $$ Even distribution; poor for range queries. 3. **Zone sharding (Tag-aware):** Custom rules to pin data to specific geographic regions. #### 3.3.4 Chunk Splitting & Balancing - Each **chunk** ≈ **64 MB** (default). - When a chunk exceeds the size limit → **split**. - **Balancer** migrates chunks between shards to equalize count. $$ \text{Target per Shard} = \frac{\text{Total Chunks}}{\text{Number of Shards}} $$ #### 3.3.5 Example Calculation Suppose a cluster has **3 shards** and **150 chunks**. $$ \text{Chunks per Shard} = \frac{150}{3} = 50 $$ If Shard 1 has 60 chunks → Balancer migrates 10 chunks to Shard 2 and Shard 3 to restore balance. ### 3.4 Application Integration #### 3.4.1 Connection Methods - **MongoDB Shell (`mongosh`)** — interactive REPL. - **Drivers (Official):** Node.js, Python (PyMongo), Java, C#, Go, PHP, Ruby, etc. - **ODM (Object-Document Mapper):** Mongoose (Node.js), MongoEngine (Python). - **BI Connector** — for SQL-based tools (Tableau, Power BI). #### 3.4.2 Connection String Format $$ \text{mongodb} + \text{://} + \text{[username:password@]} + \text{host}[:\text{port}] + \text{/database} $$ **Example with replica set:** ``` mongodb://user:pass@host1:27017,host2:27017,host3:27017/mydb?replicaSet=rs0 ``` #### 3.4.3 Aggregation Pipeline MongoDB's powerful data processing framework: $$ \text{Pipeline} = \text{stage}_1 \rightarrow \text{stage}_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{stage}_n $$ **Common stages:** `$match`, `$group`, `$project`, `$sort`, `$limit`, `$lookup`, `$unwind`, `$bucket`. **Example analytical question:** > *Find the top 3 districts by average CGPA:* ``` db.students.aggregate([ { $match: { cgpa: { $gte: 7.0 } } }, { $group: { _id: "$district", avgCGPA: { $avg: "$cgpa" } } }, { $sort: { avgCGPA: -1 } }, { $limit: 3 } ]) ``` ### 3.5 Deployment Architectures | Architecture | Description | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Standalone** | Single `mongod` instance | Development, testing | | **Replica Set** | 1 Primary + 2 Secondaries (min) | Production with HA | | **Sharded Cluster** | Multiple shards + config + mongos | Massive scale, big data | | **MongoDB Atlas** | Fully managed cloud service | AWS / Azure / GCP | | **On-Premises** | Self-hosted on local servers | Regulatory / data residency | | **Hybrid** | Atlas + on-prem | Multi-cloud strategy | #### 3.5.1 Production Deployment Checklist - Replica set with **odd number of voting members** (3, 5, 7). - Configure **WriteConcern** = `majority` for durability. - Configure **ReadConcern** = `majority` for consistency. - Enable **authentication** (SCRAM, x.509, LDAP/Kerberos). - Enable **TLS/SSL** for encryption in transit. - **Backup** strategy: `mongodump`, snapshots, or Atlas continuous backup. - Monitor with **Ops Manager**, **Prometheus**, or **Cloud Manager**. ### 3.6 Aggregation Pipeline Formula (Conceptual) The pipeline applies each stage to the document stream: $$ D_0 \xrightarrow{\text{stage}_1} D_1 \xrightarrow{\text{stage}_2} D_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow D_n $$ **Equi-join via `$lookup` (limited to single-stage joins in MongoDB 4.x/5.x, multi-stage in 6.0+):** $$ \text{Output Doc} = \text{Left Doc} \cup \left\{ \text{as}: [\text{Matched Right Docs}] \right\} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Replica Set Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Client1["Client Application"] --> Primary Client2["Read Preference: secondary"] --> Secondary1 Client3["Read Preference: secondary"] --> Secondary2 subgraph RS["Replica Set (rs0)"] Primary["PRIMARY NODE
Receives Writes
Maintains Oplog"] Secondary1["SECONDARY NODE 1
Replicates Oplog
Can Serve Reads"] Secondary2["SECONDARY NODE 2
Replicates Oplog
Can Serve Reads"] Primary -.Oplog Replication.-> Secondary1 Primary -.Oplog Replication.-> Secondary2 end Secondary1 -.Heartbeat 10s.-> Primary Secondary2 -.Heartbeat 10s.-> Primary Secondary1 -.Heartbeat 10s.-> Secondary2 Arbiter["ARBITER
Votes in Elections
No Data Storage"] Arbiter -.Vote Only.-> Primary Arbiter -.Vote Only.-> Secondary1 Arbiter -.Vote Only.-> Secondary2 ``` ### 4.2 Sharded Cluster Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR App["Application Server"] --> Mongos1["mongos Query Router 1"] App2["Application Server"] --> Mongos2["mongos Query Router 2"] Mongos1 --> CS["Config Servers
Cluster Metadata"] Mongos2 --> CS Mongos1 --> Shard1["Shard 1
Replica Set"] Mongos1 --> Shard2["Shard 2
Replica Set"] Mongos1 --> Shard3["Shard 3
Replica Set"] Mongos2 --> Shard1 Mongos2 --> Shard2 Mongos2 --> Shard3 subgraph Shard1Set["Shard 1 Replica Set"] S1P["Primary"] S1S1["Secondary"] S1S2["Secondary"] end subgraph Shard2Set["Shard 2 Replica Set"] S2P["Primary"] S2S1["Secondary"] S2S2["Secondary"] end subgraph Shard3Set["Shard 3 Replica Set"] S3P["Primary"] S3S1["Secondary"] S3S2["Secondary"] end ``` ### 4.3 CRUD Operation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["User Request"] --> Op{"Operation Type?"} Op -->|Create| Insert["db.collection.insertOne
db.collection.insertMany"] Op -->|Read| Find["db.collection.find
db.collection.findOne"] Op -->|Update| Update["db.collection.updateOne
db.collection.updateMany
db.collection.findOneAndUpdate"] Op -->|Delete| Delete["db.collection.deleteOne
db.collection.deleteMany
db.collection.findOneAndDelete"] Insert --> ID["Auto-generate ObjectId
if _id not provided"] ID --> Store1["Store BSON document in collection"] Find --> Index{"Index available
on filter field?"} Index -->|Yes| BTree["B-Tree Index Scan
O log n"] Index -->|No| CollScan["Collection Scan
O n"] BTree --> Return["Return Cursor or Document"] CollScan --> Return Update --> UOp["Apply Update Operators
$set, $inc, $push etc"] UOp --> UIndex["Update all affected indexes"] UIndex --> Store2["Persist changes"] Delete --> DCheck{"Match exists?"} DCheck -->|Yes| Remove["Remove document from collection"] DCheck -->|No| NoOp["No operation"] Remove --> DIndex["Update all affected indexes"] Store1 --> Done["Operation Complete"] Return --> Done Store2 --> Done NoOp --> Done DIndex --> Done ``` ### 4.4 Shard Key Distribution — Range vs Hash ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["Choose Sharding Strategy"] --> DataType{"Query Pattern?"} DataType -->|Frequent Range Queries| Range["Range-Based Sharding
Chunk = min, max interval"] DataType -->|Need Even Distribution| Hash["Hash-Based Sharding
Chunk = Hash key mod N"] DataType -->|Geographic / Regulatory| Zone["Zone Sharding
Tag-based placement"] Range --> R1["Shard 1: A - H"] Range --> R2["Shard 2: I - P"] Range --> R3["Shard 3: Q - Z"] R1 -.Hotspot risk.-> R1 R1 -.Most users start with A.-> R1 Hash --> H1["Shard 1: hash A - J"] Hash --> H2["Shard 2: hash K - S"] Hash --> H3["Shard 3: hash T - Z"] H1 -.Even spread.-> H1 Zone --> Z1["Shard 1: Kerala users"] Zone --> Z2["Shard 2: Tamil Nadu users"] Zone --> Z3["Shard 3: Karnataka users"] ``` ### 4.5 Index Types Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB Index["MongoDB Index Types"] --> SF["Single Field Index"] Index --> CF["Compound Index"] Index --> MK["Multikey Index"] Index --> SP["Special Purpose Indexes"] SF --> SFex["{name: 1}"] CF --> CFex["{lastname: 1, firstname: 1}"] CF --> ESR["ESR Rule
Equality, Sort, Range"] MK --> MKex["{tags: 1} on array field"] SP --> TXT["Text Index
Full-text search"] SP --> GEO["Geospatial
2dsphere / 2d"] SP --> HAS["Hashed
Used in Sharding"] SP --> TTL["TTL
Auto-expire docs"] SP --> UNI["Unique
Enforce uniqueness"] SP --> PAR["Partial
Conditional index"] SP --> SPR["Sparse
Only indexed field exists"] ``` ### 4.6 Deployment Architecture Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR Dev["Development"] --> Standalone["Standalone mongod
No redundancy"] Test["Testing"] --> RS["Replica Set 3 nodes
Basic HA"] Prod["Production Small"] --> RS ProdBig["Production Large Scale"] --> Sharded["Sharded Cluster
Multiple shards + mongos"] Cloud["Cloud Native"] --> Atlas["MongoDB Atlas
Fully Managed"] OnPrem["On-Premises"] --> Self["Self-hosted
Ops Manager"] Hybrid["Hybrid"] --> Atlas2["Atlas + On-Prem"] Standalone --> Lim1["Limits: ~Few GB
No failover"] RS --> Lim2["Limits: ~Few TB
Manual scaling"] Sharded --> Lim3["Petabyte scale
Auto balancing"] Atlas --> Lim4["Auto scaling
Backups included"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between SQL and NoSQL databases. List any two advantages of MongoDB over traditional RDBMS.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | SQL (RDBMS) | NoSQL (MongoDB) | |---|---|---| | Data Model | Tables with rows & columns | Documents (BSON) | | Schema | Fixed / rigid | Dynamic / flexible | | Scaling | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) | | Query Language | SQL | MongoDB Query Language (MQL) | | Transactions | ACID (full) | Multi-document ACID (4.0+) | | Joins | Native | `$lookup` / embedded docs | **Two advantages of MongoDB:** 1. **Schema flexibility** — Documents in a collection can have different fields, allowing rapid iteration in agile development. 2. **Horizontal scalability** — Sharding distributes data across multiple servers, supporting petabyte-scale workloads. 3. **High performance** — Embedding related data reduces the need for joins, enabling faster reads. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **What is a BSON document in MongoDB? Explain the structure of an ObjectId.** **Model Answer:** **BSON (Binary JSON):** It is a binary-encoded serialization format used by MongoDB to store documents. BSON extends JSON by adding support for additional data types like `int`, `long`, `double`, `Date`, `ObjectId`, `Binary`, etc. **Maximum document size = 16 MB.** **Structure of ObjectId (12 bytes = 96 bits):** | Bytes | Field | Description | |---|---|---| | 4 bytes | Timestamp | Seconds since Unix epoch | | 5 bytes | Random value | Generated once per process (machine identifier) | | 3 bytes | Increment counter | Auto-incrementing counter initialized randomly | The ObjectId is **unique across distributed systems** without requiring centralized coordination, making it ideal for sharded clusters. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a MongoDB **replica set** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the **primary**, **secondary**, and **arbiter**. How does automatic failover occur when the primary fails? **(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain MongoDB **indexing**. Compare **single field**, **compound**, and **multikey** indexes. What is the **ESR rule**? ##### **Model Solution for (a):** **Replica Set Architecture:** A replica set is a group of `mongod` instances that maintain the same dataset. It provides **high availability** and **redundancy**. **Components:** 1. **Primary (P):** - Receives all write operations. - Maintains the **oplog** (operations log) of all changes. - Only **one primary** exists at any time. 2. **Secondary (S):** - Continuously replicates the primary's oplog. - Applies operations to its own data set asynchronously. - Can serve read queries if `readPreference` is set to `secondary`. 3. **Arbiter (A):** - Holds **no data**. - Participates **only in elections** to break ties. - Does not become primary. **Diagram:** *(refer to Section 4.1 above)* **Automatic Failover Process:** 1. Secondaries send **heartbeat** to all members every **2 seconds**. 2. If heartbeats from Primary are missed for **10 seconds**, an **election** is triggered. 3. Each member votes for a candidate; the candidate with **majority votes** becomes new Primary. 4. Quorum rule: Available members $\geq \frac{N+1}{2}$ where $N$ = total voting members. **Example:** For a 3-member replica set (1 Primary + 2 Secondaries): $$ \text{Quorum} = \lceil 3/2 \rceil = 2 $$ If Primary fails, the 2 secondaries can elect a new primary. If one secondary also fails, only 1 remains — **no majority** — system goes to read-only. ##### **Model Solution for (b):** **MongoDB Indexing:** An index is a special data structure (B-Tree by default) that stores a small portion of the collection's data in an easy-to-traverse form, drastically improving query performance. **Example — Creating an index:** ``` db.students.createIndex({ roll_no: 1 }) ``` Without index: `O(n)` collection scan. With index: `O(log n)` B-Tree lookup. **Types of Indexes:** | Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Single Field** | Index on one field | `{name: 1}` | | **Compound** | Index on multiple fields | `{lastname: 1, firstname: 1}` | | **Multikey** | Index on an array field; creates one index entry per array element | `{tags: 1}` when `tags` is array | **ESR Rule (Equality, Sort, Range):** When designing a compound index, the field order should be: 1. **Equality** fields first (e.g., `{department: "CS"}`). 2. **Sort** fields next (e.g., `sort({cgpa: -1})`). 3. **Range** fields last (e.g., `{age: {$gt: 20}}`). **Optimal Example:** ``` db.students.createIndex({ department: 1, cgpa: -1, age: 1 }) ``` This index efficiently supports queries filtering by `department`, sorting by `cgpa`, and filtering `age` range. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe **sharding** in MongoDB with a neat diagram. Explain the role of **shard**, **config server**, and **mongos**. Compare **range-based** and **hash-based** sharding. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CRUD operations** in MongoDB with examples. Write a query to update the `cgpa` of all students in "Computer Science" department by incrementing it by 0.5, only for those whose current cgpa is below 9.0. ##### **Model Solution for (a):** **Sharding in MongoDB:** Sharding is a method of **horizontal partitioning** of data across multiple machines to support deployments with very large datasets and high throughput. **Components:** 1. **Shard:** Each shard contains a subset of the data. Production shards are deployed as **replica sets** to provide high availability. 2. **Config Server:** Stores cluster metadata — including chunk ranges, shard-to-chunk mappings, and authentication config. Must be deployed as a replica set (3 members). 3. **mongos (Query Router):** Acts as the interface between client applications and the sharded cluster. Routes queries to the appropriate shard(s) and aggregates results. **Diagram:** *(refer to Section 4.2 above)* **Range-Based vs Hash-Based Sharding:** | Feature | Range-Based | Hash-Based | |---|---|---| | Partitioning | Shard key values grouped into contiguous ranges | Hash of shard key, then `mod N_shards` | | Range queries | Highly efficient | Inefficient (queries scatter to all shards) | | Distribution | Can be uneven (hotspots) | Even distribution | | Best for | Time-series, ordered data | Random, high-write workloads | **Example — Range Sharding:** Shard 1: `_id` 0-1000 Shard 2: `_id` 1001-2000 Shard 3: `_id` 2001-3000 **Example — Hash Sharding:** Shard 1: `hash(_id) mod 3 == 0` Shard 2: `hash(_id) mod 3 == 1` Shard 3: `hash(_id) mod 3 == 2` **Chunk balancing:** When any shard's chunk count deviates significantly from the average, the **balancer** automatically migrates chunks. $$ \text{Target per Shard} = \frac{\text{Total Chunks}}{N_{shards}} $$ ##### **Model Solution for (b):** **CRUD Operations in MongoDB:** **1. Create (Insert):** ``` db.students.insertOne({ roll_no: 142, name: "Anjali", department: "Computer Science", cgpa: 8.5 }) ``` **2. Read (Query):** ``` db.students.find({ department: "Computer Science" }) ``` **3. Update:** ``` db.students.updateOne( { roll_no: 142 }, { $set: { cgpa: 8.7 } } ) ``` **4. Delete:** ``` db.students.deleteOne({ roll_no: 142 }) ``` **Required Query — Increment CGPA by 0.5 for CS students with CGPA < 9.0:** ``` db.students.updateMany( { department: "Computer Science", cgpa: { $lt: 9.0 } }, { $inc: { cgpa: 0.5 } } ) ``` **Explanation:** - `updateMany` → updates all matching documents. - Filter clause: `department = "Computer Science"` AND `cgpa < 9.0`. - Update operator: `$inc` increments `cgpa` by `0.5`. - Atomic per-document operation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Loss of Marks):** > - Students often write `{$set: {cgpa: cgpa + 0.5}}` — **WRONG** in MongoDB. There is no implicit reference to current value; you must use `$inc`. > - Forgetting the filter `cgpa: {$lt: 9.0}` will incorrectly increment students who already have CGPA ≥ 9.0. > - Using `updateOne` instead of `updateMany` will only affect the first match. > - Confusing `$lt` (less than) with `$lte` (less than or equal) — the question clearly states *below* 9.0, so `$lt` is correct. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):** **A. Core MongoDB Concepts** - MongoDB is a **document-oriented NoSQL** database using **BSON** format. - **16 MB** is the maximum document size. - ObjectId is **12 bytes**: 4B timestamp + 5B random + 3B counter. - Schema is **dynamic** — no fixed structure enforced. **B. CRUD Operations** - **Create:** `insertOne()`, `insertMany()` (use `ordered: false` for bulk efficiency). - **Read:** `find()`, `findOne()`, with operators like `$eq`, `$gt`, `$in`, `$regex`, `$exists`, `$elemMatch`. - **Update:** `updateOne()`, `updateMany()`, `findOneAndUpdate()`; operators: `$set`, `$inc`, `$push`, `$pull`, `$addToSet`, `$unset`, `$rename`. - **Delete:** `deleteOne()`, `deleteMany()`, `findOneAndDelete()`. **C. Indexing** - B-Tree based; reduces time complexity from $O(n)$ to $O(\log n)$. - **ESR rule:** Equality → Sort → Range (in compound index field order). - Special types: Text, Geospatial, Hashed, TTL, Unique, Partial, Sparse. - **Trade-off:** Indexes speed reads but **slow down writes**. **D. Replication** - Replica set = Primary + Secondaries (+ optional Arbiter). - Automatic failover triggered after **10 seconds** of missed heartbeats. - Quorum rule: Available members $\geq \lceil N/2 \rceil$. - **Oplog** is the mechanism for replication. **E. Sharding** - Components: **Shard** + **Config Server** + **mongos**. - Shard key must be **indexed** and **immutable**. - Range sharding = good for range queries, risk of hotspots. - Hash sharding = even distribution, poor for range queries. - Default chunk size = **64 MB**; balancer auto-migrates. **F. Deployment** - Dev: Standalone → Prod: Replica Set → Scale: Sharded Cluster. - Atlas = managed cloud service (AWS/Azure/GCP). - Production requires: authentication, TLS, WriteConcern=majority, monitoring, backups. **G. Frequently Asked 3-Mark Topics** 1. Difference between SQL and NoSQL. 2. BSON document structure. 3. ObjectId structure. 4. Replica set components. 5. Sharding components. 6. CRUD operations with examples. **H. Frequently Asked 14-Mark Topics** 1. Replica set architecture + failover mechanism. 2. Sharding architecture + range vs hash sharding. 3. Aggregation pipeline with example. 4. Indexing types + ESR rule. 5. CRUD operations with complex query examples. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Strategy:** For KTU 2024 ESE, always include a **labeled diagram** for 14-mark questions. Examiners award 1-2 marks for clear visualization. Practice at least 5 complex `updateMany` and `aggregate` queries before the exam.

topics.title: Cassandra: Data Model, Key Space, Table Operations, CRUD Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST634 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cassandra: Data Model, Key Space, Table Operations, CRUD Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Apache Cassandra — Formal Definition

**Apache Cassandra** is a highly scalable, distributed, eventually consistent, master-less (peer-to-peer) **NoSQL database** built on the **Dynamo-inspired** architecture (Amazon Dynamo) and the **Bigtable-inspired** data model (Google Bigtable). It is engineered to deliver **single-digit millisecond latency** at extreme write throughput and is designed to run across multiple commodity servers (data centers) without a single point of failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Syllabus Definition Snapshot:**
> Cassandra is a wide-column store / partitioned row-store that organizes data in **Column Families** (called *tables*) residing inside **Keyspaces**. The unit of physical distribution is the **partition** (identified by the **partition key**).

---

## 1.2 The Cassandra Data Model — Hierarchy

The data model in Cassandra is organized as a **four-level hierarchy** (from outermost to innermost):

| Level | Cassandra Name | Relational Analogue | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cluster** | Database Server Farm | The complete set of nodes (machines) |
| 2 | **Keyspace** | Database / Schema | Defines replication strategy |
| 3 | **Table** (Column Family) | Table | Holds rows of related data |
| 4 | **Row** | Row | A unit identified by a primary key |
| 5 | **Column** | Column | The actual name-value pair |

> [!NOTE]
> Inside a row, columns are grouped into **partitions**. Rows that share the same partition key are physically stored together on the **same node**. This is the fundamental unit of distribution.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Building" Model

Imagine Cassandra as a **massive library system with millions of books, but with NO central librarian**:

- 📚 **Cluster** = The entire library building.
- 🏷️ **Keyspace** = A *thematic wing* of the library (e.g., "Science Wing", "History Wing"). Each wing has its own *rulebook* for how many copies of each book must be kept (replication).
- 🗄️ **Table (Column Family)** = A *specific shelf* inside the wing (e.g., "Physics Journals", "Physics Books").
- 📖 **Row** = A *single book* sitting on a shelf. The book's unique **call number** is the *Primary Key*.
- 🪧 **Column** = The *labels* on the side of the book — the actual data fields (title, author, year, etc.).

If you ask "Where is the book?", the library uses a deterministic rule (consistent hashing on the partition key) to immediately tell you **which wing → which shelf → which book**. You don't have to walk around asking — this is why Cassandra is fast!

---

## 1.4 Why Cassandra's Model is Different from RDBMS

| Feature | RDBMS | Cassandra |
|---|---|---|
| Data organization | Rows & columns in tables | Partitioned rows with dynamic columns |
| Schema | Rigid, predefined | Flexible / schema-on-read (optional columns) |
| Scaling | Vertical (scale up) | Horizontal (scale out) |
| Consistency | Strong (ACID) | Tunable (eventual → strong) |
| Query first, then design | Yes | **No — design query-first (query-driven)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Many students confuse Cassandra's *Column Family* with RDBMS *Tables*. Remember — a Column Family can have **millions of columns** per row, and rows are NOT required to share the same columns!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Cassandra Data Model

### 2.1.1 The Column
The atomic unit. A triple: `(name, value, timestamp)` plus optional `ttl`.
```
column = < column_name : column_value : [writetime | ttl] >
```

### 2.1.2 The Partition
A partition is the **physical storage unit**. All rows sharing the same partition key live on the **same node** (and are physically grouped on disk via SSTables). The partition key is hashed to determine the node.

$$\text{Token} \;=\; \text{Hash}(\text{PartitionKey}) \pmod{2^{127}}$$

(Cassandra uses **Murmur3Partitioner** by default, producing a 64-bit token; legacy RandomPartitioner used MD5 giving 128-bit tokens.)

### 2.1.3 The Primary Key = Partition Key + Clustering Columns

```
PRIMARY KEY (
    partition_key_column(s),          -- determines the node
    clustering_column_1,               -- sort order within partition
    clustering_column_2, ...
)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Partition Key** → decides **WHERE** the data lives (which node).
> - **Clustering Columns** → decide **HOW** data is sorted **INSIDE** the partition (ascending by default).

### 2.1.4 Wide Rows vs Tall Rows
A "wide row" simply means *many clustering columns per partition*. Cassandra handles this natively — no JOIN, no index needed for range queries inside the partition.

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Wide row** | Many rows share a partition key | Sensor data: `(sensor_id, timestamp)` |
| **Tall row** | Each row has a unique partition key | Users: `(user_id)` |

---

## 2.2 The Keyspace — Conceptual Deep Dive

A **Keyspace** is the outermost container that defines:
1. **Replication Strategy** — how copies are placed
2. **Replication Factor (RF)** — how many copies total
3. **Durable Writes** — whether writes go to commit log of all replicas

### 2.2.1 The Two Replication Strategies

**(a) SimpleStrategy** — used for single data center only:
- First replica on the partitioner's node.
- Remaining replicas placed on the **next clockwise nodes** in the ring.

**(b) NetworkTopologyStrategy** — used for multi-DC deployments (production standard):
- Replicas are placed in **specific data centers**.
- RF can be configured **per data center**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Always choose `NetworkTopologyStrategy` even in single-DC production setups — it allows you to expand later without migration headaches.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Property Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Property | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Token | $T = \text{Murmur3}(PK) \bmod 2^{64}$ | Default partitioner |
| Replication Factor | $RF \le N$ (number of nodes) | $RF=N \Rightarrow$ full copies everywhere |
| Quorum Read | $Q_R = \lfloor RF/2 \rfloor + 1$ | Strong consistency |
| Quorum Write | $Q_W = \lfloor RF/2 \rfloor + 1$ | Strong consistency |
| Consistency Level — ONE | $CL = 1$ replica | Fastest, weakest |
| Consistency Level — ALL | $CL = RF$ | Strongest, slowest |
| Consistency Check | $Q_R + Q_W > RF$ | Guarantees strong consistency |
| Sliding Window (WINDOW) | $W = \vert Q_W + Q_R - RF \vert$ | Stale read window |
| Partition Size Limit | $\le 100 \text{ MB}$ (soft) | Hard limit ~2GB |
| Columns per row | $2 \text{ billion}$ | Theoretically supported |
| Max partition key size | $65{,}535$ bytes | Practical limit |
| Default gc_grace_seconds | $864{,}000$ s (10 days) | Tombstone retention |

> [!WARNING]
> Never embed `|` inside table cells. Always write `$\vert x \vert$` in math mode to avoid breaking markdown.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Cassandra's data model is purpose-built for:
- **Time-series workloads** — IoT, telemetry, metrics (sensor readings).
- **Messaging platforms** — WhatsApp, Discord, Instagram direct messages.
- **Recommendation engines** — wide columns store interaction histories per user.
- **Write-heavy OLTP** — order management, fraud detection (millions of writes/sec).

The trade-off: **you sacrifice ad-hoc querying**. Cassandra refuses a query that doesn't filter on the partition key + clustering columns (to prevent cluster-scanning anti-patterns).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 CQL Syntax — Building the Data Model Step-by-Step

Below is the **complete operational derivation** from cluster creation → table creation → CRUD operations. Every syntactic element is explained.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Creating a Keyspace

```sql
CREATE KEYSPACE IF NOT EXISTS iot_platform
    WITH replication = {
        'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy',
        'datacenter1': 3
    }
AND durable_writes = true;
```

**Logic Walk-through:**
- `IF NOT EXISTS` — avoids an exception if the keyspace already exists.
- `'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy'` — recommended for any production-grade deployment.
- `'datacenter1': 3` — **replication factor = 3**, meaning **3 copies** of every piece of data are stored across the cluster.
- `durable_writes = true` — writes are first written to the commit log before memtable (default behavior; useful to disable for batch analytics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Once created, you cannot change the `class` (strategy) of a keyspace via `ALTER KEYSPACE`. You can only change the RF values.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Using a Keyspace (Equivalent to `USE db`)

```sql
USE iot_platform;
```

This is analogous to `USE database_name;` in MySQL.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Creating a Table (Column Family)

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sensor_readings (
    sensor_id     text,
    reading_time  timestamp,
    temperature   double,
    humidity      double,
    location      text,
    PRIMARY KEY ((sensor_id), reading_time)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (reading_time DESC);
```

**Logic Walk-through:**
- `sensor_id` is wrapped in **double parentheses** → it is the **partition key**.
- `reading_time` is the **clustering column** — sorts rows within each sensor_id partition.
- `WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY` — newest readings come first.
- Composite partition key would use multiple columns inside `((c1, c2))`.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Composite Partition Key Example

```sql
CREATE TABLE user_messages (
    user_id      uuid,
    thread_id    uuid,
    sent_at      timestamp,
    body         text,
    PRIMARY KEY ((user_id, thread_id), sent_at)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (sent_at ASC);
```

> Here `(user_id, thread_id)` is the **composite partition key** — both together determine the partition.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Table Altering Operations

```sql
ALTER TABLE sensor_readings ADD battery_level int;
ALTER TABLE sensor_readings DROP battery_level;
ALTER TABLE sensor_readings WITH comment = 'Updated schema v2';
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** You can only ADD new columns. You cannot *modify* an existing column's data type in-place; doing so requires creating a new table and copying data.

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Truncate & Drop

```sql
TRUNCATE sensor_readings;   -- removes all rows but keeps schema
DROP TABLE sensor_readings; -- removes schema + data
DROP KEYSPACE iot_platform; -- removes all tables in keyspace
```

---

## 3.2 CRUD Operations — Analytical Walk-through

### 3.2.1 **C** — Create (INSERT)

```sql
INSERT INTO sensor_readings
    (sensor_id, reading_time, temperature, humidity, location)
VALUES
    ('SENSOR_001', toTimestamp(now()), 24.7, 60.2, 'Kerala');
```

**Optional clauses:**
- `USING TIMESTAMP 1234567890` — manually set the write timestamp (used for conflict resolution in last-write-wins).
- `USING TTL 86400` — automatic deletion after 24 hours.

> **How INSERT works internally:**
> Data is first written to the **Commit Log** (for crash recovery), then to an in-memory **Memtable**, then periodically flushed to disk as an immutable **SSTable**.

### 3.2.2 **R** — Read (SELECT)

```sql
-- Point query (efficient: single partition lookup)
SELECT * FROM sensor_readings
WHERE sensor_id = 'SENSOR_001';

-- Range query within a partition (efficient: uses clustering column)
SELECT * FROM sensor_readings
WHERE sensor_id = 'SENSOR_001'
  AND reading_time >= '2024-01-01'
  AND reading_time <  '2024-02-01';
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A query that **omits** the partition key triggers a **cluster-wide scan** — never do this in production. Always include the partition key in `WHERE`.

### 3.2.3 **U** — Update (UPDATE)

```sql
UPDATE sensor_readings
SET humidity = 65.5
WHERE sensor_id = 'SENSOR_001'
  AND reading_time = '2024-01-15 10:00:00';
```

In Cassandra, "UPDATE" = "INSERT with same primary key". There is **no in-place row modification** — every write creates a new cell with a newer timestamp; older cells are eventually compacted away.

### 3.2.4 **D** — Delete (DELETE)

```sql
DELETE FROM sensor_readings
WHERE sensor_id = 'SENSOR_001'
  AND reading_time = '2024-01-15 10:00:00';

-- Delete all readings for a sensor:
DELETE FROM sensor_readings WHERE sensor_id = 'SENSOR_001';

-- Delete a single column:
DELETE humidity FROM sensor_readings
WHERE sensor_id = 'SENSOR_001'
  AND reading_time = '2024-01-15 10:00:00';
```

Deletes in Cassandra insert a **tombstone** marker, not a physical delete. Tombstones remain for `gc_grace_seconds` (default 10 days) before compaction purges them.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Key Components

| Question | Choice | CQL Snippet |
|---|---|---|
| Distribute by user? | Partition key = `user_id` | `PRIMARY KEY ((user_id), …)` |
| Distribute by user AND region? | Composite partition | `PRIMARY KEY ((user_id, region), …)` |
| Need unique row? | Add clustering column | `PRIMARY KEY ((uid), msg_id)` |
| Need uniqueness across cluster? | Use `IF NOT EXISTS` | `INSERT … IF NOT EXISTS` |
| Read-mostly wide rows? | Clustering by timestamp | `CLUSTERING ORDER BY (ts DESC)` |

---

## 3.4 Quorum / Consistency Derivation

For RF=3, the consistency levels mathematically evaluated:

$$
Q_R = \lfloor 3/2 \rfloor + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

$$
Q_W = \lfloor 3/2 \rfloor + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

**Strong consistency check:**

$$
Q_R + Q_W = 2 + 2 = 4 > RF = 3 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Sliding window (stale-read window):**

$$
W = \vert Q_W + Q_R - RF \vert = \vert 2 + 2 - 3 \vert = 1
$$

Only **1 stale write** can be observed — minimal latency penalty.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Cassandra Data Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cluster nodeA] --> B[Keyspace iot_platform]
    A2[Cluster nodeB] --> B
    A3[Cluster nodeC] --> B
    B --> C1[Table sensor_readings]
    B --> C2[Table user_alerts]
    B --> C3[Table device_metadata]
    C1 --> D1[Partition Sensor 001]
    C1 --> D2[Partition Sensor 002]
    C1 --> D3[Partition Sensor 003]
    D1 --> E1[Row ts 1000 temp 24.7]
    D1 --> E2[Row ts 1100 temp 24.9]
    D1 --> E3[Row ts 1200 temp 25.1]
    E1 --> F1[Column temperature]
    E1 --> F2[Column humidity]
    E1 --> F3[Column location]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Write Path (INSERT) Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    W[Client Write Request] --> A[Coordinator Node]
    A --> B[Write to Commit Log]
    B --> C[Write to Memtable]
    C --> D{Ack from RF replicas}
    D -->|Quorum met| E[Return Success]
    C -.->|Periodic flush| F[SSTable on Disk]
    F --> G[Compaction Process]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Read Path (SELECT) Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Client Read Request] --> CO[Coordinator Node]
    CO --> RR[Replica Node 1]
    CO --> RR2[Replica Node 2]
    CO --> RR3[Replica Node 3]
    RR --> M1[Memtable Lookup]
    RR --> S1[SSTable Lookup]
    RR2 --> M2[Memtable Lookup]
    RR3 --> S3[SSTable Lookup]
    M1 --> BC[Block Read / Row Merge]
    M2 --> BC
    S1 --> BC
    S3 --> BC
    BC --> RT[Return Latest by Timestamp]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Partition Distribution (Consistent Hashing Ring)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Partition Sensor A] -->|murmur3 hash| N1[Node 1]
    P2[Partition Sensor B] -->|murmur3 hash| N2[Node 2]
    P3[Partition Sensor C] -->|murmur3 hash| N3[Node 3]
    P4[Partition Sensor D] -->|murmur3 hash| N4[Node 4]
    N1 -.Replica.-> N3
    N2 -.Replica.-> N4
    N3 -.Replica.-> N1
    N4 -.Replica.-> N2
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — CRUD Lifecycle Inside a Partition

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Partition_Sensor001
        direction TB
        R1[Row ts=1000 INSERT temp=24.7]
        R2[Row ts=1100 INSERT temp=24.9]
        R3[Row ts=1100 UPDATE temp=25.0]
        R4[Tombstone ts=1200 DELETE]
        R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    end
    CO[Coordinator Client] -->|INSERT| R1
    CO -->|UPDATE| R3
    CO -->|DELETE| R4
    CO -->|SELECT| ME[Merge by writetime]
    ME --> RS[Return latest non-tombstoned value]
```

## 4.6 Schematic — Keyspace Replication Strategy Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SimpleStrategy_SingleDC
        S1[Node A]
        S2[Node B]
        S3[Node C]
        P[Partition] -->|primary| S1
        P -->|next clockwise| S2
        P -->|next clockwise| S3
    end
    subgraph NetworkTopologyStrategy_MultiDC
        DC1[DC1] --> N1A[Node 1A]
        DC1 --> N1B[Node 1B]
        DC2[DC2] --> N2A[Node 2A]
        DC2 --> N2B[Node 2B]
        P2[Partition] --> N1A
        P2 --> N2A
        P2 --> N1B
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### **Q1. Define a Keyspace in Cassandra. List any two replication strategies.**
`[KTU University Exam — Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Keyspace** is the outermost container in Cassandra that defines replication settings for all tables within it. It is analogous to a *database* or *schema* in RDBMS.

Replication strategies:
1. **SimpleStrategy** — used in single data-center deployments; replicas are placed on the next clockwise nodes in the ring.
2. **NetworkTopologyStrategy** — used in multi-DC deployments; replicas are explicitly placed in specified data centers (recommended for production even in single-DC setups).

*(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Partition Key and Clustering Column in Cassandra.**
`[KTU University Exam — Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Partition Key | Clustering Column |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Decides **which node** stores the row | Decides **sort order** within the partition |
| Number per row | Exactly one value (composite allowed) | Zero or more |
| Position in PK | First (in `(( ) )`) | After partition key |
| Query necessity | **Mandatory** in WHERE clause | Optional, used for range queries |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the Cassandra data model in detail with a neat diagram. Compare it with the relational data model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss Keyspace creation and replication strategies with suitable CQL examples. Explain how a partition key is mapped to a node using consistent hashing. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Cassandra Data Model — Detailed Explanation**

Cassandra's data model is a **multi-dimensional map** indexed by a key. It is organized as a hierarchy: **Cluster → Keyspace → Table (Column Family) → Partition → Row → Column.**

**Comparison with RDBMS:**

| RDBMS | Cassandra |
|---|---|
| Table has fixed columns | Table can have variable / sparse columns |
| JOIN allowed | JOIN not allowed |
| Foreign keys supported | No foreign keys |
| Aggregation via GROUP BY | Aggregation limited (no GROUP BY across partitions) |
| Normalization encouraged | Denormalization is the norm |
| Query-first, design-later | **Design-query-first** approach |

The Cassandra data model is built to scale horizontally on commodity hardware. Each row is identified by a **Primary Key**, which consists of a **Partition Key** and optional **Clustering Columns**. Data is physically grouped by partition key on the same node.

**Diagram:** *(refer to Section 4.1 of these notes)*

*(7 marks — 2 marks hierarchy, 3 marks RDBMS comparison table, 2 marks diagram)*

---

**(b) Keyspace Creation & Consistent Hashing**

```sql
CREATE KEYSPACE social_media
WITH replication = {
    'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy',
    'DC1': 3,
    'DC2': 2
} AND durable_writes = true;
```

- **NetworkTopologyStrategy** — places 3 replicas in DC1 and 2 in DC2.
- **SimpleStrategy** — uses clockwise neighbor placement, single DC only.

**Consistent Hashing Mapping:**

$$
\text{Token} = \text{Murmur3Hash}(\text{PartitionKey}) \bmod 2^{64}
$$

Each node owns a range of tokens on the hash ring. The coordinator hashes the partition key, finds the responsible node, and forwards the request. Replicas are stored on the next N nodes clockwise (where N = RF).

**Example:**
- Partition Key = `user_id = 101`
- `Murmur3(101) = 5723498572349857234`
- Modulo ring → node `nodeC`
- Replicas placed on `nodeD` and `nodeE`
