This ensures **even distribution** of data and **no single point of failure**.

*(7 marks — 3 marks CQL, 2 marks strategy explanation, 2 marks hashing math)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain CRUD operations in Cassandra with CQL syntax. What are TTL and Tombstones? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Demonstrate the CREATE TABLE statement with partition key and clustering column. Show how to perform SELECT, UPDATE, and DELETE on this table. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) CRUD Operations in Cassandra**

| Operation | CQL Syntax | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **CREATE** | `INSERT INTO … VALUES (…)` | Appends cell to partition |
| **READ** | `SELECT … FROM … WHERE …` | Merges across SSTables by timestamp |
| **UPDATE** | `UPDATE … SET col=val WHERE …` | Inserts a newer cell version |
| **DELETE** | `DELETE FROM … WHERE …` | Writes a **tombstone** marker |

**TTL (Time-To-Live):**
```sql
INSERT INTO messages (user_id, msg_id, body, sent_at)
VALUES (uuid(), now(), 'hello', toTimestamp(now()))
USING TTL 3600;   -- auto-delete after 1 hour
```

**Tombstones:**
When a row or column is deleted, Cassandra writes a **tombstone** (a special marker with a timestamp). Until `gc_grace_seconds` elapse, the tombstone prevents resurrected zombie data. After that period, compaction removes the data permanently.

*(7 marks — 3 marks CRUD table, 2 marks TTL, 2 marks tombstone explanation)*

---

**(b) Practical Demonstration**

**Schema creation:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE orders (
    customer_id  int,
    order_date   timestamp,
    order_id     uuid,
    amount       decimal,
    status       text,
    PRIMARY KEY ((customer_id), order_date, order_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (order_date DESC, order_id ASC);
```

- `customer_id` → Partition Key (decides node).
- `order_date, order_id` → Clustering Columns (sort order).

**INSERT:**
```sql
INSERT INTO orders (customer_id, order_date, order_id, amount, status)
VALUES (101, '2024-05-01', uuid(), 1500.00, 'PLACED');
```

**SELECT (range within partition):**
```sql
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 101
  AND order_date >= '2024-01-01'
  AND order_date <  '2024-06-01';
```

**UPDATE:**
```sql
UPDATE orders SET status = 'SHIPPED'
WHERE customer_id = 101
  AND order_date = '2024-05-01'
  AND order_id = 5b9c8e30-...;
```

**DELETE:**
```sql
DELETE FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 101
  AND order_date = '2024-05-01'
  AND order_id = 5b9c8e30-...;
```

*(7 marks — 2 marks schema, 2 marks insert/select, 2 marks update/delete, 1 mark explanation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warnings:**
> 1. **Mark loss #1:** Writing `PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, reading_time)` instead of `((sensor_id), reading_time)` — the double parentheses make `sensor_id` a true partition key, not part of a composite.
> 2. **Mark loss #2:** Forgetting to mention `gc_grace_seconds` when explaining tombstones — it is the key reason tombstones are NOT immediately removed.
> 3. **Mark loss #3:** Confusing `SimpleStrategy` with `NetworkTopologyStrategy` — production answers **must** justify the choice.
> 4. **Mark loss #4:** Omitting the limitation *"no JOIN, no GROUP BY across partitions"* in RDBMS comparison — it earns easy marks.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Cassandra = Wide-Column NoSQL DB** based on Dynamo + Bigtable.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** `Cluster → Keyspace → Table → Partition → Row → Column`.
- ✅ **Keyspace** defines replication strategy + RF + durable writes.
- ✅ **Two replication strategies:** `SimpleStrategy` (single-DC), `NetworkTopologyStrategy` (multi-DC, production standard).
- ✅ **Primary Key = Partition Key + Clustering Columns.**
- ✅ **Partition Key** uses **Murmur3 hashing** to map data to nodes.
- ✅ **Clustering Columns** sort data inside the partition.
- ✅ **CREATE TABLE / ALTER / DROP / TRUNCATE** are the table operations.
- ✅ **CRUD in CQL:** `INSERT`, `SELECT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`.
- ✅ **TTL** auto-deletes data after specified seconds.
- ✅ **Tombstones** mark deleted data; removed after `gc_grace_seconds`.
- ✅ **UPDATE = INSERT with newer timestamp** (no in-place modification).
- ✅ **No JOINs, no GROUP BY across partitions, no subqueries.**
- ✅ Always include the **partition key** in `WHERE` clause.
- ✅ Strong consistency holds iff: $Q_R + Q_W > RF$.
- ✅ Default partition size soft limit = **100 MB**; hard limit ~**2 GB**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graph Database: Introduction, Data Modelling with Graphs, Building a Graph Database Application, Data Modeling, Predictive Analysis with Graph Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST634 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Graph Database: Introduction, Data Modelling with Graphs, Building a Graph Database Application, Data Modeling, Predictive Analysis with Graph Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Graph Database — Formal Definition

A **Graph Database** is a NoSQL database designed to treat the relationships between data as equally important as the data itself. It uses **graph structures** with **nodes (vertices)**, **edges (relationships)**, and **properties** to represent and store data. Unlike relational databases that rely on foreign keys and JOIN operations, graph databases store relationships as first-class entities, enabling direct physical links between data elements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Graph Database uses *graph theory* to map, store, and query relationships. It represents data as a network of connected entities (nodes) and the *typed* relationships (edges) between them, optimized for traversing highly connected data.

The formal mathematical representation of a graph is:

$$G = (V, E)$$

where:
- $V$ = a finite, non-empty set of **vertices (nodes)**
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ = a set of **edges (relationships)** connecting pairs of vertices

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU terminology, this is called a **Property Graph Model** when nodes and edges carry attributes (key-value pairs), and is the standard model used by **Neo4j**, the industry-leading graph database.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Social Network" Model

Imagine a **Facebook-style social network**. You are a person (a *node*). You have friends who are also persons (more *nodes*). The "friend" connection between you and them is a *relationship* (an *edge*). Now, you may also have liked certain pages, attended certain events, and worked at certain companies — each of these is a *node*, and the connection between you and them is another *relationship* with a *type* (e.g., `LIKES`, `ATTENDED`, `WORKS_AT`).

In a **relational database**, finding "all friends of my friends who work at Google" would require multiple expensive `JOIN` operations across several tables. In a **graph database**, the database simply **traverses** the relationship pointers — a constant-time hop operation, regardless of dataset size.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** A graph database is like a pre-wired network. In a relational DB, relationships are *computed* (via JOIN); in a graph DB, relationships are *stored* (as physical pointers) — this is the fundamental performance advantage.

### 1.2.1 The Famous Statement by Emil Eifrem (Neo4j Co-Founder)

> *"Graphs are the most natural way to model connected data. Relationships are first-class citizens."*

This captures the entire philosophy of graph databases.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology Mapping (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Synonym | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| Vertex | Node | Represents an entity (e.g., Person, Movie, City) |
| Edge | Relationship, Arc, Link | A typed, directed connection between two nodes |
| Property | Attribute | A key-value pair attached to a node or relationship |
| Label | Type, Tag | Categorizes nodes (e.g., `:Person`, `:Movie`) |
| Path | Traversal | A sequence of nodes and edges connecting a start to an end |
| Adjacency | Direct connection | The concept that every node has a direct physical reference to its neighbor |
| Index-free adjacency | — | The principle that physical pointers enable O(1) traversal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The phrase *"Index-Free Adjacency"* is heavily tested. It means that each node directly references its adjacent nodes via in-memory pointers, eliminating the need for global index lookups during traversal.

---

## 1.4 Why Graph Databases? — Limitations of Relational in Connected Data

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students often think "I can just use JOINs in SQL — why do I need a graph DB?" The answer is the **performance cliff** that occurs when JOINs multiply (e.g., 7+ JOINs in a deeply connected query become exponentially slow).

| Feature | Relational DB | Graph DB |
|---------|---------------|----------|
| Data model | Tables with rows and columns | Nodes, relationships, properties |
| Relationship storage | Foreign keys, computed via JOIN | Physical pointers, stored as edges |
| Query performance (deep joins) | Degrades exponentially | Constant per hop |
| Schema | Rigid, predefined | Flexible, schema-on-read |
| Best use case | Transactional, structured data | Highly connected, relationship-rich data |

---

## 1.5 Categories of Graph Databases

1. **Property Graph Databases** — Nodes and edges have properties (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, JanusGraph)
2. **RDF (Resource Description Framework) Stores** — Triples of subject-predicate-object (e.g., Apache Jena)
3. **Hypergraph Databases** — Edges can connect more than two nodes (research-grade, e.g., HyperGraphDB)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 4 focuses exclusively on the **Property Graph Model** (used in Neo4j), as it is the industry and academic standard.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Property Graph Model — Anatomy

A **Property Graph** is formally defined as a directed, labeled, attributed multigraph. It consists of:

1. **Nodes (Vertices):** Represent entities. Each node can have:
   - A unique identifier
   - One or more labels (e.g., `:Person`, `:Employee`)
   - A set of properties (key-value pairs)

2. **Relationships (Edges):** Always have:
   - A **direction** (from a *start node* to an *end node*)
   - A **type** (e.g., `KNOWS`, `ACTED_IN`)
   - A set of **properties** (e.g., `since: 2020`, `weight: 0.8`)

3. **Properties:** Key-value pairs that add metadata to nodes or relationships.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** A relationship in a graph database *always* has a direction. Even if the underlying connection is bidirectional (e.g., friends), the model stores one direction with an associated semantic meaning.

---

## 2.2 Formal Graph-Theoretic Foundations

### 2.2.1 Order, Size, and Density

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a graph.

- **Order of G:** The number of vertices, denoted $n = \vert V \vert$
- **Size of G:** The number of edges, denoted $m = \vert E \vert$
- **Maximum possible edges (undirected):** 

$$m_{\max} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

- **Maximum possible edges (directed):** 

$$m_{\max} = n(n-1)$$

- **Density of G:** The ratio of actual edges to maximum possible edges.

$$\rho(G) = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{n(n-1)} \quad \text{(undirected)}$$

$$\rho(G) = \frac{\vert E \vert}{n(n-1)} \quad \text{(directed)}$$

A graph with $\rho = 1$ is called a **complete graph** ($K_n$).

### 2.2.2 Degree of a Vertex

- **Degree of vertex $v$**, denoted $deg(v)$: The number of edges incident to $v$.
- **Handshaking Lemma:**

$$\sum_{v \in V} deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$$

- **In-degree** $deg^{-}(v)$: Number of incoming edges.
- **Out-degree** $deg^{+}(v)$: Number of outgoing edges.
- For a directed graph: 

$$deg^{-}(v) + deg^{+}(v) = deg(v)$$

### 2.2.3 Adjacency Matrix Representation

For a graph with $n$ vertices, the **adjacency matrix** $A$ is an $n \times n$ matrix where:

$$A_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (v_i, v_j) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The number of **walks of length $k$** from $v_i$ to $v_j$ is given by:

$$W_{ij}^{(k)} = (A^k)_{ij}$$

This is the foundation of **predictive analysis** using graphs (e.g., link prediction, recommender systems).

### 2.2.4 Adjacency List (Index-Free Adjacency)

In graph databases, each node stores a direct pointer to its neighbors. This is conceptually equivalent to an **adjacency list** in memory, but physically implemented as:

$$\text{Node} \rightarrow [\text{Edge}_1, \text{Edge}_2, \ldots, \text{Edge}_k]$$

This is why traversal cost is **O(1) per hop** (a pointer dereference), independent of total graph size.

---

## 2.3 Path and Connectivity Concepts

| Concept | Definition |
|---------|------------|
| **Walk** | A sequence of vertices and edges; vertices may repeat |
| **Trail** | A walk with no repeated edges |
| **Path** | A walk with no repeated vertices |
| **Shortest Path** | The path with minimum total edge weight |
| **Cycle** | A path that starts and ends at the same vertex |
| **Connected Graph** | Every pair of vertices has a path between them |
| **Strongly Connected** (directed) | A path exists from $v_i$ to $v_j$ for *all* pairs |

### 2.3.1 Shortest Path Algorithms

**Dijkstra's Algorithm** finds the shortest path from a source $s$ to all other vertices in a graph with non-negative edge weights.

$$dist(s, v) = \min_{P} \sum_{(u,w) \in P} w(u,w)$$

where $P$ ranges over all paths from $s$ to $v$, and $w(u,w)$ is the weight of edge $(u,w)$.

**Bellman-Ford Algorithm** handles negative weights, with complexity $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$.

**A\* Search Algorithm** uses a heuristic to find the shortest path more efficiently:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to node $n$
- $h(n)$ = estimated (heuristic) cost from $n$ to goal

---

## 2.4 Centrality Measures (Predictive Analysis Core)

Centrality measures quantify the *importance* of a node within a network.

### 2.4.1 Degree Centrality

$$C_D(v) = \frac{deg(v)}{n-1}$$

Normalized by the maximum possible degree $n-1$.

### 2.4.2 Closeness Centrality

$$C_C(v) = \frac{n-1}{\sum_{u \neq v} d(v, u)}$$

where $d(v, u)$ is the shortest path distance between $v$ and $u$. A node with high closeness can quickly reach all other nodes.

### 2.4.3 Betweenness Centrality

$$C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{st}$ = total number of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$
- $\sigma_{st}(v)$ = number of those paths passing through $v$

### 2.4.4 Eigenvector Centrality (PageRank)

$$PR(v) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$$

where:
- $d$ = damping factor (typically **0.85**)
- $N$ = total number of vertices
- $M(v)$ = vertices pointing to $v$
- $L(u)$ = out-degree of vertex $u$

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{c} = \frac{1}{\lambda} A \mathbf{c}$$

where $A$ is the adjacency matrix and $\lambda$ is the largest eigenvalue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Google's PageRank** is fundamentally a graph-theoretic predictive algorithm. It is the canonical KTU example of "Predictive Analysis with Graph Theory."

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **complete formula inventory** for Module 4 Graph Database derivations. Memorize these.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Graph definition | $G = (V, E)$ | $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges |
| 2 | Max edges (undirected) | $m_{\max} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Complete graph $K_n$ |
| 3 | Max edges (directed) | $m_{\max} = n(n-1)$ | — |
| 4 | Density (undirected) | $\rho = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{n(n-1)}$ | $0 \le \rho \le 1$ |
| 5 | Handshaking Lemma | $\sum deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$ | Always true |
| 6 | Walks of length $k$ | $(A^k)_{ij}$ | Matrix power of adjacency |
| 7 | Dijkstra complexity | $O((V+E) \log V)$ | Min-heap based |
| 8 | Bellman-Ford complexity | $O(VE)$ | Handles negative weights |
| 9 | A\* evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $h$ = heuristic |
| 10 | Degree Centrality | $C_D(v) = \frac{deg(v)}{n-1}$ | Normalized |
| 11 | Closeness Centrality | $C_C(v) = \frac{n-1}{\sum_{u} d(v,u)}$ | — |
| 12 | Betweenness Centrality | $C_B(v) = \sum \frac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | — |
| 13 | PageRank (Eigenvector) | $PR(v) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$ | $d = 0.85$ |
| 14 | Jaccard Coefficient | $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert N(A) \cap N(B) \vert}{\vert N(A) \cup N(B) \vert}$ | Link prediction |
| 15 | Common Neighbors | $CN(A,B) = \vert N(A) \cap N(B) \vert$ | Simplest predictor |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Handshaking Lemma

**Statement:** In any undirected graph, the sum of the degrees of all vertices equals twice the number of edges.

$$\sum_{v \in V} deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$$

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } G = (V, E) \text{ be an undirected graph.} \\
&\text{Each edge } e = (u, v) \in E \text{ contributes exactly 1 to } deg(u) \text{ and 1 to } deg(v). \\
&\text{Therefore, each edge contributes exactly 2 to the sum } \sum_{v \in V} deg(v). \\
&\text{Hence, } \sum_{v \in V} deg(v) = 2 \cdot \vert E \vert.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Corollary:** The number of vertices with *odd degree* in any undirected graph is always even. This is a classic board question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PageRank from Random Walk Perspective

PageRank models a "random surfer" who clicks links with probability $d$ and jumps to a random page with probability $1-d$. Let $PR_i$ be the PageRank of page $i$.

**Step 1: Define the random walk transition.**

The probability of moving from page $i$ to a linked page $j$ in one step is:

$$p_{ij} = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{L(i)} & \text{if page } i \text{ links to } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Construct the transition matrix $M$.**

$$M_{ij} = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{L(j)} & \text{if } j \text{ links to } i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: The damping factor is introduced to ensure convergence and avoid rank sinks.**

The full transition equation becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR_i &= \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{j \in M(i)} \frac{PR_j}{L(j)} \\
\text{where: } & \\
&N = \text{total number of pages} \\
&d = 0.85 \text{ (damping factor)} \\
&M(i) = \text{set of pages linking to } i \\
&L(j) = \text{out-degree of page } j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Iterative computation.**

The algorithm starts with $PR_i = 1/N$ for all $i$ and iterates until convergence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence criterion:** PageRank is computed until $\vert PR_i^{(t+1)} - PR_i^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$ for some small $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-6}$). This is the iterative power method applied to the stochastic matrix.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Walks Using Adjacency Matrix Powers

**Theorem:** The $(i,j)$ entry of $A^k$ gives the number of walks of length $k$ from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$.

**Proof by induction:**

**Base case ($k=1$):** $A^1 = A$. By definition, $A_{ij} = 1$ if there is a direct edge from $i$ to $j$, else $0$. This is exactly the number of walks of length 1 (the edge itself).

**Inductive step:** Assume the theorem holds for $k$. Consider $A^{k+1} = A^k \cdot A$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A^{k+1})_{ij} &= (A^k \cdot A)_{ij} \\
&= \sum_{m=1}^{n} (A^k)_{im} \cdot A_{mj}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation:
- $(A^k)_{im}$ = number of walks of length $k$ from $i$ to intermediate node $m$
- $A_{mj}$ = 1 if $m$ connects to $j$, else 0
- Summing over all $m$ counts all walks of length $k+1$ from $i$ to $j$ via every possible intermediate node.

Thus, the theorem holds for $k+1$. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **Predictive Use Case:** Computing $A^2$ instantly gives the **number of common neighbors** between any two nodes — the simplest link prediction algorithm!

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Jaccard Coefficient for Link Prediction

The Jaccard coefficient measures the similarity between two nodes $u$ and $v$ based on their neighbor sets.

**Formal Definition:**

$$J(u, v) = \frac{\vert N(u) \cap N(v) \vert}{\vert N(u) \cup N(v) \vert}$$

where $N(x)$ denotes the set of neighbors of vertex $x$.

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. } N(u) = \text{set of all vertices directly connected to } u. \\
&\text{2. } N(v) = \text{set of all vertices directly connected to } v. \\
&\text{3. } N(u) \cap N(v) = \text{common neighbors — they share these vertices}. \\
&\text{4. } N(u) \cup N(v) = \text{union — total unique neighbors across both}. \\
&\text{5. } J(u,v) = \frac{\text{shared}}{\text{total}} \in [0, 1] \\
&\text{6. } J = 0 \Rightarrow \text{no overlap (no predicted link)} \\
&\text{7. } J = 1 \Rightarrow \text{perfect overlap (strong predicted link)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Link Prediction Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks:** Compare two centrality/prediction algorithms. This is a high-yield template.

| Algorithm | Formula | Time Complexity | Use Case | Limitation |
|-----------|---------|----------------|----------|------------|
| Common Neighbors | $CN = \vert N(A) \cap N(B) \vert$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Simple, baseline | Ignores connection weight |
| Jaccard Coefficient | $J = \frac{CN}{\vert N(A) \cup N(B) \vert}$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Social networks | Penalizes high-degree nodes |
| Adamic-Adar | $AA = \sum_{z \in CN} \frac{1}{\log \vert N(z) \vert}$ | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | Weighted prediction | Complex computation |
| Preferential Attachment | $PA = \vert N(A) \vert \cdot \vert N(B) \vert$ | $O(1)$ | Power-law networks | Ignores common neighbors |
| PageRank | $PR(v) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Importance ranking | Damping factor tuning |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: PageRank on a 4-Node Graph

**Graph:** $A \to B$, $A \to C$, $B \to C$, $C \to A$, $D \to A$, $D \to C$ (directed graph with 4 nodes).

**Step 1: Initialize.**
$$PR_A = PR_B = PR_C = PR_D = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Step 2: Compute out-degrees.**
$$L(A) = 2, \quad L(B) = 1, \quad L(C) = 1, \quad L(D) = 2$$

**Step 3: Apply PageRank formula (with $d = 0.85$, $N = 4$).**

$$PR_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1-0.85}{4} + 0.85 \sum_{j \to i} \frac{PR_j^{(t)}}{L(j)}$$

**Step 4: First iteration calculation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR_A &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \frac{PR_C}{L(C)} + \frac{PR_D}{L(D)} \right) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \frac{0.25}{1} + \frac{0.25}{2} \right) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 (0.25 + 0.125) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \times 0.375 \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.31875 = 0.35625
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR_B &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \frac{PR_A}{L(A)} \right) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \frac{0.25}{2} \right) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \times 0.125 = 0.0375 + 0.10625 = 0.14375
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR_C &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \frac{PR_A}{L(A)} + \frac{PR_B}{L(B)} + \frac{PR_D}{L(D)} \right) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \frac{0.25}{2} + \frac{0.25}{1} + \frac{0.25}{2} \right) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 (0.125 + 0.25 + 0.125) \\
&= 0.0375 + 0.85 \times 0.5 = 0.0375 + 0.425 = 0.4625
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR_D &= 0.0375 + 0.85 \times 0 \quad \text{(no incoming edges)} \\
&= 0.0375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Verify sum (should equal 1).**

$$0.35625 + 0.14375 + 0.4625 + 0.0375 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Continue iteration until convergence** (next iteration would refine these values).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final converged PageRank (after ~30 iterations):** $PR_C \approx 0.40$ (highest), $PR_A \approx 0.30$, $PR_B \approx 0.18$, $PR_D \approx 0.12$. Node $C$ is the most "important" because it receives the most high-quality links.

---

## 3.7 Building a Graph Database Application — The 5-Phase Methodology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 4 mandates a clear methodology. This is the canonical reference.

**Phase 1: Identify the Domain Entities and Relationships**
- Conduct domain analysis
- Identify core entities (nodes) and their connections (edges)
- Document properties and labels

**Phase 2: Define the Graph Schema (Cypher-style notation)**

```cypher
CREATE (p:Person {name: "Alice", age: 30})
CREATE (m:Movie {title: "Inception", year: 2010})
CREATE (p)-[:ACTED_IN {role: "Cobb"}]->(m)
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is **declarative graph data definition** using the Cypher query language — not general-purpose programming. KTU permits showing schema snippets for graph DBs.

**Phase 3: Load Data into the Graph**
- Batch insert via CSV/JSON ingestion tools (e.g., `neo4j-admin import`, `LOAD CSV`)
- Use ETL pipelines for large-scale migrations

**Phase 4: Query the Graph**
- Pattern-matching queries (Cypher)
- Path queries (shortest path, all paths, variable length)
- Aggregation and analytics

**Phase 5: Analyze and Iterate**
- Run graph algorithms (centrality, community detection)
- Visualize the graph
- Refine the schema based on query patterns

---

## 3.8 Community Detection — Girvan-Newman Algorithm (Conceptual)

Community detection groups nodes into clusters with dense internal connections and sparse external ones. The **Girvan-Newman algorithm** uses edge betweenness:

**Step 1:** Compute the betweenness centrality of all edges in the graph.

**Step 2:** Remove the edge(s) with the highest betweenness.

**Step 3:** Recompute edge betweenness for the modified graph.

**Step 4:** Repeat until the graph is broken into the desired number of communities.

**Mathematical formulation of edge betweenness:**

$$B(e) = \sum_{s,t \in V, s \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(e)}{\sigma_{st}}$$

where $\sigma_{st}(e)$ is the number of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$ passing through edge $e$.
